title.rendered: Episode 659: Inviting Visitors Is Easy Online

content.rendered:
Synopsis

In the first month of BNI Online™, BNI Members passed more than $1 billion worth of business.

Many members have shared how easy it is to invite visitors to BNI Online™ meetings.

  • It’s more convenient and saves time.
  • There’s no travel and no meal costs.
  • I live in an area with terrible traffic jams, so it’s definitely easier.
  • People aren’t afraid they’ll get Coronavirus at an online meeting.
  • You don’t have to find childcare to visit an online meeting.
  • It’s even easier to bring employees to the online meeting.
  • We’ve had visitors come every single week since we started meeting online.
  • It’s easier for introverts.
  • It’s less of a time commitment.
  • You can share your screen for your feature presentation.
  • It helps you feel part of a business community, even if your business is closed right now.

One member has brought 10 visitors since BNI Online™ started. Priscilla’s chapter has had two new members join since BNI Online™ started.

You don’t need to get into a lot of detail when you invite visitors. Just ask them to come and experience a BNI meeting. Giving people too much information in advance can scare them off. Until they know what it’s like to be in a meeting, the details aren’t important.

More Podcasts About Inviting Visitors

Visitors are a frequent topic on The Official BNI Podcast. Here are some of Dr. Misner’s favorite episodes about inviting visitors to your BNI meeting.

458 – Inviting People – Good, Better, Best

486 – Inviting Visitors to Your Feature Presentation

541 – Inviting Visitors (Classic of 278)

547 – Interviewing Prospective Members

572 – Focused Invite Days

633 – I’ve Invited Everyone I know

Episodes 541 and 547 focus on the “We’re interviewing” technique.

Complete Transcript of Episode 659

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing great Priscilla and you know, I miss the “How are you and where are you?” I do.

Priscilla:
I do, too.

Ivan:
You know, I travel all over the world and people come up to me and say, “Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?” So it’s a line that people have picked up all around the world. We’ll get back to it, though, as soon as we start traveling again.

Priscilla:
Okay, that sounds good. So what do you have in mind for us today?

Ivan:
So I received a message from a BNI member who was distraught, because he said he had no idea how to invite people to BNI online. And I was really surprised by that, because I thought it would be easier to invite people, not harder to invite people. And so to confirm that, or to find out if it was harder, I actually asked members on my social media, I sort of crowd sourced feedback. And I asked them if they thought it was easier or harder to invite people online and why. And Priscilla, virtually everyone said it was easier.

And so I want to talk about some of the ways it is easier. And I’m gonna quote some of the crowd sourced comments from people all around the world, and what they said about why it was easier. And then I’ve got some closing thoughts, and I’m going to ask your opinion on it, as well. We’ll see when we get to the end. So here are some of the kinds of comments that I received.

When I asked, “Is it harder or more difficult to invite online?” Frasier said that it is more convenient and saves time. And Frasier also thanked BNI for its forward-thinking by using the latest technology during this time period. Karen and many others said it’s easier because there’s no travel and no meal costs. So she said, “Our group is actually considering maintaining the online element in the future or possibly doing some hybrid.”

I think you know, as I mentioned in a previous podcast, the genie is out of the bag and I think we’re probably going to see some kind of hybrid. I’ll talk a little bit more about that at the end. Omar says it’s more convenient, definitely more convenient. He said he lives in an area with constant traffic jams and so the online platform is easier. Vicki said it’s not scary going to the meetings because of the virus issue. And, boy, I think that’s a big issue. People, you know, no one is going to want to go out and in most countries, you cannot go to a meeting with a lot of people and so because of the virus issue, it’s just not scary but you can still network. She also said that it’s great for anyone with childcare issues, that they can still do BNI online. Jamie said she thoroughly enjoyed her virtual meeting as a visitor. So here is a visitor who responded to my social media request. And she said she really enjoyed our virtual meeting as a visitor and thinks that this would be an incredible hybrid system down the road. Rachid said, it’s even easier to bring employees to the meeting. So if you’ve got people who work for you, bringing them to the online meeting is even easier. These are all of the different thoughts that have been given to me about it being easier.

And not one person, not one, said it’s harder to invite. Some people said they miss the face to face, the in-person, but not one person said it’s harder. Brenda said it’s definitely easier for a visitor come and it allows more flexibility. She said we’ve had visitors come to the meetings every single week since they transitioned to online. Mike said that people are struggling in business, and people need BNI more than ever. Yes, Mike, people need BNI more than ever. He said being online allows them to network during this time.

Very true. Petti says that being online will give more awareness to the BNI program. And I think that’s very true. I think when this is all over that BNI membership will actually increase substantially because of BNI online. Of course, the larger the organization, the more name recognition; the more name recognition, the easier it is for you to invite people to the organization because you’ve hit that tipping point in the creation of brand recognition. Sujata says that it’s easier for introverts. Now I love that comment, that if you feel at all like you’re an introvert, it’s easier to come to the online platform than it is if you’re an extrovert, so that was one I had not thought of – great, great point.

Kundun says it’s less of a time commitment. And he and others said the same thing, much less of a time commitment because you don’t have to, you know, drive there or get up early, get prepared, go drive there and drive back. Reetou says you can just log in without the pressure of dressing up and driving to the venue, which is similar to Kundun. Jeff said he has had 10 visitors since BNI Online – 10 visitors since they went to BNI Online. Charles said getting visitors is easy and they just inducted a new member through BNI Online. I think these are really great suggestions and I want to point out that I still love the in-person meetings. But I definitely agree with the comments that I just shared that it is easier than ever to invite people. I want to mention some other episodes, some other podcasts on inviting people in a minute, but before I do, Priscilla, tell us what your chapter’s experience has been.

Priscilla:
Thank you, Ivan. We have had two new members join since we went online, which is really encouraging, and we had a visitor last week that was quite interested, she might join. So that was great. As far as our President, his attitude is when we talk about how to invite, his attitude is not to share a lot of details about how much it costs and what all the requirements are in terms of attendance, and you know, all the details of BNI – but just to invite somebody to see if they enjoy the meeting, and if it feels like something rewarding and something they might want to get involved with. And then you kind of can tell them more about what it might cost in the future and what your requirements are. And I just want to add, there’s something else that wasn’t mentioned. But it was mentioned in my group, which is one of the advantages of the online meeting is the screen sharing aspect, so that people who have websites or have digital products to share with the other members, it’s very easy to do. Whereas, before it was kind of difficult and you had to hook up some kind of overhead projector and get there early and hope that it worked and so forth. And now you can just share what’s on your computer with your members.

Ivan:
Oh, that’s a great point. That’s one of those, I like to call it a BFO. I don’t know that I’ve used that expression on the podcast before – Blinding Flash of the Obvious.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
That’s a really good point, thanks. So one of the things that your President said that I agree 100% with, don’t overload people with information. And when I was inviting people in the early days of BNI and there was, you know, literally just 1,2,3,4 chapters. I would invite people and they’d say to me, “Do you have a brochure?” I didn’t have anything, Priscilla, I opened 20 chapters of BNI with no printed material. Not one thing. People said, “You have to have something.” I said no, no, you have to experience a meeting, come to the meeting. And I wouldn’t give them a ton of information, I would just say, “Come to the meeting, you have to experience the meeting and be prepared to meet you know, 20 30, 40, 100 people depending on the size of your group, be prepared to meet a lot of people. And that was it. I did not have material, it made no difference.

As a matter of fact, I find that if you start giving people tons of information, they won’t even come to experience the meeting. And you’ve got to get people to come to experience the meeting, whether you’re talking about in-person or online, as we’re talking right now, they’ve got to experience the meeting. So I agree with your President.


Priscilla:
I have one more thing to say. Which is, in this time that we’re going through right now with the isolation and you know, staying at home and so forth to be safe, BNI helps with creating community, and it makes you feel connected to other people and even if your business is shut down during this period, you feel like you’re part of a business community and that when it does open up, you’ll have work ahead for you, that people will be looking for work for you. And that’s a great feeling in this time of socially isolating.

Ivan:
Yeah. Wow, you’re 2-and-0 Priscilla, those are two great points that nobody else brought up. So thank you for sharing those. I appreciate it.

So for those of you listening, I think the invitation process is really very similar to doing it in person. And so I want to give you some episodes of the BNI Podcast to go to that talk about inviting people. So here, there’s about five or six of them; six or seven of them, actually. Episode Number 633 – I’ve Invited Everyone I Know. I’ll make sure links go into this podcast so you can click on them. I’ve Invited Everyone I Know is the first podcast. Episode 572 – Focused Invite Days. Episode 541 – Inviting Visitors. Episode 486 – Inviting Visitors to Your Feature Presentation. Love that concept. Episode 458 – Inviting People – Good, Better, Best. And my personal favorites, Episode 541 – Inviting Visitors, which discusses the “we’re interviewing” technique that I talked about. And then the companion Episode 547 – Interviewing Prospective Members.

So these are great podcasts. Check these out. When the Great Pause is over, I like to call it the “Great Pause” rather than lockdown. When the Great Pause is over, I believe we will go back to in-person meetings or some type of hybrid. Until then, health and safety are key issues and at this time, we don’t want people to go back to meeting in person until it is safe. As I’ve been saying throughout this situation, today more than ever, you need your network. BNI has invested heavily in making sure that the BNI platform is here for you. While others are at a loss for networking, BNI members can still generate referrals and support as you mentioned, Priscilla, and I urge you to go 100% in with the online platform until such time as it’s safe to consider our options. On a closing note, I recently heard that in the first month of BNI online alone, first month, according to BNI Connect, members passed and generated more than 1 billion, with a “B”, 1 billion US dollars worth of business, worth of referral business in the first month of BNI Online. Today, more than ever, you need BNI. Over to you Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Oh, I think that’s great. Okay, well thank you so much, Dr. Misner. I just like to say that the sponsor for this podcast is Ivan’s Inner Circle. Go check out the great content available at www.ivansinnercircle.com. Ivan has assembled a large variety of interesting topics for you to learn about and also participate in. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: BNI members share the advantages of inviting visitors to BNI Online™ meetings, both for members and for visitors.

title.rendered: Episode 658: Looking Your Best for Virtual Meetings

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This week photographer and documentary filmmaker Antoine Didienne from the BNI Partners in Growth chapter in San Diego joins Dr. Misner to talk about how to look better on virtual meetings–including your BNI online™ meetings.

Two of the most important factors in how you look on a video call are positioning your camera and lighting yourself. You can improve both of these without buying any special equipment.

  • Always face the light source.
  • Use “daylight white” (5,000K) light bulbs.
  • Position your camera at or above eye level. Using a laptop or tablet? Put it on a stack of books.
  • Natural light: sit facing the window at a 45-degree angle.
  • Artificial light: position the light source in front of and above you.
  • You can open a blank Word doc to use your screen as a source of light.
  • Position the conference window as close to the camera as you can, so you look into the camera.

Learn more about Antoine and see his photography at https://www.147photos.com/

Complete Transcript of Episode 658

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you today?

Ivan:
I’m doing great. Priscilla, thank you so much. And I received recently an email from one of our Directors in San Diego, Catherine Lowell. And she sent me a link to a really good blog. And that’s the topic for today’s discussion. I read this blog by Antoine Didienne who I’m about to introduce. He’s an award winning wedding events and family documentary photographer. He specializes in storytelling through authentic candid moments in life. He’s French American. He’s been married to Glenda for 20 years and I love this and one has four children, two human girls and two dogs. For the past 10 years, and and when you were born in France, we have a lot of big chapters a lot in being my friends. Tell everyone First of all, welcome to the BNI podcast and tell everyone where in France you were born.

Antoine:
Well, thank you, Ivan. I really appreciate you inviting me on the on the podcast today. It is a time sensitive issue to be looking your best on you know for virtual meetings, but yeah, so it’s great to be here. I’m from a region of France that is close to to Leon. It’s a it’s a region called La Bias. And specifically, I’m from a small town called _ So whoever is from shout out to my young people in France.

Ivan:
That’s fantastic. We really have some great chapters in France. My wife and I spent almost two months in Paris working and taking a little time off. We love France. All right, well, we’re here to do some work. So let’s – What about your blog? First of all, I I absolutely love it. In the blog you talked about some do’s and don’ts for virtual meetings and the camera. So talk to us about about the those do’s and don’ts.

Antoine:
So I really wrote this from the point of view of a photographer because you know, this is what I do all day long. And I saw in the first few weeks of the of BNI moving on online because of you know, the times that we’re going through right now – I noticed a lot of a lot of things that could just could be done better for for the BNI community and just and just for everybody else, you know, so mostly I see the problems in terms of positioning you camera and lighting yourself. So it’s mostly the two areas where I think we need to with, you know, zero additional dollars on just all of this for free, then we can just do things slightly differently and so that we can look better.

So for example, the big big no no would be, do not put your back to the light source as in if you’re going to record by a window, which is what I would recommend. Anyways. Do not do it with your back to the window. Always face the light source. Because this way, your, your face won’t appear just well lit, because your webcam is not intelligent, it will grab on to the light source that is closest to the webcam and will light that reflective surface the best.

Ivan:
And we’ve, we’ve all seen this where somebody is, you know, in front of a light source and so they’re basically a silhouette. You can hardly make your face, right?

Antoine:
Exactly. So that’s when that’s when your face is far from the camera. And the webcam will actually grab on to adjust for the light that is outside of the window. So this way, that’s why you appear silhouetted if your face is closer to the camer. You will be led correctly but the back of you will be completely blown out. And in the end people don’t pay attention to your face, they pay attention to what’s in the background. So yeah.

Ivan:
You even had a recommendation about well, about lighting and the kind of light bulbs to us – you want to talk about that?

Antoine:
Yes. If you use 5000 Kelvin, color light bulbs, which is 5000 Kelvin is basically just a color temperature, it’s daylight white. So if you use daylight white light bulbs, your skin color will be the same as the skin color that you have when you’re outside. Basically, it’s the color of the science, the color of the day. If you use warmer lightbulbs, again, your webcam is going to have to make adjustments and you will, your skin tone will appear really yellow. It’ll be very difficult for your webcam to adjust to these things. And in the end, it’s just not, you know, just doesn’t work as well.

Ivan:
Talk a little bit about positioning.

Antoine:
Right so that’s also one of the the other big issues and very often when we do these online meetings, we’re on phones, we’re on tablet or we’re on a laptop, and these are great but they light, they record from below. And when your record actually from below that just from the point of view [unknown, 2 syllables] I, it makes you look like you’re looking down on someone because the point of view from below and it just increases your chin size and increase your eye. Your eyes are pulling down. It makes you look like you have almost closed eyes. So it doesn’t it doesn’t look like you’re paying attention to actually to who you’re speaking to. So raise your camera at minimum eye level or even better just slightly above your eye light. And you will all of a sudden have a much more pleasing angle. Your face will actually be the the size and shape that it really is when you’re actually talking to people face to face. And this solves a lot of problems.

Ivan:
You know, I never thought of the eye level in terms of, you know, an issue in it. Your eyes look slightly closed. I think that’s a great suggestion. I have a really big nose. And the last thing to do is look up my nose, right? I’m wondering if I put the camera too low. So I figured that out but, but the eyes being open with something new for me.

Antoine:
That was good, no. But so my recommendation is if you’re using a laptop, a tablet or phone, put that on a stack of books. And that’s the fastest way to solve that problem. And to solve the you know, the high problem just put your laptop or your tablet on the stack of books and make sure that it leans forward so that your your camera is directly looking into you.

Ivan:
Yeah, I’ve done that many times while traveling. Books make a decent tripod. Yeah,exactly. Great. You know, the lighting i think is so important and it’s one that a lot. My biggest challenge is too much light because I’m sitting in front of huge windows. So I have to actually pull drapes down Well, I don’t have drapes on the window, I have to actually put up a sign. I’ve got the sign in my office and I just put it in the window. So that blocks out some of the lights. So sometimes you make it, you need to make it not so bright. But the biggest problem is it’s too behind –

Antoine:
You know, what you could do is is step away a little bit from the window. Because the further you are the further you are from the light source, the softer the lights and the light is going to be actually

Ivan:
Yeah, with cameras right up with my computer which is in the corner.

Antoine:
That might be a problem.

Ivan:
But when I do other recordings, I step away, yeah, into my office. So great. What other suggestions?

Antoine:
Well, just one more thing. For example, if you have really large window and you almost have too much sun, put a white sheet in front of the window, and that will diffuse a lot of the lights and that will help you have just a softer, more diffused light; it will be it’s pretty easy. It’s a pretty easy way to solve this. You could also use a lamp for people that are in an office where they don’t have necessarily a lot of light or no natural light at all. The best solution is put a lamp and the best way to do this is put a lamp either right behind the camera so that you can actually face the light right completely head on. Or my preference is to put it at 45 degree either to my left to my right depends on your on your quote unquote your good side that you have to figure out yourself. And if possible, elevate it at 45 degrees above you as well because this is the basically the angle of the sun. Right. The sun, you know unless you’re at at noon outside the sun kind of always lights you 45 degree angle, right? Looking down on you. So you want to try to reproduce these, these conditions as much as possible so that you have a look that is familiar to most people, which is basically their light.

Ivan:
Excellent. So do you need to buy anything to do what you’re describing here?

Antoine:
No, you don’t have to magically just, uh, yeah, it’s kind of a little bit of a trial and error at the beginning, especially if you’re using a lamp or if you’re using a window. That’s really easy, you know, you just don’t face the window, or my preference is around like, a 45 degree to 90 degree angle to the window. That gives a really nice pleasant shape to your to your face that provides structure a little bit. And that’s just, you know, for me, it’s the most pleasant way to to like myself.

Ivan:
Now I use the camera settings as well. You know, I think if you know what you’re doing using the camera settings to make it darker or lighter. Yeah, it has been an effective technique for me and I would recommend that people practice with it before they go live on something.

Antoine:
But actually, I have another little pro tip here. If you want you can actually open a Word document on your on your screen. And because your laptop or your desktop emits light, your basically your your your screen becomes a light source and you can increase or decrease the light source you know, by just reducing or increasing the intensity of your, of your of your screen brightness. And this way you actually provide additional or less lighting to your face.

Ivan:
Great suggestion, and one great suggestion. And all of these suggestions are really important because you need to look your best when you’re doing any kind of online meeting but particularly online meeting – and the eye thing is very important for looking attentive and being being attentive Of course. Yeah, also very important.

Antoine:
I want to mention one more thing just to make sure that you are you look really attentive also. Let’s take the example example of Zoom because a lot of a lot of BNI chapters use Zoom and that’s the case for my chapter partners is to put the the conference window as close as possible to the, to the camera itself, to the webcam itself.

Ivan:
You’re looking at it.

Antoine:
Exactly. You’re looking at it. Because you know, you look at people in the eye when you when you talk. And that’s the that’s the fastest way to look like you’re paying attention.

Ivan:
Great suggestions, and I really appreciate your help. We’re going to include a link to the blog that I read that had me invite Antoine to this podcast, and when can you give your website for those people that are interested?

Antoine:
Yeah, it’s pretty simple. It’s 147photos.com. That’s it. That’s really easy.

Ivan:
It is indeed. Antoine, thank you so very much for being on BNI Podcast. I really appreciate it. These are great suggestions everyone. Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay. Well, thank you very much. Both of you. That was great, great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Two of the most important factors in how you look in a virtual meeting are positioning your camera and lighting yourself.

title.rendered: Episode 657: Inviting a Visitor Who Cannot Join (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This episode is a rebroadcast of Episode 452. This is a Classic Podcast – Ivan is NOT traveling at this time.

Synopsis

Today’s question comes from Victor in Latvia.

I know a person I’d like to invite as a visitor, and I’m pretty sure he would join as a member, but his category is already taken in our group. Since he can’t join our group, our group couldn’t benefit from inviting him? Should I still invite him? If he joins another chapter, how can our chapter benefit?

The short answer is “Yes, absolutely you should invite him”–but first talk to the person in your group who represents that profession. Reassure that person that you are not bringing the visitor in as competition.

How can your group benefit from inviting a visitor who can’t join your chapter?

  • Inviting this person is an investment in social capital. If you want to build a powerful personal network, you need to build relationships with many professions.
  • Building strong chapters near you helps to create a strong BNI brand. The better BNI looks overall, the more likely good people are to want to join your chapter.
  • If you help a group nearby, the more likely they are to help you.
  • Any visitor, whether or not they join your chapter, has a network and can refer members of your group to them.

Send your questions to askivan@bni.com.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcript of Episode 452

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing great today, Priscilla, and I have a really interesting question. It is a long one, so stick with me. It is kind of detailed. The question comes from Victor, A BNI member in BNI Wagoner in Riga, Latvia. Yes, we have chapters in Latvia, which I think is so cool. It is a long question so let me read it to everyone and then we will talk about it.

He says, “I know a person who I would like to invite to BNI, and I am pretty sure that he would join as a member, but his category is already taken in our group. So inviting him to the meeting and introducing him to the BNI program, he may like the meeting, but he wouldn’t be able to join my chapter. He could possibly join another chapter in a nearby city where his category isn’t taken. But in that way, the whole group and I really couldn’t benefit from bringing him in.”

So here is his question: “Should I be selfish and not invite him or should I invite him? And if I invite him and he joins another group, how can we as a chapter benefit from that? How can we benefit from bringing in somebody who we know can’t join?”

That’s his question. Interesting question, don’t you think?

Priscilla:
Yeah. I can come up with a reason, at least one reason why he should invite that person.

Ivan:
Alright. Hold that reason because I have two and I would love to hear your reason.

Priscilla:
Okay.

Ivan:
So I have two comments anyway. One really good reason, but two comments for sure. The first is if you are going to invite him into your group- and I think the short answer to the question of should I invite him, the answer is yes. Absolutely. You should invite him.

Before you do, first, talk to the person that represents that profession in your group. Make sure that they are not surprised. Now, you don’t have to ask for their permission to invite them, but you don’t want to blindside them, either. So you want to make sure that they know that you are inviting them, but you are not trying to encroach on their classification. And that you want to bring it to their attention that you are not bringing them in so that they can do business with other members of the group or that you are going to do business with them, but that you just want to show an associate, someone you know, what BNI is all about.

Just make sure that the existing member feels your commitment because if you don’t show that, it is going to be a problem because the existing member is going to be very upset that you basically brought in someone that is in a similar classification. That is the first thing that you have to do.

So to answer the question of how you can benefit, that is easy. That is so easy. This is a classic example of givers gain. If you want to build a powerful personal network, you need to build relationships with many professions. And sometimes overlapping professions.

Now, the best way to build a relationship is invest in your social capital with other people. Invest in other people. Help them. If you can find a way to help people, that is the quickest way to build a relationship. So view this as an investment in the social capital with this person.

Here is a third one for you: helping to build strong chapters near you helps to build your group by making a stronger brand. Let’s reverse this. If a group next to you, Victor in Riga or anyone listening to this podcast- if a BNI group next to you is horrible, it reflects badly on your group because it gives a bad reputation to BNI so that when you invite someone to BNI, they go, “No, no, no, no. I don’t want to go to BNI near you. They were awful.”

Flip that. If they go to a group next to you that is strong and it reflects well, it then reflects well on your group it helps your reputation. They may have gone to another group and they couldn’t join, “I went to another group. They were fantastic but I couldn’t join.”

That helps you as a group. So the stronger that you make nearby chapters, the stronger your group can be.

I will give you a fourth reason. If you help a group nearby, you are better able to get them to help you. You can help them if you can help build their chapter. Then reach out to them from time to time. Say, “Hey, we sent you a couple visitors. How did they work out?”

“Oh one of them became a member. He turned out to be a great member and he has brought four or five new members.”

“Fantastic. Here are a couple of categories that we have open in our chapter. Do you have them filled?”

“Yeah, we have them filled.”

“If you know somebody in those categories that you have filled would you send them to us because we need them?”

“Yeah. We would be glad to do that. We would be glad to reciprocate.”

Priscilla:
Right. That is really a good idea.

Ivan:
It is really about looking at the world from an abundance mentality versus a scarcity mentality. If you view the world with an abundance mentality, you go through it and you are just trying to help people. You are building a better brand. You are building relationships with people. And you are building a relationship with the other chapter. It will be a reciprocal relationship where they want to do the same thing.

So there are four–one comment and three reasons why, Victor, I think it is a good idea for you to invite this person to your group.

Priscilla, you had a good one. What was it?

Priscilla:
Yeah, so it’s the reason that you want to invite guests whether they can join or not anytime- because they have a network and since he hasn’t pledged himself to that group yet, he is not dedicated to the people in that group so when he meets the members of your group, he might refer them to his network. So there is definitely a benefit to the other members.

Ivan:
That is one that I didn’t even think of. Priscilla, that is a great observation because, Victor, you may be assuming that this guy would want to join your chapter or would want to join another chapter. He might not. You know, every week might be too much for him or who knows what the reason might be. But he might know of people who could benefit and would be interested. And he would most likely refer them to your group.

I think that is your point, right Priscilla?

Priscilla:
Exactly.

Ivan:
He may actually invite someone to your chapter even if he doesn’t join BNI. So I think that is a great additional point.

Victor, this is a fantastic question. I really appreciate you sharing it with me, and I hope I have answered your question. Priscilla, you had a great addition to it. Please feel free, everyone, to keep those questions coming. I can’t promise to answer every one. I get a lot of them but I try to pick the ones that I think are coming up most often or are the most important, critical ones and try to answer those. Send them to, was it askivan@bni.com, I think?

Priscilla:
Yep, that is it.

Ivan:
Okay, great. That’s all for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, thank you, Dr. Misner. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. Thanks for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered:
This entry is part 29 of 33 in the series Ask Ivan

Inviting a visitor who can’t join your BNI chapter strengthens BNI as a whole and builds social capital for your chapter.

title.rendered: Special Episode 4: Thoughts About Our Transition

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This is an additional episode for the week. The regularly scheduled episode will post as usual, later this week.

Dr. Misner returns with a fourth Special Episode to address BNI’s plan for transitioning back to holding in-person meetings.

  • The plan will not be a cookie cutter approach.
  • BNI Directors and members from all over the world have been giving input.
  • Chapters will need to follow specific guidelines in order to help insure the safety of their members.
  • Almost $1 billion (USD) was generated by members last month through referrals via BNI Online™.
  • Be safe. Be patient. We can do this together.

Complete Transcript of Special Episode 4

Hi, this is Ivan and I am doing a very short, special podcast about the pandemic and what some of our thoughts are for the near future. Please share this with your fellow members.

I know some restrictions are being relaxed in some areas of the world relating to the pandemic and I wanted to share some thoughts about our next steps in BNI. First and foremost, our number one objective is the safety of our members. At the same time, I understand that this is an organization made up of entrepreneurs and we all want to get back to doing business and doing it in person. I want to assure you that we will. We are working on a plan to help us do this as safely as possible.

The plan we are working on will not be a cookie cutter approach. Different parts of the world are in different situations and the impact of the pandemic is still evolving. Many BNI Directors and members from all over the world have been giving input and our plan will take those local conditions into consideration.

This is important… group meetings are a higher-risk environment and we want our members to be safe. When we do transition back to in-person meetings, we will need chapters to follow specific guidelines in order to help insure the safety of our members. The guidelines are only part of the plan that needs to be in place before groups start meeting in-person again. We’re working on this. It is a high priority but must be done right and we cannot rush into this.

In the meantime, BNI online™ has been very effective at keeping us all connected in order to provide support and business for each other. Almost $1 billion (USD) was generated by members last month through referrals via our online platform. That is an amazing result in a world that has been put on pause for many weeks.

I ask that everyone continue to be patient. I know that sometimes it doesn’t feel like this will ever pass. But it will. And when it does, I want us all to look back and be proud of how quickly we transitioned to BNI Online™ and equally important, how safely we transitioned back to our in-person meetings (or even possibly some hybrid of online and in-person in the future).

I am proud of how members and Directors have rallied to implement an alternative to our in-person meetings. I want to look back and be equally proud as to how we transitioned back to in-person meetings in a way that was safe and fruitful for everyone.

My last podcast was about hope. Hope is more powerful than fear. Hope plus action leaves fear in the dust. Hope is hearing that little voice inside you whispering about… what “can be” when everyone around you is shouting what “can’t be.”  Let me help that little voice and say – you’re going to get through this. It’s not easy but you have a network. A network of people who are there to help you. Your Leadership Team, your fellow members, your Directors and me. We’re here to help. While the world is going through the Great Pause. You can lean on your network and get ready for the great release.

Have hope AND take action. BNI Online™ allows us to do both.

Be safe. Be patient. We can do this together.

This is Ivan Misner, Founder of BNI. Thanks for listening.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

Dr. Misner returns with a new Special Episode to address BNI’s plan for transitioning back to holding in-person meetings.

title.rendered: Episode 656: Hope Is More Powerful Than Fear

content.rendered:
Synopsis

In this week’s podcast, Dr. Misner responds to two different critiques regarding some of his social media posts about “hope” during a crisis.

One respondent was outraged that Dr. Misner was offering people “false hope.” The other was a BNI member who found it difficult to stay positive but was willing to keep an open mind.

What is the positive in being locked down at home with your three children needing to be home-schooled?

Daniel, a BNI Member

Fear vs. Focus

Even in a global pandemic, we are in charge of our attitude. Rather than get caught up in fear, focus on the solution.

Almost all of us are stuck at home. We can choose to make the most of it and come out better and stronger, or we can choose to be overcome by it.

False Hope?

To the first critic, Dr. Misner replied that hope is hearing that little voice inside you whispering about what can be when everyone around you is shouting about what can’t be.

Hope is more powerful than fear. Hope plus action leaves fear in the dust. Have hope, take action, and that’s the way you deal with situations in life.

Read “Hope Is More Powerful Than Fear” and watch the video on IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 656

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you today?

Ivan:
Hey, Priscilla, I am doing great. And I have a podcast today that I think is unique. It is hope is is more powerful than fear. And you probably know, I know you’ve seen a lot of my posts. I’ve been posting messages on my social media ever since the COVID virus and all of the resulting things because of that. I’ve been posting a lot of my social media about hope and focus when times are difficult. And as a result, I had someone give me a gift. His gift was a rather harsh critique. He said, basically, in part, “What are you thinking, giving people false hope with posts like this?” Now I call it a gift because it gave me an opportunity to do this podcast. I want to comment about that critique in a minute.

But before I do, I want to talk about another message that I received from Daniel in Canada. He’s a BNI member and Daniel’s comment was much different. Now come back to the critique towards the end. Daniel said he appreciated my positive feedback. But he asked for help understanding what is the positive in not being able to work while being locked down at home with his three children needing to be homeschooled?

Yeah. So I mean, I get that. And what I liked about Daniel’s comment was, he appreciated the positive approach, but he was unsure how he could apply it. But he was open minded, and I feel like I can work with that. You know, people don’t have to agree with my position. But you know, if they ask for how do you apply that? I get it. Well, first of all, I’ve talked about in the special episodes that I did, you know, the importance of making effective use of being online and online or in person.

There are a number of things that you could do, but here’s what I said to Daniel, because we were as he said in lockdown. I like to call it the great pause. We’ve all had to pause in life, but call it what you will. This was my answer to Daniel. I said to him, “It sounds like you have your hands full. And I remember being at home with kids. And in the summer when I would work from home one day a week. And so sometimes it was in your hands to feel full.” So I said to him, “I like to be positive. I’m also a realist. And the situation we’re in with COVID virus, the situation we’re in sucks.” I said that twice. And I also said, “That’s the technical term. It sucks. And I get that.”

And I quoted to him part of that, Charles Swindoll, quote, about attitude and a lot of people have read that and I’m not going to read the whole quote, but I’ll read an excerpt from it. So Swindoll said

The longer I live, the more I realize the impact of action today on life. Attitude to me is more important than facts. It’s more important than the past, than education, than circumstances and failures, than success, than what other people think or say or do. I am convinced that life is 10% what happens to me and 90% how I react to it.

Charles Swindoll

And so he said, “And so it is with you. We are all in charge of our attitudes.” And I really, you know, I read that many, many years ago, and I printed it out. I it’s up in my office. That printout is up in my office. I have to remind myself of it periodically, but I really believe in it.

And so I continued my response to Daniel by saying that my spin on this concept is fear versus focus. Rather than get caught up in fear, I try to focus on the solutions. I told him, “I don’t have the specific answers to you, but I do have the questions that you can reflect upon.” I’ve talked to BNI members about the kinds of things they can do online like one to ones, but he was talking a lot about his children and being there with his kid. So I tried to address my question to that. So as you listen to the questions I posed to them, ask the questions of yourself that are relevant to you in your situation, in terms of dealing with whatever life is, whatever, whatever life has handed you, how do you deal with it?

And so I said to him, “These are the questions: How can you deepen the relationship with your children at this time? What can you do to have fun in teaching them when they are studying? Wouldn’t it be a good time for you to study or watch something motivational? Or do one to ones with your BNI members? Can you find something you’ve read or watched as a lesson for your children? Who can you offer moral support to during this challenging time? These are questions that only you can answer and I think the answer to those questions, helps you know how to put a positive spin on it here.”

The bottom line here I told him is that almost all of us are stuck at home, we can choose to make the most of it and come out better and stronger, or we can choose to be overcome by it. I choose better and stronger and to do that I choose to focus on solutions. And when I do, I almost always feel better. So, back to my critic, who I shall leave nameless. I will not name him. He and I live with a completely different worldview. Priscilla, I believe a positive attitude has little to do with what happens to you. It’s more about what you do with what happens to you. Am I giving people false hope?

My answer to him is no. Absolutely not. Hope is hearing that little voice inside you whispering about what can be when everyone around you is shouting what can’t be. I believe that hope is more powerful than fear. Hope plus action leaves fear in the dust have hope to take action. And that’s the way you deal with challenges in life. And that was my message to both parties. Priscilla. Any thoughts?

Priscilla:
Yeah, I love it. I’m definitely an optimist. So even in the worst of times, I try to figure out what could be the lesson or what could I take from this that would help me, and it’s hard sometimes. I mean, you know, especially at first if you get a blow of some kind, you know, it takes awhile to come to terms with it, but I’m always looking for the bright side. Always. Yeah, I do agree with you that it’s more powerful than fear.

Ivan:
You can absolutely can be and, you know, when times are tough, people tend to live in that place of fear, rather than to try and find hope. And look, I’ve been there. There have been times where I’ve been very concerned. And there have been times where I’ve thrown myself a pity party. And you know, I’m here to tell you, at the end of the pity party, nothing has changed and I feel just as bad. So pity parties, I realized a long time ago, they better be short, because you’re not gonna really accomplish anything in doing so.

That’s why I really feel like you got to focus on on what’s potentially positive and in one of my I think it was a special podcast number: “Why?” I talked about how I, when I was diagnosed with cancer, the first thing I did was come up with a list of possible positive outcomes and that everything on that list ended up coming true but I had to focus on what could be possible. And I think that’s that’s the key here. And that’s my goal with this podcast, is you know that there’s a lot of repercussions that are still left yet to be felt from the COVID virus and I really am a believer that hope is more powerful than fear. Any last thoughts or questions, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
No, I think that’s great what you’ve said. I really appreciate it.

Ivan:
Well, thank you Priscilla. And that’s it for today.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Thank you, Dr. Misner.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Hope plus action leaves fear in the dust. We can choose to make the most of this situation and come out better and stronger.

title.rendered: Episode 655: I Have, I Need, I Will

content.rendered:
Synopsis

[IMPORTANT CORRECTION]: At 8 minutes into the original podcast, Ivan misspoke when he stated that “some chapters are transitioning back to in-person” meetings. This is NOT accurate.  What he meant to say is that “at some point, chapters will be transitioning back to in-person.”

For the record, at this time, NO chapters are transitioning back to in-person meetings for obvious health and safety reasons.

Ivan sincerely apologies for this error. The audio file has now been corrected.

Jason Avery, author of Constructing Success and a member of BNI Referral Masters (Clearwater), joins Dr. Misner this week to talk about Power Teams.

When Jason started meeting with his Trades Power Team, he discovered that BNI does not have a standard Power Team agenda. His Power Team (which now has 33 members) developed its own format; I Have, I Need, I Will.

This format provides each member with specific actions to take to help other members grow their businesses.

Other key factors in the group’s success are

  • Attendance policy: members must attend 2 consecutive meetings to participate in the shared advertising plan, and will be removed if they miss 3 consecutive meetings.
  • Shared Advertising: every quarter, the group has 5,000 rack cards printed with all the members’ businesses on it.
  • Consistency: meeting every two weeks at the same time, in the same place. (They are temporarily meeting on Zoom, at the same time as the regular meeting.)

Ivan recommends that large, successful Power Teams invite at least one member who is part of another Power Team or is not yet in a Power Team to attend as an observer. This can help other Power Teams develop.

Complete Transcript of Episode 655

[IMPORTANT CORRECTION]: At 8 minutes into the original podcast, Ivan misspoke when he stated that “some chapters are transitioning back to in-person” meetings.  This is NOT accurate.  What he meant to say is that “at some point, chapters will be transitioning back to in-person.”

For the record, at this time, NO chapters are transitioning back to in-person meetings for obvious health and safety reasons.

Ivan sincerely apologies for this error. The audio file has now been corrected.

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla, and I’m really excited that I have a guest on today. His name is Jason Avery. Jason owns Avery construction which is a state certified General Contractor. They remodel kitchens and bathrooms and build new room additions in the greater Tampa Bay area. And since 2011, Jason’s built a team of 40 employees who complete over 4 million in renovations annually. He gets nearly 60% of all their new business by referrals. And Jason now works on his business instead of in his business. I love that. He’s the author of the number one best selling book Constructing Success Blueprints for a Referral Based Business of which I was proud to do the foreword. Jason’s been a member of BNI Referral Masters since it started and he’s been president of the chapter twice. Now, this is what I love about this story, Referral Masters as a Diamond Chapter. They have 105 members and his power team, the power team, which is what we’re going to talk about today. I have I need I will is really about power teams; his power team has 33 participating members. That’s bigger than some chapters. Jason, I’m impressed.

Jason:
I’m glad to be here today.

Ivan:
It’s a pleasure to have you on. So talk to me a little bit about the power team agenda. Because the purpose here is I want you to be talking about how you do this amazing power team. So what’s the power team agenda that you guys use?

Jason:
Well, I found that there really was no standard BNI-issued agenda for power teams. And my group decided that we didn’t really want more of the same. And so we developed our own meeting agenda: “I have, I need, I will”. I have is the first opportunity to go around each member speaking; there’s no set timer. It’s 30 to 60 seconds and it’s a chance for members to horn to about the great things going on in their business today. And maybe they just put another truck on the road. Maybe they just made another key hire. And maybe they just landed a big contract. And with, you know, an amazing referral partner attached to it. So we explain to each other, you know, what’s going on in our world, and therefore paint the picture for the other members to see what they could also help us to create. It might create another opportunity for myself the presenter, but I might also stimulate somebody’s thoughts on how I could create an opportunity for them while I’m working in a particular facility. Maybe I could introduce them to the head of maintenance in that department.

I need is, you know, as simple as it sounds – just an opportunity to go around and say what other members can do to help us. So often, we will be recruiting, you know, for new talent and, you know, maybe you can introduce us to some other people in the industry. Sometimes we are looking for testimonials. I just realized that I need to build up the reviews I have on Yelp or on Google or on Facebook. And we work together in that and we work on proactive strategy and in that process as well, how we can create more referrals instead of just waiting for the map. And then finally, I will is our opportunity to make a commitment. That’s where the action plan comes in. Each member says, I will do this for another member before we meet again, so that there’ll be two weeks out to the next meeting. And it just creates that, again, proactiveness about creating referrals instead of waiting for it to happen, being reactive.

Ivan:
I like the concept. I have, I need, I will. So how often does your power team meet aside from a BNI meeting?

Jason:
We meet twice a month, always on the second and the fourth Wednesday, I believe it’s very important to keep the meetings consistent, so they’re always at the same location, the Perkins on this particular corner. We have a side room that’s set apart for us so that we’re not being disturbed by the rest of the commotion of the restaurant. And we meet for one hour from 8am to 9am. It’s about 10 minutes of open networking. At the beginning, everybody places a food order and gets the waitress out of the way. And then we go ahead and proceed around the table. And then three different, you know, roundtable events. So with 33 people, you can finish that still in an hour.

Ivan:
I have, I need, I will in an hour.

Jason:
Yeah, we at the most, we might run over five to 10 minutes on a, you know, an abnormal day, but for the most part, we get through the entire meeting in just one hour. And it’s very effective. We have several other key fundamentals that make the power team work, though, and we got to have some semblance of accountability. And so we have a simple attendance policy when a new member comes into the chapter and they want to join the power team. They have to come to two of our meetings consecutively in a row before we will allow them into our advertising plan together.

See, we share a rack card an advertisement that has each of our businesses represented on it, and the tool that we all use to create additional referrals for each other. We give, you know, these cards to our clients. And when you put this card in the clients hands and they skim over it, they say, “Oh, wow, I was looking for somebody to do pressure washing.” And they create opportunities for us to generate referrals. So the attendance policy says that you have to come in to meetings consistently in order to get yourself on to the advertisement. And then if you ever miss three meetings in a row, you’re going to be removed from the next printing of the advertisement. We’re not kicking you out of the power team or kicking you out of the chapter yet, but it’s a matter of getting back to that advertisement because it is so powerful, and, and the accumulated cost for each member on the power team to be on an advertisement. It’s only about $20 per quarter. And each quarter we print 5,000 of these which get distributed. So quarterly updates also allows us to cycle in the new members joining into the power team and cycle out maybe some members who have left.

Ivan: 
So let me let me just talk about the advertising because I love the concept. I don’t see too many chapters that have done that historically. But I think it’s a great idea. Certainly if your power team is large enough in it, and I don’t – you could be way smaller than 33 to be large enough to do that; you know, I think a half a dozen people is probably large enough to start doing the kind of print advertising that you’re talking about. So I love the concept. I think it’s a brilliant idea. And I love the attendance requirement, because, you know, you got to be present; if you want to be committed, commitment shows, you know, being present shows a level of commitment. So I love both of those and other fundamentals.

Jason:
I do want to go back to what you said, though. It started as a power team of just six members. And when I joined BNI nine years ago, the Referral Masters chapter was only 30 members at that time. And so as we’ve grown, and as a chapter and we’ve grown as a power team, as well. The power team is focused on specifically inviting to fill the gaps within the power team. But so as the chapter grew from 30 members to 105 our power team grew from six now to 33.

Ivan:
Yeah, that’s really impressive. You have achieved what most chapters really strive towards. So what well done to your group.  Are there any differences between online power teams?

Jason:
Well, I think you put out a great message as the world started to turn upside down a little. And you said, you know, don’t stop networking, just do it differently. And hence, we applied the same to our power team, we just switched to a virtual zoom meeting format, and we still get the job done. We’re still being proactive about creating introductions for others via email, and then we’re following up those introductions with additional Zoom meetings for one to ones even suggestions for doing power teams.

Ivan:
I have one in a moment that I want to give about large power teams, but do you have any other suggestions?

Jason:
Again, I think it’s just about being consistent. And if if you bounce around locations, the meetings tend to fall apart. Somebody always has a reason that they can’t make it. If you bounce around the times, you know, it falls apart. Again, you have to have the accountability. You have to have, you know, a proactive approach, creating the action plans to make sure things are getting done that are actually going to benefit each other. And quite frankly, the ideas get bigger and bigger all the time. We’ve considered what if we were to share direct mailers, you know. For one business owner to invest in direct mailers, you know, all alone, it can be very costly, but when you divide that cost against 20 or more participating people, it makes, you know, a significantly smaller bill to pay.

What about even digital billboard advertising? We’ve talked about ways that we could potentially brand together if you just had a universal 800 number that said, you know, trusted trades professionals and I’ll talk to a licensed contractor and then, you know, a variety of logos to chase around that billboard. Then you can set up a phone banking system that just directs calls. You know, if the client calls in and says, you know, for plumbing press one, for electric press two, and then that phone number would go directly to the trades member on the team. So, we haven’t launched these ideas yet. But this is, you know, where we’re heading. And it’s amazing what can be accomplished, we work together.

Ivan:
So for those of you listening to this, you’re hearing this isn’t a Power Team 101. This is in Power Team 401. This is graduate school power team play. So this is, you know, really advanced stuff. I think you’re starting small and working your way up like this is brilliant. Certainly by the time you have 33 members in your power team, which is just amazing to me. This is the kind of thing you should be doing. Here’s one other suggestion I’ve got, Jason, and if you have any closing comments, and we’re ready. But here’s my suggestion: as power teams get bigger and really stand out in a chapter, sometimes people who are not in a power team feel left out. And sometimes people that are in smaller power teams feel left out; it feels like you’re in the exclusive power team.

So here’s a recommendation I have for large power teams. You may or may not be doing this already, Jason, I recommend large power teams that are very successful is that at every meeting, invite just one person from either another power team in the chapter, or somebody who’s not in a power team, to just sit in on the meeting as an observer. You can have them share if you want, but sit in the meeting as an observer so that they can see just how incredibly powerful the power team process is. And they can take some of those ideas and apply them themselves. So it’s a very inclusive way of growing a power team as opposed to an exclusive way. I don’t know if you do that, Jason, but that’s a recommendation that I have.

Jason:
Well, actually, Ivan, that’s part of the success of Referral Masters as a whole. Again, the trades power team was one of the first teams to launch in there. But now as we’ve grown, we’ve inspired other power teams in our chapter with the results they’ve seen us achieve. So we now have a very strong financial power team with the banker and the accountant, financial planner, and we have a very strong real estate power team. We are working to grow on our wellness power team. We also have a marketing power team, and even a legal power team.

Ivan:
So these are great. And have you invited them to your team? That was the point that I was making. Try that.

Jason:
Absolutely. And because we’ve created such a presence for ourselves, we’ve actually had members from other chapters reach out to us and say, “I’ve heard about your power team. I’d like to come visit, “and we always welcome them with open arms.

Ivan:
Well, thank you very much, Jason. We’re out of time now. I hav, I need, I will. I love that strategy. Again, congratulations to your chapter. Really, really well done, Jason. I’m proud of your group. That’s everything for today. Priscilla, back over to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, well, I think that’s it for this week then. Thank you so much to both of you for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: Jason Avery shares the format his 33-member Trades Power Team follows to grow each other’s contracting businesses through referrals.

title.rendered: Episode 654: The Butterfly Effect

content.rendered:
Synopsis

The Butterfly Effect is a concept in chaos theory where huge effects can be traced to small causes. Something as simple as the flapping of the wings of a butterfly in a tropical area makes some minor change in the environment, which changes something else, which changes something else, which changes something else, which changes the weather.

In networking, the Butterfly Effect works like this. A BNI member called Ivan to ask a favor. Doing the favor led him to a speaking engagement, which led him to meet Jack Canfield, which led him to join an organization, which led him to meet someone who invited him to Necker Island, which led him to meet Sir Richard Branson.

When you meet someone, you never know who they know–and you never know who those people know. A small interaction can have big effects down the line. Never hesitate to make new connections. They can take you places you never imagined.

Read Dr. Misner’s 2007 blog post, “The Butterfly Effect of Networking.”

Complete Transcript of Episode 654

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody and welcome back to the official BSI podcast. I’m Priscilla rice and I’m coming to you from live recording studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the founder and the chief visionary Officer of DNI Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you today?

Ivan:
I’m doing fantastic Priscilla, and I’m going to share with the audience here that we just spent five or 10 minutes looking on the podcast to see if I’ve talked about a subject that I have done speeches on for years, but we could not find it. And that’s, that’s the Butterfly Effect of Networking. Now I’ve made reference to it in a couple of podcasts, but I’ve never shared the story apparently, you know, when you do, we’ve got six over 600 podcasts that we’ve done. And we do that many sometimes you don’t realize some of your long standing stories. So don’t get out as a podcast. And this is such a great concept that I want to do a podcast on it. Okay, great. Tell us about it. Well, it’s a story on how I came up with the concept. And I was thinking about this because I had just a month or so ago, I just spent a week in the Caribbean on an island called Necker Island. And it made me think of the first time I was there, and came up with this concept of the butterfly effect. So I’m going to share it with you here. This was 2007. I’m on this island in the Caribbean in the great room. A hugeroom that had 300 degree view of the Caribbean, there was just a little bit behind us that I couldn’t see. Otherwise I was able to see the entire Caribbean it was just so beautiful. And I had a deadline to write an article for entrepreneur at the time, my blog, IvanMisner.com. Around September 24th I think is when I did the article to this blog, and I was stuck. I had nothing.

Ivan:
I don’t know if you write but sometimes when you write you’re just completely dry. I couldn’t think of a single thing to write and I had a deadline to get that out. So I’m sitting there, completely clueless as to what I’m going to write about and while I’m sitting there, up walks Sir Richard Branson. Who owns Necker Island? You may or may not recognize the name, but he’s the founder of Virgin Atlantic, Virgin hotels, Vogue, Virgin cruise ships, Virgin Galactic- Virgin everything. And he walked by Priscilla. He looked at me and said, “What are you doing?” At the time in 2007, it was the only place you could get internet on the island, and I said, “Well, I had to come here to write an article and upload it. I’ve got a deadline today.” And he said, “Oh, well, I understand deadlines. But look, we’re down by the pool in the ocean. We’re having a blast. Come on down and join us. If for some reason I don’t see you, make sure to sit next to me at dinner tonight. I’d like to learn more about this networking thing.” And I said, “Sure!” and he walked off.

Unknown Speaker
I sat there and I thought, “Dang, how did I get here? What brought me to this moment in my life where I’m sitting on an island in the Caribbean and a billionaire walks by and says, ‘Hey, what are you doing? Come on out and play. Come join us. We’re having fun. And if I don’t see you sitting next to me in the lunchroom, I want to talk. I want to get to know what you do more.'” And I sat down, I thought, How did this happen? How did I get here at this moment? And so what I did well, as I was sitting there, is I reverse engineered how I got there. Who was it that invited me to spend a week on the island in the first place? It was a special seminar that I was invited to, and what was it that invited me, how did I meet them andnd where did I meet them? How did I get invited to the place that I met them- which was actually the transformational leadership council. I met the person who invited me to Necker at The Transformational Leadership Council which I’ve talked about many times on my podcast, it’s organization started by Jack Canfield.

Ivan:
It meets twice a year and has transformational leaders and thought leaders. And I was somebody that was in there that invited me and I thought, “Why did I get invited to TLC? I came from Jack Canfield, he invited me, so how did I meet Jack?” I reverse engineered it and it went back two and a half years. So this is 2007 it was somebody I met back in 2004 who asked me for a favor. The person who asked me for a favor was a BNI member and she was a solopreneur business coach and she asked me for a favor and -we all get asked favors and sometimes they’re really on mission for you and sometimes they’re not. Well, this is this was something I really wanted to do. She wanted me to do some training material for this coaching company. So I said, “Sure I’ll do it.” Now, there was no money upfront, but it’s possible down the road and I did it. And that led to being hired to speak for that organization, which led to me meeting Jack, which led to me meeting several people in TLC who one of which led me to be introduced to someone who knew about the seminar on Necker that then got me onto Necker Island. That whole process took two and a half years. And when I reverse engineered it, I realized that this is a great example of a concept called the Butterfly Efect.

Ivan:
The Butterfly Effect is a concept in mathematics called the Chaos Theory. The Chaos Theory is explained through the flapping of the wings of a butterfly. That something as simple as the flapping of the wings of a butterfly in a tropical area makes some minor my new change in the environment, which changes something else which changes something else which changes something else which changes the weather. And it hit me that what I had just experienced was the Butterfly Effect of Networking; That is that a simple conversation with a solopreneur who is a business coach, led me to meet somebody, which led me to meet somebody, which led me to meet somebody, which led me to meet somebody, which led me to spend a week on Necker Island and get to know Richard Branson.

Unknown Speaker
Having just spent another week as my third visit there, recently the story came back to mind and that became my article that day. You can find the original article on my blog now at IvanMisner.com. If you go back to September 24th 2007, you can see the original article that I wrote, and I’ll make sure there’s a link here in the podcast. But here’s the relevance to anyone listening to this podcast; You never know who someone knows, and who they can introduce you to. Who it is that they can introduce you to. Now, that person may not be a Richard Branson, but they’ll know somebody who knows somebody, who knows somebody and if you keep following those connections, it’s like the ripple effect by dropping a pebble in the water. You drop that pebble and there’s a ripple that goes out and that ripple connects you with somebody else, who connects you with somebody else, who connects you with somebody else. So I know that I mentioned the Butterfly Effect of Networking -at least I’m pretty sure I did, in the podcast called Networking Up that I did here on my podcast.

Ivan:
That is part of the Networking Up because when you’re networking, peer to peer or you’re networking with somebody who’s maybe not as successful with you, all too often we kind of think, “Well, that person doesn’t have any context that will help me then why would I? Why would I connect with them?” Well, the truth is, they know people, who know people, who know people and this one simple introduction led to multiple relationships and a lot of very strong friendships, including Jack, who was just on our podcast a few months ago. I met him as a result of of this and it’s led to several visits on Necker and had an opportunity to spend a fair amount of time with Branson and I know I’ve talked about him and humility. He’s a very humble person. It’s really quite amazing. One of our upcoming podcasts I plan on talking about how he is at networking and how he is as a person and how we can all learn from that, because oftentimes, I have found really successful people can be very arrogant.

Ivan:
Your ego enters the room before they do, but his does not. I’ve been very impressed with that. So I plan on in a future podcast talking about how someone could become a billionaire and not be totally arrogant. What I see as some of the characteristics of that, and I’ll tell you what, I’ll include a photograph but maybe we can put up in the podcast of Richard, my wife and I on one of our very first visit there. He invited us to a new island that he just bought Moskuito Island- not like the insect but with a K instead of a cue, Moskuito Island. And He just bought that island and he invited us for a little tour of the island, which was really quite amazing. I’ll put a photograph of that. But the the Butterfly Effect of Networking is very powerful. Here’s the secret; Look for people who are good at what they do. Whether you’re networking down, peer to peer or networking up, look for people who are good at what they do, make a connection, and then follow that connection through the process. It can lead to amazing places. And by the way, when you find yourself in one of those experiences, where you go, “This is what he (Ivan) has been talking about. I’m experiencing the Butterfly Effect.” It’s really important to pick up the phone and call the person that started the flapping of the wings of that effect.

Ivan:
In my case, it was a woman named Kim was a BNI member solopreneur business coach. So two and a half years later after I had worked with her, I called her up and she said, “Ivan, well, what a surprise! It’s great to hear from you.” I said, “I was just calling you to thank you for introducing me to Richard Branson.” And I’ll never forget, she was like, “Uhh, hello, Ivan? I don’t know a Richard.” And I said, “Well, let me tell you a story…” And I told her the story about how my meeting her led for me to be on Necker Island.

Ivan:
So the last thing I want to leave you with is if that happens to you, pick up the phone and call the person that started the process of your own personal Butterfly Effect. And that’s the podcast for today.

 

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody and welcome back to the official BNI podcast. I’m Priscilla rice and I’m coming to you from live recording studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the founder and the chief visionary Officer of DNI Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you today?

I’m doing fantastic Priscilla, and I’m going to share with the audience here that we just spent five or 10 minutes looking on the podcast to see if I’ve talked about a subject that I have done speeches on for years, but we could not find it. I sat there and I thought, “Dang, how did I get here? Ivan:
You know what, that’s a great question. And when I do the next podcast where I’ll talk about some of his characteristics that I think are a really healthy characteristics for any business person, I’ll add that. Because he did absolutely have an impact. I thought I was pretty good at something but observing him, I thought, “Oh, yeah, I could be better.” So I’ll share that on the next podcast I do about Richard in a few weeks.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. I look forward to it. Well, that was some wonderful information. I just wanted to let you all know that the sponsor for this podcast is Ivan’s Inner Circle. Go check out the great content available at www.IvansInnerCircle.com where Ivan has assembled a variety of interesting topics for you to learn about and participate in.
