In baseball, an “at bat” is the opportunity to get a hit. It’s not a guarantee of a home […]

title.rendered: Episode 623: The 24/7/30 Follow-up System (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 448.

Synopsis

Today’s topic comes from Dr. Misner’s book Avoiding the Networking Disconnect. Dr. Misner’s co-author Brennan Scanlon came up with this system for following up with new contacts after meeting them at networking events. It’s called the 24/7/30 Follow-up System.

  1. When you meet someone at an event, drop them a note within 24 hours after meeting them. Handwritten notes are the best, but using email is better than not doing it.
  2. Within 7 days, connect and engage with them on the social media platform they use the most. Do not sell to them.
  3. Within 30 days, set up a face-to-face meeting (in person or by video conference). Do not make this a sales call. Make it a relationship-building opportunity.

Yes, it’s difficult to make time for a lot of face-to-face meetings, but without them, you’re never going to take the relationship to the next step.

Do you have suggestions for improving follow-up? Share them in the comments.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcript of Episode 448

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing great, Priscilla, and I have a topic that is really out of a new book that I have written. I have mentioned it a couple of times on the podcast, Avoiding the Networking Disconnect. If you are listening to the podcast, you can get it on amazon.com. Avoiding the Networking Disconnect.

A lot of the stuff in this book that BNI members might find valuable. I want to talk about a piece of it because I am often asked by people, “What is a good follow up system?” My co author came up with this concept. His name is Brennan Scanlon. He is an Executive Director there in Ohio, a great BNI Director. He and I wrote this book together, but this concept was originated by Brennan.

You know, I often see people make the mistake of meeting a lot of new people at networking events but then not having a system in place to follow up with the new contact afterwards. So they ask me what is a great way to follow up?

So here is a simple follow up formula that we call the 24-7-30 follow up system.

When you meet someone at a networking event, drop them a note within the first 24 hours. It can be a personal hand written note or an email. Use whatever approach you do consistently. Let them know that it was a pleasure meeting them and you hope your paths cross again.

The key is you have to do this consistently. People ask me which is better, an email or a handwritten note? Generally, a handwritten note, I think is the best. Nobody does it anymore. The problem is, Priscilla, I know I have talked about this in a podcast. I don’t do them. I have horrible handwriting. And you know, I have not found a card yet that has spellcheck.

Now, we do have BNI members with Send Out Cards. That is a great program. I love that and programs like that where you can do it online and send out a personal card.

In either case, follow up with either a handwritten note, a professional card through someone like that organization which I have recommended for many years, or an email. I am probably a little better at the email, so that is what I tend to do, or send them a card through an organization like Send Out Cards. I use Send Out Cards. That is where I fall. So within 24 hours.

In seven days, connect with them on social media.

Priscilla:
Oh, really?

Ivan:
Yeah. Make a connection via LinkedIn or Facebook. Follow them on Twitter or join them on Google+. Find ways to connect and engage with them via social media platforms that you and they use the mose.

So find the one that they use the most. I have some people who just are Twitter fanatics, so I try to connect with them on Twitter. I have others who are all about Facebook, so I try to be sure to connect with them there. Some don’t like either of those. They are completely LinkedIn.

Find a way that you can connect with them, but particularly in a sandbox that they like to play in. Find ways to connect and engage with them via social media platforms they they use. Don’t do this. This is really important- don’t do this as a way to sell to them.

Priscilla:
Right. I hate that when you get stuff from LinkedIn. Somebody asks to connect and the next thing you know, you are getting pitched by this long email.

Ivan:
It drives me crazy, you know. When I say yes to these connection requests- and it is ironic that it tends to happen more on LinkedIn than anything else. I say yes to one of thee connection requests and then within a couple of hours, I get this, “Thank you for the connection. I really appreciate it. I have a new product or service.” I am like, oh, really?

My favorite was the one who sold lead generation lists. He opened up with “I read your profile and I am very interested in what you do. I supply lead generation lists.” I am like, liar, liar, pants on fire. You never read my profile. You would have known that I have no interest in a lead generation list. He is just using it for cold calling.

Don’t sell to people. That is not the purpose here. Try to engage in a conversation. You know, tell them, “Hey, we just met at a recent chamber event or a recent BNI meeting and I loved this post that you did. This is a great post.” Something like that when you make that connection.

24-7-30. Within 30 days, reach out to them to set up a face to face meeting. If you live near each other, meet in person. If you live far away from one another, set up a meeting via Skype or phone. At this meeting, find out more about what they do and look for ways to help them in some way. Don’t make it a sales call. Make it a relationship building opportunity.

I have said this before on my podcast recently. It is more important to be interested than interesting. You want to be interested in them and ideally, look for opportunities or ways that you can possibly help them. But no matter what, it is about building a relationship.

So, Priscilla, if you use this 24-7-30 system to follow up with the people you meet, I believe you will develop a powerful routine that will help make your networking efforts meaningful and successful. Use technology to help remind you to follow up at the proper intervals.

I use my Outlook calendar all the time to remind me to do certain things on certain days. After you send the first note, set up email reminders on your phone or calendar to follow up at the one week interval, at the one-month interval. As the late, great motivational speaker, Jim Rohn, used to say, “The fortune is in the follow up.” That is very, very true.

Priscilla:
Okay, so the way that I am thinking about this and the resistance that I have- I don’t have any problem with connecting within 24 hours. I don’t have any problem trying to find them on Facebook or LinkedIn or whatever within a week. But scheduling an in person meeting on the 30th day or within 30 days- I hesitate to do a whole lot of meetings, so I am just- I know that is wrong, but what do you say to that because it takes so much time?

Ivan:
It did take time, but networking is more about farming than it is about hunting and this is part of the farming process. You know, I think everyone in BNI understands that is about relationships. It is all about relationships.

How do you build the relationship? Well, look. Not everybody is in your BNI group, so we are talking about followup not only inside of BNI but outside of BNI. You go to the chamber function, or Rotary, Kawanas or Lions. When you go to a fundraiser, these are great opportunities to follow up. Certainly, visitors who come to your BNI group, that is a great way to follow up.

How does a relationship begin? It is at that first meeting and then at that second step, where you are getting to know them a little bit better. You may know them a little bit better. Hey look, I know we did a podcast a little bit ago about kissing a lot of frogs. We kiss a lot of frogs to find our prince or princess, to find somebody who we may be able to support and help in some way.

But if you don’t follow up, I can guarantee you that you will never build the relationship that you want with the people that you need to build relationships with to build their business. You have to follow up. It really starts with, I think, that face to face or that Skype meeting. You are never- I say never, but it could happen occasionally.

Even a blind squirrel can find a nut. We can stumble over business. But real referral business comes from relationships, and relationships come from meeting people with some regularity.

You know, we all have friends whom we have lost touch with, and we have lost touch with them through benign neglect. It wasn’t on purpose but we just kind of stopped connecting with them. If you never start to build that relationship, you are never going to have that relationship.

Yeah, it is kind of a leap to call them and set up an appointment, but you set it up for the right reasons. You know, “I met you at [such and such event]. I have been following what you are doing on Facebook [or LinkedIn]. I would really like to know more about what you do. This is really just a relationship builder for our businesses. I would like to learn more about what you do.” Get together over coffee or if they are far away, do a Skype call and “tell me more about who you are and what you do.”

For me, networking is all about relationships and this is a step toward building that relationship. If I made those calls, 9 out of 10 people would say yes.

Priscilla:
I like your idea about the Skype call. You know, just because it might be 45 minutes but you are all ready to go to your next event. You are not hung up in the coffee shop.

Ivan:
I usually only recommend the Skype call when you are too far away from each other. There is nothing better than meeting with somebody, shaking hands and looking them in the eyes and having a conversation.

Priscilla:
That’s true.

Ivan:
Skype is okay when you can’t do the face to face, but I really recommend the face to face over Skype.

Priscilla:
I agree.

Ivan:
So 24-7-30. You can find the full description of it in my new book, Avoiding the Networking Disconnect with my co author Brennan Scanlin. We will put a link up to it here on the podcast. I would love to hear your comments if you have any suggestions on how to improve your follow up or how others can improve their follow up. Please put it here on BNIpodcast.com. Thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
That’s great, Ivan. Thank you so much. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: The 24/7/30 Follow-up System provides a proven method for developing relationships with new contacts you meet at networking events.

title.rendered: Episode 622: Don’t Be That Guy

content.rendered:
Synopsis

A BNI member once told his BNI director that he wasn’t going to renew his membership because he wasn’t getting enough business. The director was surprised, because he had attended this chapter’s meetings and seen people give this members referrals. “Yeah,” the member said, “But it hasn’t turned into business, so I’m not renewing.”

The vice president of that very same chapter then called the BNI director to say that they weren’t going to renew that member because there were so many complaints that he didn’t follow up on referrals.

Suggestions for Following Up

  • Follow up on the same day you get the referral. If you wait, the prospect may hire someone else.
  • Ask your referral partner for information about the prospect and their relationship.
  • When you reach the prospect, start by talking about the person who referred you.

What if you get a referral that doesn’t seem like a good referral? You still need to follow up—but this time, with the person who gave you the referral, to explain how they can turn a lead into a great referral for you.

Complete Transcript of Episode 622

Priscilla:

Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by The Founder and Chief Visionary Officer, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan! How are you today?

Ivan:

I am doing fantastic today, Priscilla, and today’s topic – unusual title, “Don’t Be This Guy” is about not following up on referrals. I am going to tell you a true story. You are going to think I am making this up. I am not. It is an absolutely true story. I had a BNI Director who once told me about a member who called him and said he was going to be leaving his group. He was coming up for renewal in about a month and he was not going to renew because he wasn’t getting enough business. 

The Director said, “I am a little confused because I have been to the meetings and saw that you have gotten referrals.”

He said, “Yes, it’s just not turning into business for me. I am not going to be renewing.”

So the Director said, “Okay, I understand. I understand. I am sorry that it hasn’t worked out.”

It’s a sad story but it gets worse. That very same day,  the Director received a call from the membership committee of that very same chapter. It was the Vice President, and the Vice President told the Director that they were not going to renew a particular member and they wanted to tell the Director because the Director knew the member. The Director said, “Okay, who is it?”

And it was the guy who called in the morning!

The Director said, “That’s interesting because he said he wasn’t getting any business and he wasn’t going to renew because he wasn’t getting any business.”

The Vice President said, “Are you kidding me? We are not renewing because there have been too many complaints about him not following up on the referrals he has been given by the members.”

Can you believe that? So here is a member who is complaining he is not getting enough business but he is not following up on the referrals to the point where members have gone to the membership committee to complain that this guy is not following up on referrals. I think it’s really important to reiterate something I have been teaching for a long time and that is when you give a referral, you give a little bit of your reputation away.  

If you give a referral with a strong recommendation and that service provider doesn’t actually follow up with the contact, it is not only the service provider who looks bad. You look bad for giving a referral to somebody who didn’t follow up. 

Priscilla: 

Right. So true.

Ivan: 

That is just incomprehensible to me. So here are some tips on what to do when following up. First of all, when you follow up, you should follow up, I believe, the same day. So if you get a referral in the morning, by golly, before that day is over – as a matter of fact, I always followed up before lunch time. I would follow up and make a phone call to the referral. 

Now, I believe follow up that same day unless the member specifically said, “Don’t follow up until Friday.” Or “Don’t follow up until tomorrow.” If they have given you specific instructions as to when to follow up, then put it on your calendar and follow up. Why let a referral go cold? I have seen it. I have seen people who have not followed up on referrals quickly. They followed up but they haven’t followed up immediately. And I have had members say to me, “You know what? They sat on that referral for a week and they got tired of waiting and ending up buying from someone else.” What are you thinking when you wait a week?

Priscilla:

Right. They obviously don’t need the work or they would call them.

Ivan:

You know, that may be correct but I actually think we get so busy doing stuff that we forget to be doing the stuff that will take care of us next week and not right now. They are not thinking of the future and that is probably what happens. When I was a management consultant and I first saw the data on how many businesses fail the first year and how many – when I saw that 50% of all businesses fail within their first three years, I used to be really surprised about that, Priscilla. But now that I have worked with small businesses for so many years, I am actually a little surprised that the other 50% make it after three years because of business practices like this where it seems so incredibly obvious. You follow up, you follow up immediately.

Now, how do you follow up? You follow up the same day. I recommend that you follow up, reach out to the person and the first thing you should talk about is not what your services are. The first thing you should talk about is the person who referred you. This is Networking 401; it’s advanced networking. Sometimes when people are new to networking, they are just excited to follow up on the referral. Someone referred me! And then they call up and say, “This is what I can provide for you.”

Well, think it through. Call and edify the person who referred you. For example, if Priscilla, you referred me to someone, the first thing I would do is ask you some questions about the person. How do you know them? How long have you known them? How is the situation? How did it come up? So I get a little bit of background information on it. Are they expecting my call? You can do all of that in 60 seconds or two minutes at the most. 

So then you go in armed with information. I would call the referral and the first thing I would say is, “Hey, I understand that you have been a friend of Priscilla’s for a long time” or what ever the situation is. If you just met, “I understand you just met Priscilla,” but generally speaking, you are giving referrals to people who you have known for a little while. Then I would edify you.  I think it is so important if you give me a referral, that the first thing I do with that referral is tell that referral how great it is working with you. And here is a good reason for it for me. That is that a third party testimonial is incredibly powerful. You are my third party testimonial. So rather than me call the referral and start bragging about myself, what I want to do is brag about you because the one thing that referral has in common with me is you. And so if I am talking about you, that helps warm up the relationship a little bit before I start launching into how I can provide whatever services that I need to do.

So follow up the same day. First of all, get information on the referral and then follow up the same day. Don’t sit on it, and then edify the person who referred you. That is so important for a successful referral.

What are your thoughts?

Priscilla:

Right. Well, sometimes I think what might happen is you receive a referral that is really just a lead and so you may put it on the back burner. You don’t go to it right away because it doesn’t seem like a real valid referral.

Ivan:

That has happened to me. My argument would still be you have to follow up. But you follow up with a different person. So if you get a referral and you really feel it is not a valid referral, you have to follow up and you should follow up that day also. But you follow up with the person who gave you the referral. You sit down with them and talk to them about the referral and how it feels like possibly a lead and not a referral, and what it would take to make it a true referral.

So when I was a BNI member, I would get leads like that. You don’t want to put them on the spot. Be professional. Don’t get angry with them. But say to them, “Thank you so much for thinking of me. I appreciate it. Let me tell you how this would be a great referral for me. If you could call them and let them know that I am going to be calling because you said they don’t know I am calling – in order for me to call – otherwise it is just a cold call and they don’t know who I am. If you could call them and say, ‘Are you willing to talk to him? If so, I will have him give you a call.’ And when they say yes, that’s a legitimate referral for me. Then if you have any opportunity to talk about your experience in working with me and why you are recommending me, those would all be really good things, and that would make this a legitimate referral.

I did this all the time with people when I was a member and I have recommended it for years. It works. It works in one of two ways. One, it helps the person who is giving you the referral recognize what a good referral is so they are giving you good referrals. And it also helps if they are just giving away a referral so they can say that they gave a referral, it is calling them on the carpet in a professional way, in a polite way. 

By the way, I would always then take that referral slip to the membership committee or to the VP and say, “This isn’t quite a referral yet. Don’t count it. He’s working on it.” And I would have them not count it. That is what I did because I don’t want people giving away referrals that are clearly not a referral. Now, we have all given referrals that didn’t turn out. That is different. That is clearly not a referral.

So my answer to your question which is a great question and I am glad you asked it, is that you still follow up, but you follow up with somebody else. Make sense?

Priscilla:

Okay. Yeah totally. 

Ivan:

There is one other point. I did a blog on this topic and there was one point were somebody said to me is this a cultural thing, you know, where following up is just really necessary in America but maybe in other countries it is not as critical? My answer to that person was, “Following up on referrals is part of an entrepreneurial culture.” That’s not an American culture. Don’t accept poor business practices and blame culture. Poor businesses practices are poor business practices. Not following up makes anyone look bad in any culture at all. 

So there is a rest of the story, there is a little bit of an ending to the story. Priscilla, would you like to hear the end?

Priscilla:

Sure. 

Ivan:

So the group did not renew the member who wasn’t following up and I heard that that member now has a job. Their business went under. What a surprise. Now has a job at the Department of Motor Vehicles.

Priscilla:

Oh no. 

Ivan:

So if you are listening to this podcast, I want you to repeat with me out loud or in your head if you want. If you don’t follow up on referrals, you are not going to get more business, and then you may have to take a job at the Department of Motor Vehicles. That’s it for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:

Okay. That’s great, Ivan. Thank you so much. Well, this podcast has a new sponsor. Go check out the great content available at www.IvansInnerCircle.com, where Ivan has assembled a wide array of topics and you are invited to participate. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: If you don’t follow up on referrals, you’ll never get business–or new referrals. Here are 3 tips to help you follow up faster.

title.rendered: Episode 621: Doing an Annual Marketing Plan

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Jared Yellin, co-host of Ivan’s Inner Circle, joins Dr. Misner on the podcast this week to talk about why your company needs an annual marketing plan and how to create one.

The Annual Marketing Plan Formula

This formula provides your marketing with the most important ingredient for success: consistency. Each year, you need to provide

  • 12 Free Webinars. Offer a 30-45 minute educational webinar each month on a topic that falls within your zone of genius. Hosting regular webinars builds expert status faster than anything else.
  • 4 In-Person Workshops. Host a free in-person workshop every quarter. Having a live audience helps you see where the audience is fully engaged and where you need to dial it up.
  • 2 Appreciation Experiences. Twice a year, host an event to show your clients and customers to thank them for making your business possible. Retaining your existing clients is just as important as
  • 2 Referral Initiatives. This is when you and your team go all-in on generating referrals for 30 consecutive days. Make it fun and offer prizes for the most referrals.
  • 2 Online Challenges. Fourteen-day challenges are the greatest way to create a sense of community on Facebook. Challenge your clients and prospects to join a private Facebook group and help each other achieve a goal over 14 days.

If you plan these activities out a year in advance, you’ll be amazed at the way your business grows.

Complete Transcript of Episode 621

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by The Founder and Chief Visionary Officer, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan! How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am finally back home from a very long trip to San Diego, Mumbai and Bangalore, so it is good to be back in my home office in Austin, Texas. 

Priscilla:
Oh great.

Ivan:
So I have a guest on this week, Jared Yelling. Jared is a serial entrepreneur. He has consistently started companies to support small business owners and fellow members. His mission, Sinduit, is to implement one million annual plans with one million change maker entrepreneurs. He has already supported over 100,000 small business owners over the last ten years. Outside of his role as the Founder of Sinduit, Jared prides himself on his number one value, which is family, which consists of his wife, Lindsay, their precious daughter Tailey, and these are Jared’s words not mine, their stud muffin of a son Riker. I love it, Jared. Welcome to BNI Podcast.

Jared:
It’s my honor to be here I am. Very excited to share some information on a topic that I am very passionate about. 

Ivan: 
I love this topic. You and I did a webinar on this. Just so everyone knows, Jared is actually the host of my Ivan’s Inner Circle platform which is now [at time of publication, August 2019] the sponsor for BNI Podcast. Jared is the person who produces the material with me for Ivan’s Inner Circle, so you will hear his voice a lot if you go to IvansInnerCircle.com and sign up on that. As I have mentioned in the past, 100% of the royalties that I get on that go to the BNI Foundation. Jared, I thank you so much for everything you have done for IvansInnerCircle. I personally appreciate it. 

Jared:
It has been fun. The group is throwing fire. They are benefitting from the inspiration, education and community that has been built. So it has been my pleasure, and I am excited to serve this podcast as well because building an annual marketing plan for me changed my life and it has for tens of thousands of others as well.

Ivan:
So what are the ingredients in your annual marketing plan?

Jared:
The key with an annual marketing plan is it forces the most important behavior and skill that exists for business owners, which is consistency. When we put together this formula, we actually call it the annual marketing plan formula. We spent months and months and months just going through our own marketing initiatives as a company just figuring out what there the consistencies around what works? What we found is that there are actually five numbers. It’s 12, 4, 2, 2, 2. 

So the first number, 12, is the number of webinars that a business should offer yearly. This means each month, you commit to doing a 30 to 45 minute free, educational webinar on a topic that falls within your zone of genius. There are many reasons why this is important. Actually, we have an entire module in Ivan’s Inner Circle that goes deeper into this, but one of those reasons is I have never found a strategy that allows you build expert status faster than hosting your own regular monthly webinars. That is the first number, 12. One webinar every month for the rest of your life or at least for a year to prove that this works for you.

The next numbers if four. Four is the number of in person educational workshops I recommend for free that you are going to hipster each year. So one every quarter. The reason why this is so important is because it also will help you. Build expert status, but this time it also helps you become a more effective communicator. The only downside to a webinar is you really can’t see your audience. You don’t know when you are really interesting and you don’t know when you are kind of boring. So when you are hosting an in person workshop, you can see where the audience is fully engaged and also where you need to dial it up a bit. There are other benefits as well.

The next number is two. Two is the number of appreciation experiences that you host each year. I recommend every six months. These are parties for your clients, your customers or your patients. This is your opportunity to say thank you for making my business possible. So many businesses focus all their effort on acquiring new business and very little on retaining existing business. If you really want to build your version of an empire, you really need to get rid of that revolving door where one person comes in the front door and one goes out the back door because you can’t grow that way. So hosting appreciation experiences were Hulu can express your. Gratitude is the perfect way to achieve that type of community based business that I am sure all of you want to build. An appreciation experience can be really creative like a wine night or you are going to show a movie screening at your place of business, or an awards ceremony or a happy hour or any fun initiative that you and your team come up with. Do it twice a year.

The next number is two.  This is the number of referral initiatives that you are going to host each year. Now, referral based marketing should be part of everything that you do in your business. One of the things that we have found is that when you commit to 30 days twice a year, so that is 60 total days over the course of the year, and you and your team go all in on generating referrals, you will actually generate more referrals during those 60 days than the entire year combined. So go all in and make it fun. It can be a healthy competition where there are prizes and people who can win awards. There is a variety of ways to do this but twice a year, go all in with referrals. 

The final two is a new strategy that we have started to deploy over the last year. It is online challenges, so we have found that 14 day challenges online are the greatest way to create a sense of community, especially on Facebook. That is where you will actually lead the challenge, and it is also perfect when it comes to your marketing plan because this is an opportunity for you to invite people to participate in something for 14 full days. The way they participate is they join a closed Facebook group that you set up and then they start and finish the process together, whether it is a challenge to drink more water, build their brand, maybe it’s design the perfect room in their home, organize their closet. It all depends on whatever your zone of genius is, but twice a year, host 14 day challenges, where you would invited your current clients, customers, patients, plus inactives plus prospective clients customers, and patients to join your Facebook Group and then take them through this process for 14 days. 

That is the 12, 4, 2,2, 2 model that tens of thousands of people have successfully deployed, and it is creating an enormous amount of time freedom for them in their business. 

Ivan:
So it was like drinking water from a firehose with all of that. A lot of great content. Give us the numbers again and in a sentence, what each one applies to just sort of as a wrap up for everybody.

Jared: 
Sure. 12 is the number of webinars that you are going to do each year, so one webinar every single month. You can do those on Facebook Live or on any webinar platform that exists.

The next number is four, the number of in person educational workshops that you do each year. I recommend quarterly. 

The next number is two. Two is the number of appreciation experiences that you host each year. I recommend one every six months.

The next number is two — referral initiatives each year. I recommend one every six months and then finally, the number is two, which is online challenges. 14 day challenges. I recommend doing these once every six months as well. 

Ivan:
So the suggestion here, and I really like this, Jared, is to create a plan with this framework so that you are thinking ahead for the year. On what webinars you want to do, what in-person events you want to do, what appreciation events, what referral initiatives, what online challenges, and if you lay that out here in. Advance, then everything that you are doing is striving towards achieving that. Is that the basic idea?

Jared:
Yeah. Here’s whatI have found. You just cant score points on defense. As small business owners and solo entrepreneurs, you live on defense in a reactive mode most of the time. And I get it when you are small and maybe when you are just getting started, there are resources to start building a team. So my recommendation is invest time on the front end to build your marketing plan for the entire year. It becomes at least a baseline for your business. At least there is a webinar happening every month. At least there are workshops happening quarterly and appreciation events happening twice a year and two referral events and referral challenges as well to create engagement. It puts you on offense. That’s really where you start to build your business.

Ivan:
So why did you spend time building this framework? Was it to put people on offense and not be in a defensive position?

Jared:
Yeah, Sinduit was one of those companies that grew so quickly so fast, and I just felt completely chaotic and 

I knew it wasn’t sustainable. I had no time for my girlfriend who is now my wife, my team was burning out. I didn’t really see the light at the end of this controlled chaos that I built and then I realized one day that I was just introspecting, like where are we struggling? We have no marketing plan. Everything we do is by the seat of my pants, ideas that I have in the shower, when I am working out, when I am running. We have a plan. A formulated plan. I spent months like I said before, dissecting things that we did in the past that work. I was interviewing other businesses that scaled their companies, finding out how they marketed their business and formulate these ingredients but over the years we really refined it.

But I did this to scratch my own itch because I felt like I was on defense and I wanted to shift to offense.

Ivan:
Well, we need to wrap up soon, but let me ask you how are businesses benefitting by implementing this annual marketing plan? To me, it is obvious, but I think it would be good to share. 

Jared:
It is the most liberating feeling when you have done it. I speak around the country and even the world on this subject and do. Probably about 150 webinars a year. And the most common question I ask is who in this room or who in this virtual room sees the benefit of having a 12 month marketing plan? Literally 100% of the room raises their hands. Everyone wants it/ It is irrefutable. Having a 12 month marketing plan is the holy grail for your business, but the direct benefits: it’s liberating. It is also time freedom. When you know you have a plan laid out, you now have time to focus on the priorities and activities of your business. It also creates space for opportunities because opportunities will show up, you can plug them into your plan and then it creates consistently when it some to acquiring new business, retaining existing business, and also reactivating business that is sitting there, waiting for you to reach out. Now, you are going to be communicating with them on a very consistent basis.

Ivan:
Right. Well, listen, you can’t hit a target that you are not aiming at and so I have always believed in creating plans. What I love about your plan is it is very user friendly. Anyone can sit down and do it. I think it is a fantastic way to create a marketing plan. If you are listening to this and you would like to have a whole one hour webinar on this topic, go to IvansInnerCircle.com. Take a look at who is participating in that. That’s a training platform, as webinar platform, but also it is part of that forum, what Sinduit offers, which is an online system of creating touchpoints with your customers and potential customers, reaching out to them and communicating with them. I obviously recommend Ivan’s InnerCircle, but I also strongly recommend Sinduit to support your marketing as well.

Well, Jared, do you have any last thoughts before I wrap?

Jared:
No, I just want to tell you how much I appreciate you. I appreciate this exceptional community that you have built. Our warcry at Sinduit is this. Together we achieve more. I know that you agree with this as well, so thank you for this honor. It has been my pleasure and I am excited to support as many of you are ready for either Ivan’s Inner Circle, Sinduit or the combination of both. Thank you again.

Ivan: 
Thanks Jared, over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay great. I think that is it for this week. Thanks to. Both of you for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Jared Yellin shares his annual marketing formula: webinars, workshops, appreciation experiences, referral initiatives, and online challenges.

title.rendered: Episode 620: Adding More Fun to Education Moments

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Jennie Edwards joins Dr. Misner this week to share tips for Education Coordinators. Jennie uses characters, songs, and costumes to liven up education moments and keep the fun in the fundamentals.

Implementing Imagination into Education

Storytelling is an important part of education moments because we remember stories we hear. Think about how you can present the information you find in this podcast or on Ivan’s blog in a memorable way. If you’re talking about the Classification Cowboy, try wearing a cowboy hat.

Involve Members in Experiential Learning

Interactive exercises increase memorability. Can you turn your education moment into a game or contest? How about turning the lesson into a song? You may not be an actor or a singer, but think about what you can do to turn your presentation into an experience.

Take baby steps toward expanding your comfort zone around creating experiences. Watch inspirational videos (Jennie’s are a good example) to get some ideas about what’s possible in a presentation.

Storytelling Empowers BNI Members

As you practice using storytelling and expand your comfort zone, you start to stand out from the competition and become more memorable. Your fellow members will start sharing your story with their contact sphere.

Listen to Episode 588 for more about keeping all your BNI presentations exciting. Check out Jennie’s YouTube channel to see some examples of short, engaging presentations.

Complete Transcript of Episode 620

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by The Founder and Chief Visionary Officer, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan! How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Bangalore, India this week. This is my third visit to Bangalore. We’ve always had amazing turnouts here in India. The Indian members are on fire, especially for the top that we are talking about today, which is education. My wife has an opportunity to visit a Blue Bell school, which is a school here in Bangalore that we have supported for many years and is part of the BNI Foundation, and actually from the Ivan MIsner Foundation we too have supported it as well.

Today’s topic is about education moments. I thought I would briefly mention how the the Education Coordinator got started, because we didn’t have it when I first started BNI, it started probably about 10 years later when I had started writing books and members would say to me, “How do we share this at our chapter?” And so we tested it out in Phoenix, Arizona. Yes, Phoenix, you were the first region in the world to test it out.

It worked so well that we opened it up to a few more regions and it has worked there well. We now have it globally. Every chapter around the world should have an Education Coordinator and today we are talking to a fantastic one; her name is Jennie Edwards. She happens to be a photographer and videographer who helps entrepreneurs create their message online, make a bigger impact in the world, and over the last 12 years Jennie has been running a successful business of empowering her clients to grow their audience, connect authentically and gain clarity with their goals and vision through video and photography. She is the current President of her chapter and has been Education Coordinator three times. Jennie, welcome to BNI Podcast.

Jennie:
Thank you so much, Ivan.

Ivan:
Well, it’s great having you here and I’ve seen some of the videos that you have done on education moments, and I’ve got to tell you, you definitely keep the “fun” in FUNdamentals. 

So there’s three topics that we want to talk about today. The first one is “implementing imagination into education.” Oh and by the way, if you are an Education Coordinator, you have got to listen to this podcast. If you are not an Education Coordinator, go tell your Education Coordinators about this podcast. And if you are thinking about becoming an Education Coordinator in the future, this is awesome for you and make note of it. But I think this podcast will work great for any member of BNI. So, with that Jennie, lets get started. We’ve got three talking points. The first one is “implementing imagination into education.” Talk to us about that.

Jennie:
So, I absolutely love the idea of educating through storytelling and most of us tend to really hold on to the storylines that we hear that are shared. I love utilizing storytelling coming from an inspiration of an Education Coordinator position coming from the podcast, IvanMisner.com. Ivan you put out so much great content that needs to be shared in relation to what the chapter needs to hear. So, as an Education Coordinator I was always in communication with the President on what the chapter may need right now and how I can research content for the podcast and then turn that into a really imaginative message to share with the chapter. I utilized different modalities with costumes, because I have a full costume collection and accents, to kind of develop the character that we are giving an education moment. It was not just me standing up and delivering this content that I’m reading form my phone and in a a monotone voice, but really putting in more infusion of imagination.

Ivan:
You know, this is aimed at Education Coordinators, but really a weekly presentation could use some of this as well.

So, you mentioned visual and that you actually have costumes, but let’s also talk about the visual experiential learning styles as a contract.

Jennie:
It’s really coming to the memorability. So, in experiential they say that deeper learning can be attracted be my memorability as well and to the subconscious minds of the viewers or listeners, depending on how you are delivering your message, by creating an experiential learning education moment that people are more interactive and there’s ways that they can get involved rather than just sitting there and only listening to the information. You need to make it more dynamic and more excitement to the morning routine to wake everybody up. There’s so many fun things that I will share as well like some of the different characters that I utilized. For example, I had a pirate and her name was Roxy Razor Nails and she was talking about adding culture, “because culture eats strategy for breakfast. Arrg!” 

Ivan:
You definitely get into them. I love it, this is great. 

Jennie:
Another favorite from our chapter was Hector the Connector. He had a large sombrero and he was connecting the right people and power teams. Oh, another favorite was Aphrodite the Disco Queen. She had an afro and her platforms because she had to invite the right people to the party, which was the play on “Who’s in your room?” 

Ivan: 
I love it! So, I believe in the experiential, I think it’s a great way to teach. When I taught management theory, I had a lot of students at the university that I taught at, and they just hated having to take a management theory class. But one of the things I did was have a lot of experiential learning. So, when I talked about specialization for example, which can’t be that much more of a boring than that topic specialization and the power of specialization. It’s a boring topic. But then, I had an experiential exercise that showed the student how through specialization, you can increase productivity and business. It was a fun game exercise! What was really interesting was at the end of this quarter, my students said, “I came to your class dreading this topic, but it was the best class I’ve ever had at the university!” This is like what you are talking about, those experiential learning moments.

Anything else on that?

Jennie:
Yes. Well, I have another couple favorite characters that to share too if you want to hear about it.

Ivan:
I do!

Jennie:
One favorite was Rita Rawhide. She was a cowgirl and she taught about wrangling in new members. You know, because you got to, “Invite them in! Fire them up! Turn ‘em out! Chat ‘em up! Check it out! Set ‘em up, Rawhide!” 

Ivan:
That’s too funny! Hey, do you know who one of those main actors was? I’m really going to date myself.

Jennie:
Who?

Ivan:
Clint Eastwood.

Jennie:
Oh, wow! 

Ivan:
He was one of the main foreman in Rawhide. He wasn’t the main character, but he was one of the main characters.

Well, I love it. What else is there? You said you had a couple.

Jennie:
Oh, we did a spoof of- remember that song “Ice Ice Baby?” We turned it into “Give give baby.” We made a whole rap to it and a dance, I actually partnered with our Power Team Coordinator. So it was all about educating people about their power team, “Alright, stop. Collaborate and listen. Power Teams are back with a brand new convention… Give, Give Baby.” 

Ivan:
Alright, so a lot of people are going to listen to this and go, “I’m not going to stand up there and sing. I’m not a good poet, I can’t write this content.” What do you recommend for people who aren’t as dynamic as you clearly are, Jennie?

Jennie:
Well, I recommend anyway that you can expand your comfort zone wherever you are. It’s just a matter of taking baby steps towards it. Maybe it’s starting by watching an inspirational video and then getting inspired by someone else that is doing. A little something different than what you already accustomed to. Just push yourself a little farther because I know personally in BNI, I’ve been a member for eight years, that it’s been such a growth process and to have the opportunity to get up and speak every week really gives you your mini-stage to be a public speaker and play with it too. Grow and expand.

Ivan:
I like the idea of growing and expanding. I have got to tell you Jennie, I am not going to stand up and sing a song, but if you can do it, then I think that is fantastic! But the key is to expand your comfort zone a little bit to be entertaining. I believe stories are sticky. That when you tell a story, the concept behind it sticks. When you stand up in a character, it becomes sticky. You’ve seen that with some of my podcasts like the “Classification Cowboy.” That phrase is very sticky. People will remember it. The stickier your presentation can be through stories, the more effective.

So, how can teaching education moments empower members and let me expand it a little, how can your weekly presentation in terms of educating people about what you do, how do does that empower members?

Jennie:
As they expand their comfort zone, it empowers them to really stand out from the competition. By using storytelling in that it really helps you to become more memorable to your members, which is your sales force, and  by sharing your story with other people that they have in their network and their Contact Sphere. They are going to be lit up about it. They are going to be excited to share it. It’s not just going to be another run of the mill mundane thing, but it’s going to be fun to share. It helps with the education of it because it helps the members take action that really creates results. Obviously, they want to get the most out of their membership and take their business to the next level. So, just showing up and being more memorable really creates that process.

Ivan:
I couldn’t agree more. There’s another podcast that I would like to refer people back to, Podcast 588, “Don’t bore me to tears,” which was a podcast done by Charlie Lawson and I. That’s really about weekly presentations but it’s a similar concept, don’t bore people to tears. 

Jennie, I think you probably have some of the most dynamic Education Coordinator presentations that I’ve seen. I am really proud of you and you have done a great job! Any last thoughts before we wrap up?

Jennie:
I just want to empower people to look at what you could do for your next weekly presentation or your education moment to you really be more memorable. Take that baby step out of your comfort zone, show up and feel more confident!

Ivan:
Outstanding! For more information about Jennie, her website is YourDynamicStory.com. You can see a lot of the kinds of things that Jennie does. 

Before we wrap up, I understand that you are an Education Coordinator yourself, Priscilla. Is that correct?

Priscilla:
That is so true, Ivan. I have been for several years because I have the best coach, which is you. So, it’s been great! People want to hear about what we teach on the podcast, from the books, and information that you tell me and other people. That has been really helpful.

So, Jennie, I’m not sure that I would be able to wear costumes and sing and dance, but it’s very inspiring. I will be looking for your wonderful videos online and see how I could take that baby step.

Ivan:
Well, Jennie, thank you so much. I appreciate it. Priscilla, over to you.

Priscilla:
I think that is it. Thank you so much for the great information!

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
