excerpt.rendered: Jennie Edwards shares her tips for bringing imagination and storytelling to education moments in BNI. A must-listen for Education Coordinators.

title.rendered: Episode 619: Be a Dog with a Bone (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 507.

Synopsis

One of the reasons for Dr. Misner’s success is his persistence. Like a dog with a bone, he never gives up. While you do need to let things go sometimes, determination gets you a long way in business. The World’s Best Known Marketing Secret went through 44 rejections before Dr. Misner found a publisher. It’s now been through four editions and sold more than 200,000 copies worldwide.

Excellence is not a single act. It’s a habit. If you want to be successful, think things through and then act on that like a dog with a bone. Often times, the more successful you are, the more failures you’ve had. If you believe in your objective, keep going.

Complete Transcript of Episode 507 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Miser and many other networking experts. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Charlotte, North Carolina this week for the the U.S. Conference. That is where BNI Headquarters has moved to. Although I live in Austin, our global headquarters has moved to Charlotte. That is where we are doing our conference. I am meeting lots of members and Directors from all over the U.S.

Priscilla:
That sounds a really good and interesting.

Ivan:
Yeah. The conferences are always fun. They are really good.

Priscilla:
So are you a dog with a bone?

Ivan:
I am absolutely a dog with a bone. This is part of kind of what I call the “Ivanisms Series.” I have been doing videos, a couple that I call Ivanisms. This is one of them. I am thinking about doing a book with all of these phrases and why they have worked for me.

So I am absolutely a dog with a bone. The lesson here is I never give up. Be like a dog with a bone. I have built a global company that operates in more than 70 countries. One of the reasons for my success is my persistence, tenacity and doggedness in dealing with challenges.

Priscilla, I can tell you I may not be the smartest man in a room or I may not be the most talented man in a room, but I am almost always the most determined man in a room.

As part of a commencement speech, Winston Churchill is believed to have once said, “Never, never, never ever give up.” I love this quote. Now, to be honest, though, the left side of my brain says when it comes to business, that is just not logical and people need to know when the time has come to give up.

Fortunately, my right brain generally wins the battle on this issue. You see, I may not, as I say, be the smartest guy in a room, but that determinedness, that doggedness has served me well in business. So as long as you can kind of get a sense of when it is time to let something go.

There are things that I have let go, but when I feel certain, when I have great information and feel confident in my vision and my goals, then I am a dog with a bone in my focus in not giving up.

So here is an example: In 1992, I completed the manuscript for The World’s Best Known Marketing Secret. 1992. The book came out in 1994. Over an 18 month period, I sent my manuscript out to 45 publishers all over North America. Out of the 45 publishers, I received 44 rejections to my submission.

Priscilla:
Oh no.

Ivan:
I swear. I know this number for a fact because this summer when I was moving my office from LA to Austin, I came across the file. It’s a handwritten spreadsheet and I counted 44 rejections for my manuscript.

Priscilla:
They just dribbled in every few days?

Ivan:
Every few days, yeah. Because I would send out, you know, this was back in 1992, so you couldn’t send it digitally. I had to print out a 200 page manuscript, put it in an envelope, mail it. So you know, I sent out maybe three or four a week. I was getting maybe three or rejections a week back. And I received 44 rejections to my inquiry, but I didn’t give up. The 45th publisher said yes, and that became The World’s Best Known Marketing Secret. It was published in 1994.

Since then, it has four editions. The book has been published in four editions. It has been translated into more than 10 languages. It is so cool seeing all of those languages up on my bookshelf. And it has now sold over 200,000 copies worldwide.

People rejected it saying the book wouldn’t sell. It has now sold over 200,000 copies. Had I given up at submission number 44, I may have never become a bestselling author in multiple markets around the world. That and a lot of other experiences that I have had over my lifetime have led me to believe that if you are confident in what you are doing, never give up. Never, ever, ever give up.

If you are persistent, I believe you will get it. If you are consistent, I believe you will keep it. Pick a lane, stay in it and do it over and over. We are what we do repeatedly. Excellence- this is really important. If you are listening to this, I am giving you ideas that helped me create a global company. Excellence is not a single act. It’s a habit.

People look at BNI and say, “Wow. You are really lucky. It’s great.”

I tell them, “Look, it was 30 minutes of inspiration and 30 years of perspiration.”

You know, it is not a single thought. It’s something I did repeatedly. So there is no secret routine or magical process. What there is is confidence, belief and hard work on consistent basis and a desire to succeed. It’s not about perfect. It’s not about being perfect. It’s about effort.

When you bring that effort every single day, that is where transformation happens. That is how change occurs. If you really want to succeed in business and in life, think things through. Think things through and then act on that like dog with a bone. That’s my message for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay. I just have one question, which is how do you deal with all of that rejection?

Ivan:
Yeah. So, look, people say, “Well, you must have thick skin. It must not get to you.”

I don’t know anybody that it doesn’t get to a little bit. It certainly, you know, got to me. I believe that you have to define yourself on your successes not your failures. Everybody has failures. Oftentimes, the more successful you are, the more failures you have had.

I know people have heard this. I am not saying anything new. There are people out there listening to this going, “Yeah, I know, but-” Whatever the “but” is, it doesn’t matter. People get depressed over rejection. I certainly do. But that is what I am talking about with being a dog with a bone- don’t let that feeling of anxiety and depression over not achieving your objective get in the way of your success. Hang on and keep doing it.

I mean, think about it. 44 rejection letters. Nobody called me up, and some of the rejection letters were pretty brutal. Maybe something just shy of “This sucks”. They were not very nice. So everybody feels that way, I think. I certainly do. I can tell you that for a fact, and you just have to work through it and keep your eye on the ball. Keep doing what you know to be true, and I think you will achieve it.

Priscilla:
That’s a great story. It’s difficult because it is hard to get rejected, but I think you are encouraging us all, so that’s really great.

Ivan:
It is hard to get rejected. No question about it. I think it also gives us strength. You know, especially — here’s the thing– especially when you finally achieve the success. First of all, I believe it makes you feel stronger. It’s like I did this. I pushed my way through this issue and I achieved what I wanted to achieve. And the beautiful thing about it is you can then be a great resource to others, saying, “Look, I did it. You can do it.”

There is no great big secret. It’s about picking a lane and staying in it, like I said, and doing it over and over and over. You heard me say in the past, “Do six things a thousand times, not a thousand things six times.” That’s what I am talking about here. Be a dog with a bone.

Priscilla:
Umm-hmmm. That’s great information.

Ivan:
Thanks, Priscilla

Priscilla:
Thanks, Ivan. Well, I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Excellence is not a single act. It’s a habit. Persistence pays off. Be a dog with a bone if you want to achieve success.

title.rendered: Episode 618: Talk to the Wallflowers

content.rendered:
Synopsis

In a recent conversation about networking, Dr Misner’s friend and colleague (and frequent podcast guest) Dr. Mark Goulston said “People should always introduce themselves to the wallflower in the room.”

No one attends a networking event wanting to stay in a corner and be alone. They’re in that corner because the most technically skilled people are often socially shy. You never know when you’re going to meet the next Bill Gates.

About 10 years ago, Dr. Misner attended a party hosted by Virgin Galactic. He noticed a man standing outside by the pool, looking very uncomfortable–and then realized this was Burt Rutan of Scaled Composites, the man whose work the party was celebrating.

Dr Misner walked up and introduced himself, and had one of the most interesting conversations he has ever had at a networking event.

Not every wallflower you meet will be Burt Rutan, but the chance to talk to someone with a different perspective is well worth your while.

Listen to Episode 97 to hear about Dr. Misner’s discovery that he’s actually an introvert.

Complete Transcript of Episode 618

Priscilla:

Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary’s Officer, Dr. Ivan Misner. How are you and where are you. Ivan?

Ivan:

Hi Priscilla. So this week I am at a Transformational Leadership Conference event at San Diego, California. I am there and next week–I think is going to be a classic podcast episode–but next week I leave straight from San Diego to Mumbai and Bangalore, India, so I will be in India next week. 

Priscilla:

Oh wow, you are such a traveler. 

Ivan:

You know, I try to visit as many BNI regions as possible and meet as many BNI members as possible. India is just on fire for BNI. They have been doing an amazing job, so I am happy to go back.

Priscilla:

Wow. That is great. 

Ivan:

So this week is an unusual topic: talk to the wallflowers. 

Priscilla:

Tell me about it.

Ivan:

Yeah, so I was recently talking about networking with a really good friend of mine, Dr. Mark Goulston, who I am pretty sure has been on a podcast or two. Mark is a psychiatrist consultant. He has been on my podcast before. He said something to me when we were talking that intrigued me. He said that people should always introduce themselves to the wallflower in the room. 

I was thinking about that, saying nobody every attends a networking event wanting to stay in a corner and be left alone. They are in that corner because the most technically skilled people are often socially shy. I started thinking you know, you never know when you are going to meet the next Bill Gates. I just thought that conversation with Mark was really interesting and I started wondering have I experienced that myself? I realized that Mark’s comment really resonated with me because it reminded me of a time a number of years ago, probably about ten years ago when I was at a party put on by Richard Brandon’s Virgin Galactic and because I had been to the island a couple of times, I was invited to this party put on by Virgin Galactic. It was relating to the testing of the. White Knight II, the mother ship that will take the spaceship up high and then the spaceship launches from the mother ship and goes into outer space. 

So I was at this party with a ton of amazing people and I walked outside the party and looked over in the corner by the pool – it was a party at someone’s house – and there was a man standing by himself looking really uncomfortable and very much out of his element. And then I noticed who it was it was Bert Rutan, the founder of the aerospace company scaled composites and the designer of the spaceship, too. He was by himself at the part. There were hundreds of people celebrating the work of the company he founded. And they were celebrating Virgin Galactic. He was out by the pool by himself in a corner. I thought this is an opportunity I can’t pass up. 

So I went up and I introduced myself to him and I asked him “Do you go to many of these events?” He looked at me and said, “Well, counting this one?” I said yeah. He said, “Well, that would be one.” I was like, “So you have never gone to anything like this?” He said no. I said, “Why did you decide to go to this one?” He said, “Because Richard asked me to come.” Richard being Richard Branson and the Founder of Virgin Galactic. 

Although Bert didn’t seem very outgoing in the setting, it was an amazing conversation that I was having so I pushed on. You have to understand he is an aerospace engineer, so definitely a techie, right? Big Bang Theory nerd. Very knowledgeable and well-known. He is an icon in his industry. I said to him,  “It must be incredible to see this amazing long term vision come to fruition.” He said, “Actually, this isn’t my long term vision of what I think the company can do.”

I was really surprised. “Really? So what is your long term vision?” He said, “Well, I believe the company can push forward past sub-orbital flights,” which is what they are still working towards — sub-orbital flights where they have passengers who will go up in outer space, be an astronaut by the way – on a sub-orbital flight, you are an astronaut. You get FAA astronaut wings. He said, “We can push past sub-orbital flights and allow space tourists to do full orbital flights all the way around the earth.” I kind of naively said at this point, “That’s an amazing long term vision.” He said, “That’s not my long term vision.”

I was like, “Okay, so what is your long term vision?” He said he felt that the company could provide orbital flights to passengers who could then stay at a hotel in space for a short time. Now at this point, I am completely blown away. Once again, I said, “That’s an amazing long term vision.” And yet again, he said, “That’s not my long term vision.”

At this point, I was, you know, mulling and I am completely fascinated with this visionary. So I asked okay, so what is your long term vision? He said, “ I believe we can launch flights into orbit, stay at a hotel in space and then take fights from that hotel around the moon and back. And that is my long term vision.”

Now, when I talked to Bert, he was probably in his late 60’s, I am guessing, when we had that conversation. I asked him one more question softly and gently. I said, “When do you think that vision can become a reality?” He said, “I think it can be done in my lifetime.”

So the British have a term for what I felt at that moment. I felt gobsmacked. I was utterly astounded by this man’s vision and I was incredibly honored to have had this opportunity to talk to him alone. Nobody else was standing there. He was the epitome of a wallflower. You know, I founded the largest networking organization in the world, BNI. I have met tens of thousands of people during my tenure in BNI. I can easily say that was one of the most interesting conversations I have ever had with someone at a party or at a networking event, Bert Rutan and of course Richard Branson’s vision of what could be done. 

Branson is definitely not a wallflower, but their vision of what can be done through their entrepreneurial efforts has left an indelible mark on me. I think the important lesson here relates to Dr. Goulston’s belief that we should always look for the wallflowers in the room. Now, not all of them are going to be a Bert Rutan. But I found that some of the most interesting ones are well worth the conversation because they are coming from a perspective that I don’t have, that other people don’t have. No one is talking to them. They are generally very happy to have somebody come on up and say hi and have a conversation with them. I would argue that every now and then you just might walk up to a wallflower and meet a future Bill Gates or Bert Rutan and that alone makes the effort of finding those wallflowers all worthwhile. 

And that is my message for today, Priscilla. 

Priscilla:

That’s great. I love that conversation. You are a bit of an introvert yourself, so you can identify with those wallflowers when you see them. 

Ivan:

I can. You know, I talk about that. Most people don’t believe that. They are hearing you say that and they are going, “Ivan is not a wallflower or an introvert.” And if you go back, I don’t know what podcast it is, but I talked about being a situational extrovert. I will look here as we are talking. I know I did a podcast on being a situation extrovert because I took a test. I thought I was an extrovert and my wife was like, “No, you are not an extrovert.”  Podcast number 97 OMG  I’m an Introvert.  It was really surprising to find that.

I am what is called a situational extrovert, that if I am talking about networking or something that I am really excited about, I come across as a extrovert, but generally, I am an introvert. Even thinking bout BNI, I could have started an organization that is a big party, a big mixer kind of network. But I developed a network that has people getting together every week, getting to know each other, building relationships, building trust. That is more of an introvert. It hit me over the head when I took that test and found out that I was only a situational extrovert.

I think the lesson here for BNI members is have a conversation with those people who are quiet, because you might find out some amazing things. These wallflowers, these quiet people, are oftentimes engineers or programmers, people with incredible knowledge and expertise. They are just not real outgoing. And I find them very interesting to talk to. That’s my message. BNI members, at the next BNI meeting,  go find the wallflower who is standing there with no one around them and have a conversation with them. I think you will be glad you did. 

Priscilla:

Okay. That is great. Thank you so much.

Ivan:

Thank you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:

So this podcast has a new sponsor. Go check out the great content available at IvansInnerCircle.com, where Ivan has assembled a wide array of topics and you are invited to participate. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.  

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Next time you’re at a networking event, introduce yourself to the wallflower. You could meet the next Bill Gates or Burt Rutan.

title.rendered: Episode 617: You’ve Given a Referral. Now What?

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Mark Applebaum joins Dr. Misner on the podcast to talk about building credibility both as a giver and a receiver of referrals.

Tips for Giving Good Referrals

  1. Make sure it’s a qualified referral. The person you refer should be expecting a call from your referral partner.
  2. Follow up with your referral partner. Check in to see how the referral is going and what else you can do to facilitate the relationship.

Tips for Receiving Referrals

  1. Follow up within 24 hours. If you can follow up within 2 hours, your chances of closing business increase.
  2. Ask the referral how that person knows the BNI member who referred you. Talking up your fellow BNI members is a good thing, and it helps start a conversation.
  3. Provide a status report to the person who gave you the referral. Let them know what you’ve done to follow up.
  4. If it turns out not to be a good referral, thank the giver and explain why this person was not a good referral for you. (If you can refer that person to someone else in your chapter, even better!)

Episodes 281 and 439 talk about how to deal with bad referrals.

Complete Transcript of Episode 617

 

Priscilla:

Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary’s Officer, Dr. Ivan Misner.

Hello, Ivan! How are you and where are you?

Ivan:

Hi, Priscilla. I am fantastic. This week I am in Charolette, NC at BNI Headquarters. I go a few times a year so I am there this week.

On my podcast today, I have someone who is back for his third visit to being a guest on BNI Podcast. His name is Mark Applebomb. Mark has been a ember of BNI for 14 years and he’s been a BNI Director for 4 years. Mark has been married to his wife, Tiffany, of 18 years and he has 2 children, Ryan and Jenna. Mark’s passion when it comes to BNI is teaching members on how to give visitors a “WOW” experience. I think that’s a great thing to teach. Mark is the Marketing Director for Hepworth and Associates Law Firm. Mark, welcome back to BNI Podcast!

Mark:

Thank you so much, Ivan! Thanks for having me.

Ivan:

So, we’re talking today about “You’ve given a referral, now what?” I think there are two different topics related to this that you want to cover. One is how do you build credibility as the giver and how do you build credibility also as the receiver in the referral process? So, lets start with the first one. How do you build credibility as the giver?

Mark:

You know when members join BNI, they join to build their business. But once they join, the number one thing they have to build is their credibility. Obviously this podcast is about building credibility, but we are going to start with being a giver and giving a referral to a receiver and what they need to do. The first thing they need to do is obviously first make sure that referral is a qualified referral, not just a name on a slip of paper. A name on a slip of paper is what we call a lead and I jokingly share with my chapters, “I’m from New York, you can say any four letter word to me and it doesn’t offend me. But in BNI, a lead is bad four letter word.” Now, obviously if the member in the groups is just asking for names- like sometimes title companies will ask for For Sale By Owner signs just to take a picture of the sign, but you want to make sure that the referral you’re giving that the person who’s name is one that referral slip is expecting a call, so that when their receiver calls them, they know that they are getting that call.

Ivan:

Mark, you mentioned an exception which I think is really important to understand and that is if the person that is receiving the referral says that, “I’ll accept something less.” A referral is the opportunity to do business with someone in the market to buy your product or service. But if someone says, “I’ll take it as a bona fide referral if you see a For Sale By Owner sign and you send that to me.” And that’s not really a normal referral, but if the individual accept that that way, that’s fine. The key here is, what is the person getting the referral want, not what you want. So, if you’ll except that, that’s great but if you haven’t made it official that you’ll except that as a referral and if I give that to you that would just be a lead. Does that make sense?

Mark:

Correct. 

Ivan:

So, what else about credibility for the giver?

Mark:

So, the giver, we’ve all heard the phrase “The fortune is in the follow up.” Now, this works both ways. From the giver to the receiver and to the receiver to the giver. In the giver to the receiver scenario, what would really impress that receiver- let’s say I’ve given you a referral, Ivan, and I followed up a couple days later and say, “Hey, How’s it going? Did you reach out to them? And if you have or if it’s not going well and you need me to reach out to them again for you, what can I do to make this process smoother for you?” Doing that for the receiver is huge. It really builds a lot more credibility because you’re goal is to help the receiver build their business and if you do more steps for them in the referral to make it happen, it really helps to close the deal. 

Ivan:

And that’s for the receiver?

Mark:

Correct. That’s for the giver to do for the receiver to follow up with the receiver to say, “hey is there anything I can do with that referral party to make sure that you as a receiver close at the end?”

Ivan:

Got it. Well that’s good. That’s sort of advanced networking, You know most people don’t do that but I think it’s something that would add a lot of value to the referral process. 

Mark:

I agree. Now the next thing would be from the receiver’s standpoint on how to build your credibility with the giver.  And referral marketing, we know that when we receive a referral that the giver is putting their name on the line and trusting you with that referral. So, everything you do will help that process strengthen. Now, the first thing to do, again it’s “fortunes of the follow up” -when you receive that referral, my recommendation would be to follow up with that referral at least within 24 hours. But I’ve heard from some of our trainings that if you follow up with a referral in at least two hours, you are 77% more likely to close that transaction.

Ivan:

I couldn’t agree more.

Mark:

And the next thing is – and maybe this is more for some of you becoming a master networker, when following is giving the giver a status report Via text, email, phone i’m just letting the giver know that you’ve left a message with The referral party, we had a conversation with them or scheduled time to talk with them. That lets the giver know what’s going on with the referral. They don’t have to wonder what’s going on with that. 

Ivan:

Yes! This is good stuff. To me this is advanced networking. Instead of 101, we’re talking more of 301 or 401, that’s good.

Mark:

You never want the giver- this is the last thing you want to do, is have the giver reach out to you a couple of weeks after and go, 

“Hey did you ever reach out to that referral I gave you?” And the answer being no. That can obviously really damage the relationship and can have you miss out on the referral opportunity altogether. 

Ivan:

Yeah, and i’ve seen that happen by the way. That’s very frustrating when that happens. Is that your last one? Because I have a last one I would like to add.

Mark:

I have one more but you can go ahead and do whatever you like.

Ivan:

OK, go ahead.

Mark:

So, if the receiver calls the referring party and it turns out that it’s not a good referral, does that happen? Yes. Here is what I would recommend doing; Keep in mind that the giver, based on what do you know about your business, is most likely doing everything they can to give you a quality referral and they are thinking that they are giving you a golden egg or a great referral. In your mind, it may not be. In any relationship, communication is key. Here is how I would handle that communication with the giver;

First, I would always start off with gratitude. Again, if you gave me a referral, Ivan, and that was a quality referral for me I would say, “Ivan, thank you so much for that referral. Thank you for looking after me and my business and wanting my business to grow. Unfortunately, I wasn’t able to help them because they’re looking for somebody that does X service but I am only somebody that does Y and Z. However, I was able to connect your referred to someone that does X and is able to help them. So, in the future, if you do come across somebody who needs Y and Z, that would be a perfect referral. Thank you.“ 

Ivan:

Yeah, that is a real tactful way of doing it. I would also recommend that if you’re listening to this podcast and you’re having issues with the bad referral, check out episode number 439 and episode number 281. Both of those episodes talk about how to deal with a bad referral and it’s in alignment with what Mark just said. 

Let me add one more that I think is really important for the receiver of the referral to do- this is advanced networking as well. I think the first thing the receiver of a referral needs to do with the person who is being referred is edify The person who gave the referral. Mark, let’s say you referred me to Priscilla and I went to her, The first thing I should do is to say, “ Mark referred me. I’ve known Mark for years, He’s a really good business professional. How do You know Mark because I think he is a very great guy and business person.” Start up a dialogue that edifies you as the person giving the referral. That’s something that very few people do but it’s win-win in a number of ways. First of all, it makes you look good because I am stating how good you are. It also gives the person who I might be doing business with a place to start talking. We both like Mark. They know you, I know you- so it makes a good referral. That’s one that I would add there, Mark.

Mark:

Thanks for including that. That’s perfect. 

Ivan:

All right, we’re pretty much out of time but maybe Real quick there’s another thing that we thought we might talk about and That is; is there something members shouldn’t do when they are giving referrals?

Mark:

There are a few cases where this doesn’t become a rule but sometimes you’ll have members that during the chapter meeting have a certain ask. “I’m asking for Jim Smith from XYZ company.” Reading during referral and testimonial time, a member will say, “Here’s your referral from Jim Smith.” When in fact they never really spoke to Jim.

Ivan:

I agree with that. Unless you really, really no Jim well, you’re personal buds, he’s in your mobile phone and you know for a fact that you can make that connection, you should wait until you actually talk to Jim. 

Mark:

I want thing that is great about BNI, is this amazing app we have that after minutes the meeting is over, I can contact Jim and say, “Hey I would like to give you a referral, may I have somebody contact you?” Mediately put into the app that the member email or text saying that they received a referral.

Ivan: 

There are always exceptions to things, there might be a couple to this one, but as a rule what Mark is talking about is right on the money. You shouldn’t give a referral on the spot. You should confirm to make sure that the person is absolutely ready and willing.

We are out of time, Mark. Thank you so much for coming back on BNI podcast. It’s been great having you here for the third time!

Mark:

Thank you so much, Ivan.

Ivan: 

Priscilla, over to you. 

Priscilla:

OK, great. Thank you both for the great information. This podcast has a new sponsor! Go check out the great content at IvansInnerCircle.com where Ivan has assembled a wide array of topics and you are invited to participate! Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Mark Appelbaum shares tips for increasing your credibility with fellow BNI members when giving and receiving referrals.

title.rendered: Episode 616: Cross-Chapter TYFCB

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Jeremy Walsh joins Dr. Misner to explain a change in the way BNI Connect handles TYFCB (Thank You for Closed Business) across BNI chapters.

For many years, BNI did not allow referrals across chapters, because there was no way to know they were legitimate referrals. Today, with BNI Connect, it’s possible to track referrals and closed business across chapters, anywhere in the world.

TYFCB is the celebration piece of receiving a referral. It’s our responsibility as members to recognize our fellow BNI members for sending us new business. Your TYFCB stats measure your “Giver” status in “Givers Gain.”

You can find the TYFCB Worksheet that explains how to calculate the value of the referral in Episode 289.

With the BNI Connect app, you can submit your TYFCB right away–you don’t have to wait until the meeting. Just be aware that your numbers won’t change until your Vice President takes attendance and updates the numbers for the chapter.

If you have any questions about how to submit a Thank You for Closed Business, contact your regional director.

Complete Transcript of Episode 616

Priscilla:

Hello everybody and welcome back to the official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hi, Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:

I am doing fantastic today, Priscilla, and I have a guest who has been on BNI Podcast a number of times and I am bringing him back because we have made a change on BNI Connect. Change always makes people a little nervous, but I think this is a great change and I am bringing on Jeremy Walsh who has been on the podcast before and will tell us how this is all going to work. Jeremy has been on the podcast before and  Jeremy is in charge of important documentation for BNI worldwide. He helps to keep the BNI Connect project focused. The tech support team provides support 24 hours a day 5 days a week support for issues globally. The team is a global team and 99% of the inquiries are responded to within one business day. It is truly amazing to me. He has helped to help the online help forums which get 52,000 hits a month on BNI Connect, and we have a YouTube Channel which has logged 195,000 views. 

In his spare time, Jeremy owns The Right Click, an IT consulting business with 17 employees and he is a BNI member. Jeremy, welcome back to the BNI Podcast.

Jeremy:

Thank you so much, Dr. Misner. it is always great to be here with you.

Ivan:

It’s great to her here with you, and Jeremy, call me Ivan. Let’s talk about Thank You for Closed Business, this is such a powerful thing to know and members. If you haven’t been around here a long time, ten years ago, we calculated thank you for closed business based on my doctoral dissertation and we had a formula. Now we can track actual closed business. For many years, we did not allow referrals across chapters because we had no way to manage them or know they were true. By the way, when we first started BNI, we allowed it and it was a disaster because we had no way to track whether those were legitimate referrals. 

Well, that is different today with BNI Connect. We just pulled the trigger to allow I think every country. Is that correct, Jeremy?

Jeremy:

That is correct.

Ivan:

Every country now, you make make cross chapter referrals anywhere in the world, so I have a few questions because I know members are going to have lots of questions. Let’s start with the acronym TYFCB, Thank You for Closed Business. What is the purpose, Jeremy, of Thank You for Closed Business?

Jeremy:

This really is the celebration piece of receiving a referral. So it is really kind of our responsibility as members to make sure that when we get a referral, somebody goes out of their way and they generate business for our business, we want to recognize them for giving us our business. And they way we do that is thank you for closed business. Fill out a slip online, send it and say, “Hey, we are going to give you credit for generating this money for me and it is going to show up on your statistics. In Givers Gain, this is your measurement of being a giver in your chapter. 

Ivan:

Yeah, that is really important. From day one at BNI we wanted to do it cross chapter, but it just wouldn’t work. We couldn’t do it because we had no way to manage it, but we do now. So let’s talk about what should be recorded and maybe you could do a few examples of industries. By the way, if you are listening to this, go back and do a search  on the BNI podcast for the term “TYFCB” and you will see several podcasts I have done. One was with the then National Director of BNI USA, John Meyer. In that, we actually have a handout in PDF on how to do and how to calculate Thank You for Closed Business. Go back and listen to that. Jeremy, can you give a few examples?

Jeremy:

Sure. Absolutely. I am not going to lie: sometimes it can get a little bit complicated but we have to keep in mind the spirit of what this is all about. Generally speaking, what we are doing is we are recording the gross receipts to our company. None of us have to share our profit margins and what we are actually putting into out pockets. It is more about how did my company’s revenue increase by through this transaction? As a computer guy, if I sell a computer to somebody and do a couple hours of service installing it and maybe do a little bit of consulting on the side, whatever that invoice was, that typically is what I will turn in for Thank You for Closed Business. I don’t think I need to go in and remove the cost of the computer and profit margin on the memory upgrades and all that stuff but that is typically what we do when we turn in for Thank You for Closed Business.

Ivan:

And if it’s a real estate agent and you sell a house, it’s not the sale price of the house but the total price of the commission that gets split between the agents, the Realtor and a number of others, so you don’t have to say just the amount that the real estate member made but can say it’s just the commissions that was basically made, gross commission. 

By the way, you can find this on Episode 289. We have a PDF on that. You can also find it on Episode 524. Those two podcasts will help out out with this topic father if you want to go deeper. Here is the real question: now with BNI Connect, Jeremy, how and when do you record Thank You for Closed Business?

Jeremy: 

There are a couple of times when you should record Thank You for Closed Business. One of them is obvious and one of them is less so. The obvious answer is as soon as you receive money from a referral that was given to you, you should absolutely turn that Thank You for Closed Business in. The great thing about BNI Connect on the mobile app is that you can do it right at the time that you are doing your invoicing. Or you can do it monthly or weekly. Whatever is comfortable for you, but when that referral closes, that is when you should turn in that Thank You for Closed Business.

That is kind of the obvious one. The less obvious one is that, especially in my business, we get a lot of recurring revenue, so you may pick up a client who might be on some type of service contract. Or it’s the dry cleaners where you got referred to them but you are bringing your shirts and suits there every week or every other week. That dry cleaner should not only give you a Thank You for Closed Business the first time that they come in doing business, but every time they are getting business from that original referral. 

Ivan:

A lot of chapters have issues with that and I would leave it up to the chapter, but I am inclined to do what you are describing. In fact the person’s money is just as good the second time as it is the first time. The fact they they are going to go back is like, what is the big deal? Just give the person credit because you have a new client and they are coming to you every week and buying something. It is a small number but who cares?

Now, it can go to an extreme, I know. I have talked about it in a podcast once where someone sold a ream of paper every week, they tried to count that and that was ridiculous, but while I leave it up to the chapter, I recommend that you do what Jeremy says. 

Anything else that you want to add to the how and when?

Jeremy:

No I think that is it. Just be consistent with it and make sure that you know it is our responsibility to do it once we have received the money. Our gift really is the money in our pocket. The other member’s gift is watching their numbers go up in their PALMs report.

Ivan:

So are there any challenges regarding this that you have seen so far?

Jeremy:

Two things that I just want to bring light to in this electronic age, one of which is the timing of how everything happens and that is when you submit anything in BNI Connect, it’s important to remember that now that we are doing this immediately, not just in the chapter with pieces of paper, a lot of times we expect our numbers on all of our reports to increase right away. Thank You for Closed Business, as with referrals and one-to-ones and anything else, those numbers will only officially increase after your meeting has needed and your Vice President has gone back, taken attendance and submitted that report. So it is actually going to happen within a day or two of your meeting. So if you are looking for those numbers to change the minute you put it in, take a step back and wait until after your weekly meeting to watch those numbers change.

Then the second thing that I have seen with people out there is what numbers do change, especially when it comes to cross chapter. Ivan,   think somebody sent in an example question.

Ivan:

Charlie Lawson, who is the co National Director sent a question. I will give you the Reader’s Digest version of the question. Steve meets Sue at a training event and he mentions that he needs to get his car serviced. Now, they are both members of BNI, different chapters. Sue immediately thinks of Bob a garage owner in her chapter and passes Bob a referral and her card.

So Bob does the work and everybody is happy, but who does Bob thank for the business? Sue is the member of his chapter, but who does Bob thank? I think the answer is obvious. Bob needs to thank Sue because she gave Steve as the referral. It is almost irrelevant that Steve is a member. If Steve wasn’t a BNI member, it would be the same, but the fact that Steve was a BNI member, Sue is the one that would get the Thank You for Closed Business for passing he referral. Is that correct?

Jeremy:

Absolutely correct. Just the fact that it’s all BNI members involved, it still works that way. Now, let’s say that Sue is in a different chapter from Bob, so when Thank You for Closed Business is put in and Sue gets thanked, what it’s really important to remember is that when they are in different chapters, it is actually Sue’s chapter that will see its chapter statistics go up a little bit. Remember we are celebrating who generated the business. I think over the years a lot of people confused Thank You for Closed Business for their chapter as “This is how much money our chapter made from BNI.” It is really not that. It’s “This is how much money our members generated for other people in BNI.” But now who received the money might be split outside the chapter. 

Ivan:

Here is where people might get confused. Steve might think he should get credit for the Thank You for Closed Business, but he didn’t give the referral. It was Sue, so Sue gets credit for Thank You for Closed Business, which again, would be part of the same whether Steve was a member or wasn’t a member. It is the same basic process.

Jeremy:

Absolutely.

Ivan:

So you think this has  completely clarified everything, Jeremy?

Jeremy:

Let’s hope so, but you know what, if not, please always reach out to your Director. Your Director is an amazing  source of knowledge and also to make sure that you are doing things the same way in your region. That is really one of the most important things, consistency with the other people who are reporting locally. If not, reach out to us. We are here in Support. If we can’t answer your question, we will absolutely get you to the right place:  support@bniconnect.com or just click that little question mark in BNI Connect and submit a support ticket. 

Ivan:

Jeremy, you are doing an amazing job, BNI Connect is just so much more complex than people understand. The deeper you go into it, the more you understand just how incredibly complicated it is running the program in 70 countries with literally thousands of websites connected in different languages and currencies and all of the craziness. We appreciate you and appreciate everything that you do, Jeremy, thank you so much.

Back over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:

Okay that’s great. I think that is it for this week. Thank you both so much for the great information. I am happy to announce that this podcast has a new sponsor. Go check out the great content available at IvansInnerCircle.com, where Dr. Misner has assembled never-before-seen training on a wide array of topics including networking, communication skills, scaling your business and so much more. Thank you do much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Cross-chapter TYFCB is now part of BNI Connect. You can pass referrals anywhere in BNI–and receive credit for them.

title.rendered: Episode 615: Ivan’s Inner Circle

content.rendered:
Note 10/22/2024: Ivan’s Inner Circle is no longer operative, but you can find a ton of Ivan’s material over at misneraudioprograms.com.

Synopsis

This week Dr. Misner introduces Ivan’s Inner Circle. For at least 10 years he’s wanted to go deep on topics he’s expert in, but back in the days of Networking Now, the technology wasn’t there.

Jared Yellin of Synduit collaborated with Dr. Misner to produce 22 one-hour webinars (so far) that go deep into the material from Dr. Misner’s books–and these podcasts–with live Q&A sessions for members only. All proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

There’s also a private Facebook group with live videos for members of Ivan’s Inner Circle, and the webinar platform allows interaction with other members.

Modules already recorded include

  • Who’s In Your Network?
  • Networking Like A Pro
  • List Building 101
  • Santa Claus and Six Degrees
  • What’s Your Verb
  • Storytelling
  • Your Vision, Mission, and Core Values

Complete Transcript of Episode 615

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hi, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am excited to tell you that I am in Brazil this week.  It is the first visit that I have had in Brazil and I will be speaking to many, many hundreds of members and directors at their annual conference. So I am really excited to be here. 

Priscilla:
What city are are you in?

Ivan:
It is outside São Paulo. 

Priscilla:
Oh yeah, okay. I wish I could go to Brazil. I am jealous.

Ivan:
I am excited to be here. 

Priscilla:
Yeah, so what do you have to share with us?

Ivan:
We have an unusual topic. We have a new sponsor in BNI Podcast. That is Ivan’s Inner Circle. You and I were talking about this before we went on air, and the reason that I did this is that I had someone come to me a few months ago. His name is Jared Yellin, and Jared is the founder of Synduit, which is an online platform that does a lot of email development and also webinars and other things, a virtual marketing system. He came to me. A good friend of his is also a member of TLC, Patty Aubrey, who I know from Transformational Leadership Council. Patty referred me. Jared said, “Is there anything that you have been wanting to do for a long time that you haven’t done?” 

I said, “Yeah, you know, I have had this concept for at least 10 years.” Now, I am going to really put your memory through the test here, Priscilla. Do you remember when the sponsor used to be NetworkingNow.com?

Priscilla:
Yeah, definitely.

Ivan:
That was a long time ago. That was my original intent. The thing is the technology didn’t exist to do what I wanted it to do. NetworkingNow kind of faded away but technology exists today to do exactly what I had in mind. But I don’t have the time to do it. So Jared came to me. I don’t have the time to handle the platform. Jared came to me and said “ I can handle the platform. I’ve got that. What is it that you wanted to do?”

I said, “ I wanted to do something called Ivan’s Inner Circle. NetworkingNow is supposed to be a predecessor. I want to do something called Ivan’s Inner Circle, where I can go deep on topics that I really feel confident about and do webinars that are an hour long or longer and really go deep on these topics and then do live AskIvan events just for the members of Ivan’s Inner Circle.”

Jared said “I would love to develop that for you.” So he developed the material which is not the sponsor. The website is IvansInnerCircle.com. There is a cost for Jared to run the operation. I do receive a royalty and I will tell you, Priscilla, 100% of the royalties that I get I have him cut the checks to the Foundation.  They go to the Misner Foundation, and this year, the charity I am going to forward that to is the BNI Foundation.  So the BNI Foundation this year will get 100% of all of the royalties that I get.

Priscilla:
That’s great, Ivan.

Ivan:
You know, I didn’t do this for the money. I did it because I love teaching. That is why I do the podcasts. This is an opportunity to go really deep and I will continue every year to to donate the money to charity. So I thought what I might do, Priscilla, is tell you some of the modules that I have going. Then, if you want to talk about it as we go, I am happy to do that. 

So far, we have 22 one hour modules. Actually, some of them are even longer than one hour.  There are already in the works, of which I think 18 are already posted. So one of the first ones is out of the. Book Who’s in Your Room, but it is specialized for Who’s in Your Network? So it is an entire one hour module of creating the network of your dreams. It has really great content, some of which I talk about on the podcast, but you and I were talking and you said some of these things that you have talked about. Yes, I have, but 10 minute increments as opposed to an hour, where I can really go deep. 

The next one is great for BNI members and that is Networking Like a Pro. It is content out of my book Networking Like a Pro. Module 3 is Ignite Your Brand and that one is. Run by Jared and I give color commentary. Module is List Building 101 and that too is run by Jared because he is all about helping people build a list, and I give color commentary. Module 5 is about the Networking Disconnect, and how people just do networking really wrong. Module 6 is one that I haven’t talked about in a long, long time. It is Santa Claus, Easter Bunny and the Six Degrees of Separation material from my book The 29% Solution. Module 7 is about building an annual marketing plan, which Jared runs.   The next module is about Business Networking and gender, from the book Business Networking and Sex. It is about the difference between men and women. I had the chance to really go deep and talk about the findings that. We had, the 12,000 people that took the survey. We have one on Behavior Styles, which is sort of about the disconnection rate, but how it applies specifically to networking.

Jared did the next module on Social Media Marketing with No Budget. I don’t know if you remember the material I had from the book Truth or Delusion, but I did a whole module on Truth or Delusion. I did a module called Networking Up, and I know you and I talked about that on one of our podcasts, but this is again, an hour. Actually, that one I think went over an hour because there was so much to do.  

I have a webinar on What’s Your Verb, which is really a why do you do what you do and how would you describe yourself? One on webinars. One one storytelling. If you are listening  to this and you think I am a decent storyteller, I have a whole hour on how to rock the house with storytelling. How do you tell good stories. I have one – you know, people always ask me, “How do you manage your time? You are doing so many things. How do you manage your time?” So I have a module on mastering your calendar. There is one about affiliate marketing run by Jared. 

I did a module, and this one is so much content that it is actually Part A, Part B, so there are two modules on Garage to Global, which I think we touched upon briefly on one of the podcasts, how. BNI has gone from virtually my garage and office above the garage to a global enterprise with over 9, 056 chapters now. There is so much. There are so many lessons that I have learned over the last 34 years, that it actually ended up becoming two modules.

I have a module on How Do You Create Your Vision Mission And Core Values in Your Business? I have some content there that I have never really shared anywhere, not even on a podcast. I have about an hour’s worth of material there. Jared has one called Your Call to Action? How Do I Launch an Online Challenge? And then I have a module on the Secret to Balance, which I touched upon in the podcast but I spent a whole hour on it.

So far, we have 22 modules that are scheduled. Probably 18 or 20 at up now, and I would anticipate that you will have dozens and dozens of modules. With all of the things that I have learned over the years, along with things that Jared has given, we are also working on getting few other big names. I can’t say who yet because we don’t have them and I don’t want to make promises that don’t deliver but big names who are really well known doing modules with me that will be available only here on Ivan’s Inner Circle. 

Priscilla:
That’s great, Ivan. That’s wonderful. One of the things that I was struck by when I went into the website was just the possibility that people would have to kind of interact with you in a more direct way and learn from you in a more direct way. And I think that there are probably many BNI members who would like to. Be in your inner circle. 

Ivan:
That is what I am hoping. We are actually doing. Regular Facebook Live sessions only for Ivan’s Inner Circle. It is on a hidden Facebook page, only for people who have signed up for the Inner Circle, and I will be taking questions there and answers. Jared has put together a great program with Synduit. The platform itself allows for dialogue about each of my webinars so people can get on and ask questions there and have a conversation about the things that I have said. So there is a lot of interaction that can take place there and of course there is a lot of interaction directly with me through the Facebook page and the Facebook Live events that I have already started doing. 

Priscilla:
That all sounds good and I love that it is all being donated to charity. That’s wonderful.

Ivan:
Yeah, thanks. I appreciate that. You know, I honestly don’t need it for the royalties. I have written two dozen books,  founded BNI. We talked a couple of times about the fact that I own a property development company. I started my property development company three years before I started BNI. So you know, life is good. I am happy but I love teaching, and I can no longer teach at universities because I travel too much. So I had to stop teaching at universities a long time ago. And this kind of gives ma n opportunity to teach on a regular basis and do live conversations. 

So I am really excited about Ivan’s Inner Circle. I invite you if you are listening to this podcast to go to IvansInnerCircle.com, become a founding member. It is really reasonable as a founding member of the organization of the Inner Circle, and I hope to see you there. That is everything I have today, Priscilla, unless you have a question.

Priscilla:
No, that’s it. Thank you so much. I think it is great. So now all of you know that we have a new sponsor and it is IvansInnerCircle.com and you are invited to participate so it is going to be great. Thank you all for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Introducing Ivan’s Inner Circle, which combines 22+ in-depth webinars, a private Facebook community, and live Q&A calls into the best networking and business education available.

title.rendered: Episode 614: Soft Skills Make Strong Networks

content.rendered:
Dr. Misner is a firm believer in the power of the Law of Reciprocity: the deep-rooted psychological urge to do something nice for someone who did something nice for you.

There are several scientific theories that support the Law of Reciprocity, such as Nash’s Equilibrium Theory, which states that the best result comes when everyone in the group is doing what is best for themselves and the group.

Reciprocal Altruism involves an equitable balance between collective altruism and personal need.

Enlightened Self-Interest is another concept that aligns with the Law of Reciprocity. This idea, that you can do well by doing good, underlies Givers Gain.

Social cooperation is rewarding to our brains. No matter what you call it, reciprocity is about serving others. That kind of networking is where individuals enter and communities emerge.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, PRisilla, and I have a topic today. My PhD might be slipping out a little bit because it is a little academic but I think people will find it interesting.

Priscilla:
Okay. Tell us about it.

Ivan:
People who know me would tell you that I am not much of a new age kind of guy but as an author and businessman who started BNI, and we are in 70 countries around the world, I really believe in the immense power in the law of reciprocity. This is a part of networking theory, social capital theory. It is a concept that touches upon the deep rooted psychological urge to do something nice for someone who did something nice for you. So for people like me who understand the value of soft skills but who would also like to have some evidence behind why those soft skills work, I can tell you there is actually a lot out there to support the law of reciprocity, beginning with a concept called Nash’s Equilibrium Theory. 

Ironically,  Nash’s Equilibrium Theory uses the acronym NET, which I think is funny, but it basically states that the best result will come when everyone in the group is doing what is best for themselves and the group. It is a form of reciprocity and the optimal outcome of the situation is one where no individual has an incentive to deviate from their chosen strategy after considering the other participants’ choice. If this sounds at all familiar, you may have seen it in the movie Beautiful Mind. That is Nash and his theory is the Equilibrium Theory and he won a Nobel Prize off of this concept, but there are others, other theories that help support the Law of Reciprocity. 

One is Burrell Altruism. It’s another form of reciprocity. It involves an equitable balance between collective altruism and personal need. Collective altruism looks at the needs of the group but doesn’t give strong consideration to the needs of the individual, whereas reciprocal altruism attempts to consider both the needs of the group and the needs of the individual. 

Even in early writings by Socrates and Tocqueville about ethics and government, the concept of enlightened self-interest was all about reciprocity. Enlightened self-interest is a philosophy which states people who act to further the interest of other or a group or groups that they belong to, ultimately serve their own self-interest. In other words, it is possible to do well by doing good. Let me repeat that: it is possible to do well by doing good. 

In BNI, I incorporated the use of the term Givers Gain into the lexicon or operations from the very beginning of the organization. The underlying foundation of this truth is built on the concept that what goes around comes around.  But it is more complex than that. Networking is about relationship building. I found that the best way to build a relationship with someone quickly is to help them first.

If you can help someone, I don’t mean in person–giving them an introduction and information, an article–anything that serves their need–you will begin a professional relationship with them and creating a relationship also helps build trust. And trust is the cornerstone of effective networking, so that when you practice Givers Gain often enough, you will be on the road to a powerful personal network predicated on trust built through helping to serve someone else. So some of this is a little deep, but I think it is important for BNI members to understand that there is some philosophy, there are some theories behind what we teach with Givers Gain and the Law of Reciprocity. The concept of whatever team you choose to use serves as a bridge between individuals and a community of people for collaboration of all kinds. It fuels individual and professional growth, not to mention increased growth which is what BNI is about.

Research actually has shown that social cooperation is rewarded in to our brains, Priscilla. It is rewarding to our brains. Cooperation increases. The frequency of Dopamine release within the brain. Interestingly, dopamine decreases without social cooperation..

So each of the strategies I have talked about are about cooperation and collaboration and each can increase dopamine production in the brain. Some time ago, I received an email from a BNI member in India, Amruth. Amruth sent me an email with a great philosophy or a great metaphor for this philosophy. He said words to the effect of imagine that you have two spoons. A small spoon and a large serving spoon. Which one are we using the most?

The small spoon serves only us, but the large spoon allows us to serve others. The more that we all use the large spoon, the more we ill all have plenty for the small spoon. I love this metaphor. 

A networking group that uses a large spoon for everyone has amazing success for all. In the book The Go-Giver, co-written by my friend Bob Burg, who I have had on the podcast several times, they say, “The Income is determined by how many people you serve and how well you serve them.” You can call it Nash’s Equilibrium. You can call it reciprocal altruism. You can call it Enlightened Self-Interest. You can call it Givers Gain, all of which I gave a philosophical premise to each of those theories here in this podcast.  

But I believe that reciprocity is really about taking off your bib and putting on your apron. It is about serving other people. This kind of networking is where individuals enter and communities emerge. Those are my thoughts for this week’s podcast, Priscilla. Any observations before we wrap up?

Priscilla:
I do love that reference to the small and large spoon. And I know that – you know, even people who have problems  and get depressed, feel withdrawn sometimes, they talk about the advantage of donating your time or seeing if you can be of help to somebody. It is healing. So I think that what you are talking about is essentially the same thing. The Dopamine increase, it is really helpful to give. One of the things I love about BNI is is just creates better members of society, because you are just always looking at how you can help somebody else.

Ivan:
I agree and I think sometimes people look at this and go “Yeah, but what’s in it for me?” and I think that anyone who really does any research into the concepts that I talk about, Nash’s Equilibrium, Reciprocal Altruism, Enlightened Self-Interest or Givers Gain. If you do any kind of research, you find that what’s in it for you is that what goes around comes around. By giving you feel great and oh by the way, it comes back to you, and it comes back to you many times over. It’s 2x, 4x, 10x. That is the power of building community which is what BNI does. BNI builds communities all around the world where people support, get to know and trust one another.

Priscilla:
That’s right. There aren’t enough of those kind of infrastructure communities in modern culture so it is really important. 

Ivan:
Absolutely, so soft skills makes strong networks and I really believe that BNI is where individuals enter and communities emerge. I would love for education ordinations to share this with chapters. It is a little deeper than some of the things that I talk about on some of the podcasts, but I think it gives the philosophical construct behind why Givers Gain works and why it works do well. That’s all I have for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Ivan, thank you so much. For more of Ivan’s content go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Whether you call it Nash’s Equilibrium, Reciprocal Altruism, Enlightened Self-Interest, or Givers Gain, reciprocity is about serving others.

title.rendered: Episode 613: The Value of Visitors (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 522.

Synopsis

David Clegg joins Dr. Misner this week to talk about the value of visitors in BNI. Visitors pass referrals to BNI members, whether or not they join. The average Thank You for Closed Business for a visitor in BNI is $1000.

If everyone in every BNI chapter brought one visitor per month, the average chapter would experience a bump in TYCB of $330,480. (Numbers come from BNI Connect.) The average member would experience an average of $12,000 in additional thank-you-for-closed-business from visitors.

If you follow the Power of One, as discussed in Episode 517, you’ll increase your bottom line considerably. One of the easiest ways to invite visitors is to use the acronym GRIP: Do you want to GROW your business? Would REFERRALS help? Are you INTERESTED in …? Here’s the PLACE we meet.

If you invite visitors on a regular basis, your chapter will grow and thrive, and so will your own business.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 522 –

Priscilla:

Hello everyone, and welcome back to the official BNI podcast. Brought to you by The Networking Success channel on YouTube. Which features Dr. Ivan Misner and other key experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Liveoak Recording Studio in Berkeley California. I’m joined on the phone today by the founder and the chief visionary officer oh BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner.
Hello, Ivan! How are you and where are you?

Ivan:

I am in Galveston. I’ve got a little vacation getaway condo in Galveston. So I am working this week and will be taking vacation next week, but I will still have a podcast next week.
Today, I have a guest. It’s a very interesting story. My guest today is David Clegg. David had]s been with BNI for seven years, and he manages 6 chapters for BNI- He and I launched a chapter this year. David is going to talk about the value of a visitor and the power of one really impacts the amount of business being generated in a chapter. But, before you jump into that, David, welcome to my podcast!

David:

Thank you!

Ivan:

You have a great story as to what you do. Do you want to share that with everyone? I love the way you put it.

David:

Well, thank you. I tell people all the time that I work for my 18 year old daughter. My daughter when she was nine years old- of course she just graduated from high school this year, but when she was nine she asked me for spending money, and I told her no. I said I’ll never give you spending money, but I will teach you how to earn it! So we went around the neighborhood looking for dogs to walk, knocked on every door, and someone is going to pay you to walk their dog –  which she was surprised by. So she started walking dogs through the neighborhood, then we started watching dogs in the house, and about eight years after that, we now have- well what’s  been 10 year fully, we have a 12,000 square foot facility and we do doggy day care, boarding, grooming, training, and pet photography. It is called, appropriately, Alley’s Walkabout. That’s the name of my daughter, and she has 27 employees.

Ivan:

That’s an amazing story, David! Thanks for sharing, I really appreciate it.

David:

I’m real proud of her.

Ivan:

Yeah, I bet you are! And for good reason, my friend! So, talks to me a little about the power of one and the importance of visitors.

David:

Well the value of visitors, I believe, cannot be overstated. I think it’s probably one the greatest opportunity cost’s in every chapter. As you know, a opportunity cost is when we make one decision, and it negates the ability to make another decision. So in this case, when we make the decision not to invite someone, or not to be strategic about inviting someone, it’s actually costing the chapter quite a bit of money.

Ivan:

So, explain that. Talk that through so that people can wrap their heads around that idea.

David:

Sure! Let me start at a global level and work it down to a individual member. A few months ago, I checked out membership and it was 222,000 members. I know it’s up a little bit since then. With about 8,060 chapter. Which means our average chapter has seven members. Well, if globally 222,000 people commit and execute one visitor every month, and bring visitor to the chapter, the average visitor globally – let’s just say it’s a 1,000 dollars, thank you for close business to a visitor is a thousand dollars. Again, its higher than that-

Ivan:

By the way, the number he is quoting is right out of BNI Connect, everyone. These are harden, fast numbers as of the day he was looking at them. They are available now. These are not made up numbers, these are numbers right out of BNI Connect. Sorry, David.

David:

Well, in fact, you can see them on the front page of BNI.com. So, what that would mean is 2,664,000 visitors globally or over 2.6 billion dollars in thank you for close business. So, the average chapter would experience a bump in thank you for close business to the chapter of 330,480 dollars jut from visitors, per member, per month.

So, what does that mean for the average member listing to this podcast? It mean, they would receive and additional 12,000 dollars in thank you for close business.

Ivan:

That’s on average. Some are more, some are less. But still, that pretty impressive! So, what you point out is something that not everybody realize about visitors, they do in fact bring referrals. They come to a meeting and they go, “Oh my goodness, I need that!” And they pass a referral. And that number gets added to thank you for close business.

David:

And many times you’ll run into visitors that have never seen anything like BNI. And they’re just dumbstruck by how organized it is, “Wow, this is terrific!” They literally get enthused about trying to use people in the chapter meeting.

Ivan:

They do. I have even had people in chapters who have come up to me afterwards and they say, “You know, I almost didn’t come because of this and I’ve gone to a whole bunch of meetings. Oh goodness! This is so structured and organized and there was so much business generated today!” It’s always exciting to see how the visitors respond.

David:

And typically, they are thrilled by the idea of having a team to work with.

Ivan:

Absolutely! So, the message here is, if you follow the power of one, which is to have each member bring one visitor once a month- and there’s more to it. We just did a podcast a few weeks ago pursuing – I don’t know if you remember the podcast with Colin, and we did, “The Power of One.” And let’s see if I can find it while we are talking. But, we just did one. It should be the Power of One, Episode 517. So, if you haven’t heard about this, bring one visitor per-member per-month is one piece of it. But, what David is talking about here is it will increase your bottom line, thethank you for closed business chapter considerably.
Is there anything you want to add?

David:

Yes. One of the easiest ways to invite visitors- I’m sure we’ve all heard GRIP and other acronyms that help keep the process mind. So, “do you want to grow your chapter?” G is Grow, R is would Referrals help, I is are you Interested in, and the P is talking about the Place you meet. So you go through this acronym to hit all the steps and that certainly is effective.

One of my favorite methods to use is to ask people. We have a realtor in our chapter, Jewel Buckley who is with Coldwell Banker. So, if I’m talking to a trades person, it would’ve been set for meeting jewel, I’ll simply ask the question, “Do you know Jewel Buckley? She transacts about a 150 houses a year-” on and on.

“I’d love to meet her.”

“Well, we meet pretty regularly. I’m seeing her this Thursday if you want to join me.”
It’s just about meeting jewel and it’s just the connection, then all of the sudden they have 30 other people to meet.

Ivan:

Well, that’s great information, David. I really appreciate being willing to come and share this. Any closing thoughts before we Wrap up?

David:

Just keep it on the forefront of your mind. It’s muscle memory. If you are inviting on a regular basis, you’re going to develop that muscle of inviting people to your chapter meeting. And through that, your chapter will grow and thrive.

Ivan:

You know, that a good way of putting it, muscle memory. Bringing in visitors will inevitably increase the size of chapter you’re bringing in, right? When you have a chapter of over thirty members, it becomes a force multiplier, where you don’t have to go out and sell as much. Because, people are bringing you referrals. Before we on the podcast, David, you said it was a little scary to take that step off the cliff to rely on referrals to get your business. It’s incredible how it works isn’t it?

David:

Absolutely, it really is.

Ivan:

Alright, David, thanks so much for being our guest on my podcast. It’s a real pleasure having you here. Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:

Okay, great! Well thank you both. It was wonderful information. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard
