title.rendered: Episode 612: Innovation vs. Repetition

content.rendered:
After recording Episode 610, with its emphasis on timed repetition as one of the keys to being successful, Priscilla wondered where creativity comes in. Ivan had a strong response to this question, and decided to share it with the listeners.

Businesses do need innovation as well as repetition for success, but most small businesses try to skip the repetition. If you encourage BNI chapters to innovate, they try things random—often things that have already been tried, and failed.

Innovation has to be planned. Boredom is not a reason to change. BNI does innovate, but they test their changes one at a time, one chapter at a time, before making that change worldwide.

There’s plenty of room for innovation and creativity in your BNI meeting. Get creative with your weekly presentation and feature presentation. Change them frequently, but don’t change the process.

If you have an idea that you think is going to change the BNI meeting, talk to your BNI director. Work out a system where it gets tested. Change for the sake of change is not innovation. It’s just bad business.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic. I am on my way to San Antonio. It is my wife’s birthday tomorrow. And I promised to take her to San Antonio. I have never taken her there, so we will be leaving today for San Antonio’s Texas. 

Priscilla:
Great. Have a wonderful time. Would you like to introduce this topic?

Ivan:
I would like you to introduce it, but I will preface it a little. You said something at the end of one of our podcasts and I went on a rant. We have never done a podcast where it is completely unprepared. I am just going to do a rift based on a comment that you made and a discussion that we started to have because I have a lot of energy on this. I would love for you to share what you brought up and then I will jump in.

Priscilla:
Okay. I will be happy to do that. We did a podcast recently and the guest brought up a concept of repetition, doing things the same for long periods of time because he is very much into simplicity and he feels that repetition is a very important part of being successful. I was thinking where does innovation fit into that? If you are always doing the same thing over and over again, where does creativity come in and how do you make more products and become better at what you do? You had a strong response to that.

Ivan:
Yes I did, didn’t I? I had a very strong response to that. I will try to tone it down for this podcast. But I did have a strong response, and here is where I am coming from. I think that you need both repetition and innovation, but here is the rub: as soon as you say that you need innovation, what I find in BNI is that people just start changing stuff. “Oh, Ivan says innovate.” And they start changing things. They change things that we did before, stupid things that didn’t work and don’t make any sense at all. I said I wouldn’t get too excited, but I am getting excited. They start doing things that we have done, and it is just a bad idea. 

Innovation has to be done with a plan, and it has to be planned innovation. That seems counterintuitive, but you can’t just innovate willy-nilly, especially if you are talking about a global organization with almost 9,000 chapters. So if I have to lean between repetition and innovation, I think the biggest mistake that most small businesses make is they don’t do the repetition. Timed repetition is one of the reasons why BNI is as successful as it is. It is that getting together each week, doing that weekly presentation, having a speaker who is a BNI member, passing referrals every single week. These are all things that are repetitive. 

Now, you can do things that are innovative within it, as in doing better weekly presentations, doing meeting stimulants. The Education Coordinator, that was an innovation in BNI. The Visitor Orientation was an innovation in BNI. So there are many things that are added. Certainly, BNI Connect is an innovation in BNI. 

What I see done really badly, though, with businesspeople is they just want to change stuff because they get bored. Boredom is not a reason to change. Boredom is not a reason to just change up everything you are doing. You can make a change in an element of what you are doing, but I would urge business people — and I have said this many times — do six things 1000 times, not 1000 things six times. 

When BNI innovates, we innovate by trial and error and not by saying to 9000 chapters, “Hey, here is an idea. Try this out.” No, we try it in one chapter. If that works, we try it in five chapters. If that works, we try it in 20 chapters. And if that works, then we might try it in a region. And if that works, then we try it in a country. And if that works, we try it globally. So innovation takes place through trial and error, not willy-nilly, and my fear in telling people be innovative is that they might do things that have been done and failed. Does that make sense?

Priscilla:
It does make sense. It is a very disciplined way of doing innovation. I’m coming from the perspective of improvising because I play jazz, and it is something that we are taught to do, which is come up with new ideas within a structure. It does have a structure, but there is something to be said for creativity and spontaneity, so how do you respond to that?

Ivan:
Well, listen, I wouldn’t compare jazz to a BNI meeting. You know, they are substantially different and spontaneity in jazz is a whole different ball game than spontaneity in a business meeting. But I believe that you can incorporate some spontaneity in a BNI meeting through your weekly presentations. They should not be the same week in and week out. As a matter of fact, the worst ones are the ones that are the same every week. You want to change those. That is a way to incorporate innovation into the structure that exists. 

What I am talking about is just changing the structure, changing the process without some research and development. BNI has a pretty thorough research and development program where we test things out before we do it. Every now and then — and there will be handful of directors who will give me a call when they hear me say this — but every now and then I get some crazy BNI Director who says, “I have this great idea.” In 34 years, this has happened at least two dozen times, Priscilla. “I have this great idea. We are going to start doing mixers in our chapter. Everyone will invite everybody to the mixer and then we will invite them to the BNI meeting. We invite everybody to the mixer and then we invite them to the BNI meeting. This is going to be it. I think this is going to be a game-changer for us!”

I tell them, “Oh please, don’t do that.”

“Why not? This is different. It is new. It’s exciting. It’s fun. “

“Well, I have seen it two dozen times. And out of two dozen times, I have seen two dozen failures.”

“No, no. I have done it. I did it once. It was great.”

“Yeah, it will be great for two, three, four months, but after five or six months, you are going to stop doing it and you are going to call me and say I am right.”

“No, no. I am going to keep doing it.”

Priscilla, it’s been 34 years. I have never seen anyone do it past nine months, ever. People like change and there are certain behavior profiles, high “I”s, in particular, who are very people-oriented and they love change. My wife is a high I. She loves change. But the truth is, the most successful businesses are the ones that understand that you have to find a system and you have to be like a dog with a bone. If you want to establish creativity in that system, that’s okay, but you do it within the structure and the confines of the system, so you make a great weekly presentation, a great feature presentation and change that up. Don’t change up the process.

We talk about this in the Givers Gain book, by the way. When I wrote Givers Gain, one of the things that I wanted to do was talk about my mistakes in that book. The reason I talked about my mistakes was that I wanted to show people that this did not come out fully formed as an organization. I screwed up a fair amount throughout the process. I figured it out as I went, so why do we reinvent the wheel? If you have a great idea that you think is going to change the BNI meeting, talk to your BNI Director. Work out a system where it gets tested. Don’t just make changes, because change for change’s sake is not innovation. It is just bad business. Innovation comes through testing and experimentation, trial and error, with a process in place to measure the results. That is the key, measuring the results. That is the way that you do innovation, not just to change things up and make them different.

So I believe in innovation. It is one of our core values, but you have to do it with a plan. You can’t wing it. Does that makes sense?

Priscilla:
Yeah, it does, and you are one of the most disciplined people I have ever met and you consistently do something. You refer to yourself as a dog with a bone, and I believe that. I have witnessed it over the period of time that we have been working together. So I think that I hear what you are saying and there is a lot of validity to it. I like that you at least acknowledge that there is something to be gained from being creative within the structure. 

Ivan:
There is. If you look at BNI today, 34 years after I started it, there are a lot of differences. BNI Connect and the mobile app, and BNI University — these are all things that are innovative to our process, but they are an innovative way of doing the things that we have been doing for a long time. Take a look at BNI University. What is it about? It is about lifelong learning. Oh wait, that is one of our core values. What’s on BNI University? Things like Member Success Program. Oh wait, we have been doing that for decades. It is just a new way of doing something that we have been doing for a long time. So it is another way of being consistent, but it is riding the way of technology.  So that kind of innovation makes sense to me. But changing things just to change things is just stupid. That is the technical term. It’s just bad business. 

So don’t change things just to change things. Change things with a plan and a system and a process to measure results. That is the key. So let me step off my soapbox. Here I go, Priscilla. We did this whole podcast. It was completely off the cuff. Any last thought that you have before we wrap up?

Priscilla:
No I think you put it perfectly well. I think it is a great thing to think about and I liked how it got laid out.

Ivan:
Listen, I am really glad that you brought the topic up because I think it is an important topic. I am a believer in innovation, but if I had to lean anywhere, I would be leaning towards that timed repetition and that consistency, and you integrate that innovation into that consistency. When you do that, you have a one, two punch. It is the reason that BNI is – I had a radio show host who said, “BNI is the Mt. Rushmore of networking organizations.” It’s Mt. Rushmore for a reason. It’s because we do six things 1000 times. Thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Ivan, thank you so much. For more of Ivan’s content go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: If you have an idea that you think is going to change the BNI meeting, talk to your BNI director. Work out a system where it gets tested. Change for the sake of change is not innovation. It’s just bad business.

title.rendered: Episode 611: Your Business Has a Referral Confidence Curve

content.rendered:
David Kauffman of BNI Delaware Valley joins Dr. Misner on the podcast this week to talk about the Referral Confidence Curve.

It isn’t just your behavior in BNI meetings that affects your referrability. Your behavior in every arena affects your credibility with referral partners and prospective customers.

Little details can make a big difference in people’s impression of you. What does your outgoing voicemail message say? Do you use profanity or mention politics on social media? These things can drive prospects away.

If you have employees, their behavior can affect your referrability. And above all, the way you respond when something goes wrong determines whether you’ll get any repeat business.

Be willing to listen to feedback, and to ask for it, especially if you are not getting as many referrals as you used to.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Priscilla:

Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:

I am doing fantastic! It’s good to be back home. I’ve been traveling so much, so I’m happy to be back home for a little while. I’ve got a guest here today, his name is David Kauffman. David is the Managing Director for five BNI regions collectively known as the BNI Delaware Valley Region. He is also Vice Chairman of the BNI Foundation and the Founder of ‘Your Business Basics’ that he’s developed and runs an adoptive aquatics program for the YMCA in his area, which I think is really cool.

He’s been in BNI for 18 years, he’s married and has four kids and three grandchildren. I’m jealous, all I have is grand-dogs. And I found that bringing that up to your children is not a great idea, they don’t like that. David is also the author of a book called “Your Message” which deals with today’s topic, and I was happy to do an endorsement for that book.

David, welcome to BNI Podcast!

David:

Thank you, I appreciate it very much!

Ivan:

So, the topic today is about the referral confidence curve. BNI members are probably aware of the referral confidence curve, but you say it’s broader than just the confidence that someone has in them personally, it’s also about their business. Talk about that.

David:

Over the years, dealing with a lot of business owners and BNI members, they go to the Member Success Program and they learn about the referral confidence curve and they think it stops there. They think it’s just about how they act in the meeting and how they act when they are doing a 1-2-1 it’s really a lot bigger than that. It really transforms from BNI into their business. What they don’t often think about is that people are constantly judging them and how they act in their businesses. A direct reflection on whether or not someone will use their products/services or will recommend them or will use them again and again, which is their goal: repeat business. It’s always a challenge because business owners often tend to look at themselves through their own eyes. They don’t think about the voicemails that they leave or how they use social media that will reflect on whether or not I can refer them. 

A lot of times they get in trouble, especially with social media. A lot of times they post things and all of a sudden it comes back to haunt them. 

Ivan:

You’ve got so much in with just those few words you said there. Let’s start with the social media. I counseled somebody who was a BNI member and social media was a real problem for him. He would drop the F-bomb with great regularity on his social media. 

I was like, “Dude, what are you thinking?” 

“What? It’s just my Facebook page.” 

“Yeah but if anyone goes to your Facebook page and they are thinking about doing business with you and then they see all of this…” Then he would post a lot of political stuff and that is a neutral negative. People are either not going to care or they are going to be really ticked off that you said all of that stuff. I’ve got to tell you, to his credit, he deleted all of that stuff off of his social media and it immediately started having an impact on how people viewed him. That is so important. 

David:

And in today’s world, it also transforms not just to things like that but the other day I just saw a very interesting post on politics. Someone was looking for a recommendation and went to a local community post and started searching through all the people who were recommended and came across someone who had a lot of references–but then he went and looked at his political posts and decided not to use them. It doesn’t matter what side you are on, it just should stay out of business social media all together. 

Ivan:

Now we are getting on a side subject but I can’t help myself here, I have a bachelor’s degree in Political Science. I am very involved in politics and I used to be very actively involved and am well read on politics. The thing is, no BNI members know my politics because I don’t share it since it is a neutral negative. People will either be like “Oh, okay that’s good,” or they will be mad at me–and by the way, most people probably have no idea what my politics are. They may think they do, but trust me, they don’t. I just think it’s a mistake. So your credibility is more than just a relationship you have with the meeting, it’s also in your activity on your leadership team. If people see you and how you behave on the leadership team, it gives them an impression and increases their confidence in your ability to provide a quality product/service based on what they see you do in other contexts. Would you agree with that, David?

David:

Yes, I agree with that 100%. It’s not just the leadership team, it’s also the members. So how you come dressed, how you respond to a referral, how quickly you respond to someone’s inquiry about your business, your emails, whether you are using abbreviations or are spelling things out, and voicemails. Voicemails amaze me! When I call a business owner and it says your voicemail is filled up so you can’t leave a message, it astounds me. Or when someone calls up and says, “I’ll be back from vacation on March 3rd,” and now it’s April and they still haven’t reached out. I think that people don’t think this affects their referability. I don’t think they realize that all these little pieces make up the bigger picture and they think that if I don’t worry about something that it’s “OK.” But they don’t know how many clients they lost because of what they did. I think that’s a bigger piece.

Ivan:

It really is. The little details make a big difference in people’s impression of you, not only as an individual, but also in the quality of the work you do. Because if you can’t bother to change your auto-reply, are you following up on their problem or issue they are having with your product/service? For me, that is a factor when I see something like that.

David:

I also think that as business owners, we don’t take into account how our staff, employees, or sub-contractors also affect our referability. If you go to someone’s office and they are finding that the staff is rude, short-tempered, and doesn’t want to take care or listen to your problems–all those things reflect back on the business owner and whether or not you are referable. I am amazed time and time again when I am dealing with different companies and I’ll call back and say, “I’d like to leave a message for somebody.” And they’ll say, “Why don’t you call back tomorrow?” I’m like, “Well I’m calling today. Can I leave a message?” It will totally reflect back on whether or not I will refer you or business again.

Ivan: 

So, when that happens I think it’s really important to have the conversation with the business owner, and hopefully, the business owner pays attention because sometimes the business owner will just get defensive. But when I tell a business owner about a problem that I had with an employee or a problem that I had when I called them previously and they pay attention to me, then it’s easier for me to forgive them about the issue if they hear me and react. But it’s really important to have that conversation and most people don’t have that conversation.

David:

No, they don’t have the conversation because it’s easier to find someone else. It’s just the easier route. It also comes back to is the business owner willing to listen to feedback, like you say. I will talk to people and I’ll say, ”Can I give you feedback?” Just like in BNI when I give you feedback on referrals, we should be giving feedback on the business. I also recommend to business owners that they seek out that feedback. That they call their clients asking, “How did my staff do? Could I have done anything better?” And then listen to what they are saying because when you get that information and you use it and better the customer’s experience, there’s a much greater chance that they are going to refer you. I do feel that a true judge of a business is what happens when things go wrong. 

Ivan:

Oh, yes, I couldn’t agree with you more. I had a BNI member about two or three years ago who installed a granite kitchen top In a condo that I had that I lived at downtown in Austin. It ended up with a scratch on it–I mean it was a deep scratch. So, I took a picture and I sent it to him and the owner did not do the install, it was somebody else, so I was concerned what the response would be. But that owner, who was a BNI member, jumped on it. They didn’t try to repair it, they just took out the entire granite top and put in a completely new one. So, to me, that service recovery was almost better than doing it right the first time, because everybody screws up. I screw up. But I judge people by how they deal with mistakes as much or even more than how they do the basic service. Does that make sense to you, David?

David:

I agree 100%. People are going to tell your story–good or bad they are going to tell your story. If you do the right thing, even if it wasn’t this gentleman’s fault that the counter got scratched, he did the right thing and you will sing his praises forever.

Ivan:

It was actually a woman–but I would refer her in a moment because she absolutely did the right thing and the final job was awesome! It was really good.

Anything else on referral confidence curve and what BNI members should know in getting members to understand how to refer them effectively.

David:

I think the big piece of this is don’t shy away from feedback. Feedback is your friend no matter if it’s good or bad. And I think every business owner should look at their own business. Call their voicemail and listen to it. If they have office staff, call from a phone number they don’t know and see what the response is. Ask people who have used your company for feedback. And then take all this information and make those changes that will move you up that referral confidence curve that will make people want to refer you more. 

Ivan:

Yes, I couldn’t agree more on the feedback. It’s sometimes painful to hear but you’ve got to have it. BNI has been using a system for the last few years that’s called NPS (Net Promoters Score) and if you’re a BNI member at some point you will eventually get an NPS survey to take and I would love for you to take that and send feedback–the good, bad and ugly. Because that’s what helps any business become more successful.

So, David, if people want to get a copy of your book “Your Message” which I highly recommend, they can go to yourbusinessbasics.com. Is that right?

David:

That’s correct! There’s a link right on there, it’ll take you to the Amazon site and you can order it directly off the website, yes. 

Ivan:

Well, David, thank you for all the work you do for the BNI Foundation. You do amazing work for the organization and the BNI Foundation is I think very much the heart of what we do. It’s contributing to children and education and I don’t think anyone except for a handful of people will ever know just how much you have done for the BNI Foundation. I want to go on record just thanking you for all years of service that you have given to the foundation

David:

Thank you very much. I truly appreciate it!

Ivan:

Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:

Okay, well I think that was great! Thank you so much to both of you. For more about this content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: David Kauffman of BNI Delaware Valley joins Dr. Misner on the podcast this week to talk about the Referral Confidence Curve. It isn’t just your behavior in BNI meetings that affects your referability. Your behavior in every arena affects your credibility with referral partners and prospective customers.

title.rendered: Episode 610: Amplifying Results Using a Lens

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Rick Sapio, one of the co-authors of Who’s in Your Room?, joins Dr. Misner on the podcast this week to talk about making business decisions.

Rick uses a decision-making lens to help him get the desired results. Yes/no decisions come faster when you use a lens. To use the lens effectively, you have to be conscious about your decisions.

Rick’s operating values, the lens he uses to make his decisions, are simplicity, probability, and leverage.

Simplicity means not making things more complicated than they have to be. Buy a house 5 miles from your office instead of building one 30 miles from your office.

Probability means taking the actions that increase the probability of achieving your goals. If your stated objective is to sell your business in 5 years, you don’t hire your out-of-work brother-in-law who doesn’t have experience. You hire the best process person for your office manager that you could possibly find.

Leverage is getting more done with the same investment of time and/or money by using calendaring and technology. Successful BNI chapters do things the same way, week after week, month after month, year after year.

The final concept that drives Rick is eternalism. Consider every decision as if you were going to live with it for 100 years.

Learn more about how to operationalize simplicity, probability and leverage at Rick’s Business Finishing School.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 610

Priscilla:

Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:

Hi Priscilla, I am on my way back from France. I just spent a few days with my bride of 30 years on the beaches of Normandy in France. it’s good to be coming back.

Priscilla:

Congratulations on 30 years.

Ivan:

Thank you. It both seems long in some ways and short in others. It’s amazing.

I am really excited because I have a good friend on this week’s podcast. His name is Rick Sapio. You might recognize that name because Rick is a co-author of the book Who’s in Your Room, which I have talked about a fair amount on my podcast and a lot on my blog. Rick is a lifelong entrepreneur. He started his business of a bicycle repair show when he was 13 years old. Since then,  he has started almost two dozen companies. He is the seventh of nine children and  he was raised by a mother that had some serious health issues. We talked a little bit about her in Who’s in Your Room? He is the cofounder and six time chairman of the Gathering of Titans Program which is a annual CEO events that aid held up at MIT in Boston. Rick is a good friend and a co-author of Who’s in Your Room? I am really excited to have him on the podcast today. Rick, welcome.

Rick:

Thank you Ivan. I am excited to be here.

Ivan:

It’s great to have you here and we are going to talk about business decision making using a lens that will add leverage your business. I would love for you to talk about it and then as you are talking about it I will just chime in and give some color commentary.

Rick:

Perfect. So when you think about Who’s in Your Room, it’s using values to make decisions on who you want to enter into the room of your life. This is taking a little bit further that ‘I have used in my 55 years on the planet a decision making lens that helps me get the desired result. So one of the benefits of using the lens is the yes/no decisions come faster. We are all looking for an edge. That is why we titled it amplifying results. 

So the three words we use as operating values in our businesses and actually in my family life too is simplicity, probability and leverage. So you want me to go deep on each one?

Ivan: 

Yeah. Go deep on each one of those if you can.

Rick:

Alright. So one f the things we say often in our family and in my company is we like to add consciousness to business and personal decisions. I think there is a book written by Charles Duhague that talks about how 98% of what you and I do on a daily basis is unconscious. We just do it because we do it. There is a block of decisions that if we made them consciously, we would make them differently. So we would like for simplicity, probability and leverage to be part of your conscious decision making criteria.

As an example, my wife wanted to build a house about 30 miles from where I work. So I said I watched three people get divorced building a house and I don’t believe in commutes, so it doesn’t fit the value of simplicity. She was like, you’re right. So we bought a house that was already built and it was five miles from the office. We were using simplicity and consciousness in making  decisions. 

I think that if people just used one operating value and they looked at everything they do in their business and personal life and they just said how can this be as simple as possible?  It was Leonardo da Vinci who said, “Simplicity is the ultimate sophistication,” so we like to operationalize simplicity.

Ivan:

Let me just jump in on that. I think that is such an important value to have, simplicity. On one of the times that I met ‘sir ‘Richard Branson. He said any idiot can make things complicated. It’s a really smart business person that can keep things simple. I couldn’t agree more and I see that happen in BNI all the time, where people just get crazy complicated with the BNI system when it is all about relationships. Just focus on building relationships with people and doing a good message about who you are and what you do. Go ahead, Rick.

Rick:

And I think we don’t operationalize it. We have limited time here, but if you have a business or personal life. We all have one of those two or both. Look for simplicity as an operating value and a value across the board.

The next one is probability. I love probability and I am going to use a personal example. My wife and I have four young kids. Every decision we make with the kids is to increase the probability that they hare self sufficient, independent, entrepreneurial 18 year olds. Since that’s our goal, we don’t do a lot of things that most mainstream parents do.for example, we don’t drive our kids to sports. We think that is going to make them the opposite of self sufficient, independent and entrepreneurial. We do however drive them to places that will teach them to earn more, how to be entrepreneurial or how to think for themselves. 

Ivan:

That’s an interesting one. I think people will find that one controversial but how do you apply this in a business setting?

Rick:

You think about any decision that you are making. Is it increasing the probability that you hit your stated objective – if your stated objective is to sell your business in five years, then when your office manager quits, you don’t hire out of work brother in law who doesn’t have experience in your business. You actually go out and find the best process person who could operate within that role.

So probability to us is making decisions that increase the probability that you hit your stated objectives. And again, going back to the unconsciousness we find is most people do the exact opposite. So they have habits and systems that are making things harder, and lowering that probability.

Ivan:

Excellent, if that makes sense. And then you have a third,

Rick:

Third one is leverage. And the trick Ivan is to operationalize these, and we think about simplicity, probability and leverage and all of our decisions. And I like leverage from a personal perspective. And from a business perspective. But leverage is about getting more done with the same amount of investment of time and or money. So that’s using technology. It’s using calendaring. And it’s using systems.

And to make the point very clear, I’m going to use a personal example here as well. When I met my wife, it’s my one and only wife, I got married late in life that I met her 15 years ago. And I said, it was a Monday night, it was her first day. And I said you want to go out next Monday. And she said, Yep. And so we went out the next one and the next one, and for 15 years at 730. Every Monday, we go out to dinner, and we tend to go to the same two places. So that’s leverage the babysitter knows the neighbors know that restaurant knows my wife knows it’s not my Hey, when you want to go.

And a lot of couples just cannot do that, because they don’t understand leverage. So that’s the personal example

On the business side, BNI chapters that work incredibly well, employing this, they do everything the same way, month after month, week, after week, year after year, etc. But a lot of people have not institutionalized leverage.

Ivan:

So I have said for years to the BNI members, if you want to be successful, do six things 1000 times not 1000 things six times. And too many business people are constantly chasing bright, shiny objects, and they end up doing 1000 things six times and then they cannot figure out why they are not successful. And to me, it is so incredibly obvious. They’re not using leverage, and they’re not being consistent. Would you agree with that?

Rick:

Absolutely. And I would also add, people say that I’m boring, because everything he says simplicity, probability and leverage. And then I would say that Jerry Seinfeld is boring, too. Because I was so excited to see him at Caroline’s at an impromptu thing. We got up and burped and tripped and heckled somebody in the audience. And then I saw him two years later in Vegas, and he did the exact same presentation, act, etc. So Jerry Seinfeld must be boring to people that are great at things like you are very ritualistic. And they use simplicity properly.

Ivan:

Yeah, I agree completely. And I think the problem is that, you know, you get entrepreneurs who are maybe a little bit artistic in some way, or they’re very high personalities. And they’re there. They just like things that are new and different. And they can’t do things over and over and over again. And to them I say, “That’s no problem. Just don’t expect to be incredibly successful. Because if you don’t do things over and over and over again, you’re just not going to achieve the success that you want in life.”

Rick:

We call those people people that are addicted to Yes. And addicted to Yes is a sure way to failure.

Ivan:

We’ve only got a minute or so left. And there’s one other thing that you talked about, which I absolutely love, and that’s focusing on your 100 years out. That’s crazy. Talk about that wreck.

Rick:

Yeah, it’s one of my values is eternalism. And if you think about what’s your legacy going to be, and you focus on 100 years, and a lot of people that are living today will be here in 100 years, you have to think about a based on medical technology. But if I knew that you and I were going to be friends for 100 years Ivan how careful would I be with what I say to you? How careful would I be with the business decisions that I make with you? How careful what I be in caring for the relationship? And I think companies that have 100 year vision, just do things differently? It’s the exact opposite of how the world operates today, for the most part,

Ivan:

Yeah. Which is, you know, what sale can I make this week? As opposed to what relationship can I build for the long term? Well, this is really good advice. And you have a program. I’ve been to one of your events, business finishing school, you have got a website business finishing school calm. You want to talk briefly about that. We’ve got about 20 seconds left.

Rick:

Yeah, Business Finishing School is all about operationalizing simplicity, probability and leverage, and the people that do the program, see 50 percent growth and their top line revenue year one. So business, I’m sorry, BNI members have been through the program from all over. And we just think it’s a perfect symbiotic relationship with Vienna.

Ivan:

Well, I agree completely. And as I said, I’ve been to a Business Finishing School event and I highly recommend it to BNI members. If you have an opportunity, go to businessfinishingschool.com, we’ll have a URL link here. Rick, thank you so much. Thanks for your the work you’ve done on Who’s in Your Room. And thank you for what you do for business people all over the world.

Rick:

Thank you too, as well Ivan, I appreciate your time. And I appreciate the big audience and I appreciate the stamp that you have made on the world through being is incredible.

Ivan:

Priscilla, over to you

Priscilla.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look froward to having you join again next week for another exciting episode of the BNI Podcast

title.rendered: Episode 609: Do Nonprofits Work in BNI? (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 511.

Synopsis

Today’s Ask Ivan question comes from Christina, who works for a nonprofit organization in the United States.

I was advised to reach out to you to get guidance on how to be more effective as a public charity in BNI. At first, I didn’t know where I fit because I don’t have a tangible product or service that would directly benefit others in the chapter. Could you please provide some guidance on how I can be more effective in using BNI to support our mission?

Nonprofits can be very effective and very successful in BNI. Here are ten benefits of BNI membership for nonprofits.

  1. Referrals to other companies doing charitable work to create strategic alliances.
  2. Referrals to the charity’s thrift shop or other product or service (if there is one).
  3. Invitations to special events.
  4. Volunteers for special projects.
  5. Referrals for possible board members.
  6. Referrals for speaking engagements.
  7. Don’t just look for donations.
  8. Referrals for good employees.
  9. Fine-tune your message.
  10. Introductions to local leaders.

Read Dr. Misner’s article about difficult-to-refer businesses on Entrepreneur.com.

Add your own suggestions for how nonprofits can use BNI in the comments below.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 609

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you today?

Ivan:
I am in the New England area on business. I have a very interesting topic today. It is part of the Ask Ivan series. It is a question that was asked to me recently by Christina. Christina is with a nonprofit organization in the United States. Here is her question for me, “I have been with BNI for almost two years now and I was advised to reach out to you by multiple members to get guidance on how to be more effective as a public charity in BNI. At first, I didn’t know where I fit because I didn’t have a tangible product or service that would directly benefit the others in the chapter. Could you please provide some guidance on how I can be more effective in using BNI as part of our mission?” Christina does work with community outreach, community services, family services.

You know, the title of this is, “Do Nonprofits Work in BNI?” so the short answer to the title is absolutely, positively. Nonprofits can be very effective and very successful in BNI, but that doesn’t answer Christina’s question. Let me give you some suggestions, Christina, and for everyone out there who is in a nonprofit organization, take notes. Listen to this and if you have ideas that I don’t come up with, please post them here on my podcast of some things that have worked for you.

So I gave it some thought. I knew that nonprofits could work extremely well in BNI. I game some thought to it, but I asked our resident expert and my personal resident expert on charitable work and foundations is my wife. But what is really interesting and what a lot of people don’t know is Elisabeth joined BNI in 1986, so she has been in BNI for a long, long time. She had many roles within the company as the Director of Marketing- she was the regional director of the largest region for BNI at one time, Director of Marketing, Special Projects. Then in 1998, she co-founded the BNI Foundation with me and for many years, up until recently, ran the BNI Foundation.

So she is an expert at this and I got some suggestions from her. Between the two of us, we came up with 10 different ways that nonprofits can benefit from BNI. Ten. Here is the first:

Referrals to other companies doing charitable work to work together on projects and services, to basically create strategic alliances with other organizations.

Number two, some nonprofits do have products or services. Christina, I recognize you say you don’t, but some do have products or services and those actually work really well in BNI. For example, thrift shops. I was on a board for an organization that had a thrift shop. That was a really good fit. Companies that work with special needs adults, assembling packages or materials. That is a fantastic one for BNI. So nonprofits that do that, that’s an easy one. But for those who don’t have products or services, here are some more:

Number three, invitations to special events. There is a fundraiser that we may be having or there may be an open house. One of the organizations that I belong to did a monthly open house where people would just say, “Hey, look, have you ever been curious as to this big operation that you see going on on the side of the hill?” or “Do you know people who have been curious about our children’s center? On the last Thursday of the month, come to lunch and we will give you a tour.”

Invite people to those kinds of things. You don’t always have to be asking people for money. Networking is more about farming than it is about hunting. This applies to nonprofits as well.

Get referrals for volunteers for special projects. That’s number four. And volunteers both inside and outside the BNI group. You could talk about a special project that is going on and the nonprofit is looking for people to volunteer.

I mean, here is a great one that I have seen the BNI Foundation itself do – speed mentoring, where they had BNI members as mentors working with children’s programs that were young entrepreneurs and the BNI members just volunteered. They just volunteered for an hour of their time to pour into these kids with their knowledge on running a business.

Number five, referrals for board members, again, both inside and outside the BNI group. You know, if you are looking for a board member for a nonprofit, you should show up to a [BNI] meeting and say, “This week I want to talk about board members. Here are some of the people who are on our Board of Directors now. And we are always looking for someone like this person, this person, and this person. If you know someone like that, we would love to have an introduction because these are the people who help run our organization.

Number six- this is a good one. Almost any nonprofit can do this one. Speaking engagement. A lot of nonprofits would love to get out and talk to Rotary, Lions, Kiwanis, church groups, whatever, community organizations, to just talk about what they are doing and how they are making a difference in the community.

That is a big one. If you don’t do anything else. If nothing else in this podcast resonates with your nonprofit, that is the one that you want to do with.

I also did an article for Entrepreneur many years ago about how to use a network to get speaking engagements. I will give that to Priscilla here so that we can include that link in this and go back to look at the old article.

So you can go to a BNI meeting and say, “Hey, look, I am really easy to give referrals to. I would love to talk about my nonprofit organization. So here are some letters that you can use to introduce me to people.” That is what I talk about in this article that I wrote. I will make sure that the link is included.

Number seven–this is actually a “don’t do” rather than a “do”. Don’t just look for donations. Don’t just stand up every week and say, “We need money.” That would be like somebody standing up every week and saying, “I need a sale. Buy my product now. Don’t do that every week. As a matter of fact, rarely do that. And if you are going to do that, do it for big events, “Come to our fundraiser. Come to this.” The other stuff is even more important.

Here is another one: Number eight, referrals for good employees. Nonprofits are always looking for recommendations for employees. I just recently recommended someone for an important executive assistant position for a board that I was on for many years, and they were hired. They were really happy.

So if you are a nonprofit and you are looking for good employees who believe in the mission of the organization, that is another great way that BNI can support you.

Here is number nine: Fine-tune your message on BNI members. Every BNI member I have ever met for any length of time has said to me, “Oh yeah. BNI has enabled me to fine-tune my message. I can communicate what I do much more effectively.” That is an important benefit to you as a BNI member.

Number ten: introduction to local leaders. Come to a meeting and say, “I have really been trying to meet [this person] or [that person]. Do any of you know someone who might know that individual that would be willing to make an introduction? I won’t ask them for money. I simply want to meet with them and tell them about our products, tell them about our services and what we do for the community so that they can know about us.” That’s really powerful for a lot of nonprofits.

So there are 10 things to think about if you are a nonprofit. What I would love is to have like 15 or 20, so if you are listening to this podcast, put some more. Give us some suggestions, especially if you are a nonprofit organization. I hope that if you are a nonprofit that you got some ideas here.

I want to open it up for you, Priscilla, in a second to see if you have anything to add, but before I do, I do want to clarify one thing. I do come into chapters every now and then where somebody said, “I had the nonprofit chair. Okay, we don’t have a nonprofit chair. That’s like saying, “I have the for-profit chair.” There are many different businesses in BNI. There can be, and I have seen some groups that have, more than one nonprofit organization if they are focusing on different things.

If you take this list of ten things that I am talking about, there is a lot that you can do. You know, just don’t get two nonprofits doing the same thing. Maybe you get a nonprofit that has special needs adults doing assembly of packages and then something where it is a children’s services program which is completely different. So it is possible to have more than one.

But we don’t have only one nonprofit in a chapter of BNI. It could be more. I have never seen more than two, but don’t limit yourself.

What do you think about that list, Priscilla? A couple of things that maybe you didn’t think about?

Priscilla:
Oh definitely. There are some great things on that list. I sit on the Board of Directors of a nonprofit called LivingZazz.org. We have a gala once a year and we also put on a very large concert for Dr. Martin Luther King Jr in honor of his life. People in my group, my chapter, buy tickets for those events and I consider that a referral.

Ivan:
And you are not even taking a nonprofit seat, but that is very generous of you. That’s very nice.

Priscilla:
I mean, it’s very wonderful and helpful that they actually participate or volunteer.

Ivan:
I like that. And you know what? I am going way, way, way back but I did the same thing when I was a member of a local chapter. I was always involved in charitable work. I did the same things, so that is a great suggestion.

Priscilla:
Great okay.

Ivan:
All right, so I will include that link for those of you who want to use it to get ideas on how to use BNI to get speaking engagements. That is a great article that I did for Entrepreneur many years ago. I think you will find it helpful. That is it for today, Priscilla. Thanks.

Priscilla:
That’s great, Dr. Misner. Thank you so much. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube. Thank you for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Dr. Misner shares ten benefits of BNI membership for nonprofits, even if they don’t have products or services.

title.rendered: Episode 608: Little Company, Big Vision

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Dr. Misner realized recently that there’s a story he’s been telling for decades but had never done a podcast on.

In 1986, a year after he started BNI, Dr. Misner was determined to follow Michael Gerber’s advice in The E-Myth and work on his business and not just in it.

He asked himself where he wanted to be map out his vision for where he wanted BNI to be for the next 5-10. How many states would they be in? What additional support could they provide members?

All these questions came down to the need for a more comprehensive organizational structure. He sat down to draft an organization chart for the business he envisioned.

Based on everything he and his part-time employees were doing, there were 15 boxes in the chart. His employee’s names were in four of them. His name was in the rest.

It took seven years, but he eventually replaced himself in every role but founder and CEO. BNI now employs more than 12,000 people.

Wherever you are in your business, this is a great exercise. Write out where you want to be and create your org chart.

If you’ve ever done anything like this, let us know about it in the comments.

Read more about this topic in Dr. Misner’s article on Entrepreneur.com.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 608

Priscilla Rice
Hello, everybody and welcome back to the official BNI podcast. I’m Priscilla rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the founder and the chief visionary officer of the and I Dr. Ivan Meisner. Hello Ivan, how are you? And where are you?

Ivan Misner
I am happy to say I am in Italy this week for the Italian conference be and I and I love my visits to Italy, the middle here are amazing. And of course the food is incredible. And and they have pretty good wine to which as you know, I’m a fan of.

Priscilla Rice
Yeah. Oh, that sounds like so much fun. So tell us a little bit about this topic that you’ve had.

Ivan Misner
So I realized just recently that there’s a story I’ve been telling for many decades, that I’ve never done a podcast on. And so I decided, I wrote an article on it. And I decided to do this podcast on it. And little company, big vision. It was 1986. I had just started being i a year earlier. And the concept of working on my business, not just in my business really resonated with me, I read a book by Michael Gerber, who I’ve talked about a couple of times on the podcast, and he wrote a book called The E myth. And in that book, he he talks about working in your business, working on your business versus in your business.

I wanted to work as much as possible on my business and I and I wanted to do some vision making for my business. And think ahead for where I want BNI to be for the next five to 10 years. How many members did I foresee how many states? Would I be operating in it? At that point? I wasn’t even thinking countries, but how many states would I be operating in? What additional support could we provide members, all of these objectives came down to the need for a more comprehensive organizational structure. So I needed a bigger operation to eventually handle that bigger vision of the company.

So I sat down, and I started my multi-year plan by drafting an organization chart. And this organization chart wasn’t for what currently exists. But it was for what I envisioned. And the organization chart was for what I thought the company could look like five to 10 years into the future. So I began by considering all the functions that I was currently doing. And the high employees we’re currently doing.

I had two part time employees in 1986. Two part time employees for me and both of them work 20 hours a week. So it amounted to one employee basically. And I took a look at all of the functions that all three of us were doing. So there was I put their name in, like four boxes, I did 15 different boxes, 15 different positions that I thought could exist five to 10 years from now. Their name was in four of those. My name was in the other other 11. So there was Ivan Misner founder and CEO Ivan Misner and I put I actually put our names in the boxes. And you’ll see a copy here of the organization chart that I did. Literally 1986 it’s a it’s a replica of what I did in 1986.

So, Ivan Misner, founder and CEO; Ivan Misner, marketing director; Ivan Misner, complaint department; Ivan Misner, janitor, was listed in all of these different boxes. Mm hmm. So what’s really amazing is that that gave me a vision as to where I wanted to take the company. You see, when you’re when your company is small, everyone pitches in and does multiple roles.

So I worked at deconstructing everyone’s current roles and drafting an organization chart based on all the functions that we were currently sharing. Then, I put the three of us like I said, into every box that I drew relating to the functions that that the people were currently covering, even if they were covering multiple functions, because it was a small office. This exercise, you see, gave me unwavering clarity on what I wanted to do to grow the company. And that was that I had to get out of all the boxes I was in, except for CEO.

Now, this is easier said than done. It was it was my five to 10 year plan, and I knew it would take at least five years to get there. That meant that I needed to start right now in order to achieve that within a five year period. So I began thinking about the things that I hated doing. And the accounting was at the top of that list. I knew that if I wanted to grow the company, I needed this and and other things off of my plate.

And you’ve heard me say this before, Priscilla, in my podcast, I believe in entrepreneurs either working in their flame or working in their wax. When they’re working in their flame, they’re on fire. They’re excited about what they do. You can hear it in their voice, you can see it in the way they act. When an entrepreneur is working in their wax. They’re being drained and they’re they’re tired. They don’t like what they’re doing. You can hear that in their voice, and you can see it in the way they act. So accounting was my wax. And I knew it was one of the first things that I needed to replace.

Now the beauty of business is that something that may be my wax happens to be someone else’s flame. And I remember the first bookkeeper I hired, she loved accounting. She loved it. True story. One day, she came to me and she said “The books were off by five cents. But I found it.” Now, it took her two hours. It took her two hours, she said, to find it. But she did. And she was so proud.

Now some people may be listening to this and thinking, “Wait a minute. You paid her two hours to find five cents.” Yeah, Yes, I did. And I congratulated her on her hard work. Here’s why. For me, it could have been off $50. And I would have said, Oh, well close enough. You know, cuz, yeah, I hate this. Could you imagine if it were off $500? She wouldn’t go home and sleep until she tracked that money down. Bookkeeping was her flame. And I’m glad it was. So I was happy to have someone who that was their flame. And they would commit to, to the work to make sure it was done, right.

So each person that I hired, I would put a line through the name in the box that was there. And right in the name of the person who replaced me, or one of my first employees. It took seven years. But I have virtually replaced every single person in that original organization chart. Hmm, great. Today, BNI has almost 100 employees at several corporate offices around the world. And it has over I don’t think members realize BNI has over 12,000 as of today’s podcast, has over 12,000 franchisees, executive directors, national directors, cruise director, consultants, and employees working for the company in more than 70 countries over 12,000 people. And it all began with that handwritten vision making organization chart.

Priscilla Rice:
That’s amazing Ivan. Really.

Ivan Misner
Every day, I look back and think, wow, you know, it’s amazing what this has become. But amazing things oak trees grow from a small acorn. The key, I believe, to creating a successful company, one of the keys, there are many one of the keys to creating a successful company is creating a vision, and working towards that vision, and learning how to work on the business versus in the business.

And that was my vision. Back in 1986 when I did this original organization chart. And I think this is such a great exercise. If you’re listening to this podcast, wherever you are in business, whether you’re a year old or 10 years old. I think it’s a great exercise for you to sit down and and write out where do you want to be five years from now, it doesn’t matter how long you’ve been in business, where do you want to be five years from now?

And here’s the other thing I’d recommend. And then if you want to talk about this idea, I’d be happy to but here’s one thing I’d recommend. Keep the first one that you did. Hmm, I didn’t. What what you’re seeing here on this podcast, the graphic is a duplicate. I redid it for this podcast. But I didn’t keep the hand written. It was a hand-drawn. org chart. Yeah, if I had that today, thirty-some years later, I would have an inner frame and on my wall. But I didn’t you know, it was like, Yeah, I did it. Last one screen, my name off, put in the new person. And I throw it out. I wish I’d kept it. I’d recommend that if you’re going to do this exercise. Keep it. So that’s that’s my podcast for today. Priscilla, any thoughts?

Priscilla Rice
I just it’s really amazing what you’ve done Ivan it’s just incredible that you’ve grown this little company into this very big company. And I’m always reminding the members, it whenever I do the educational moment, I’m always reminding them that they are just a little cog in a very big machine. It’s you know, now it’s maybe in 73 countries.

Ivan Misner
Yeah, it’s over seventy countries. And you know, I like to say, ’cause “a little cog in a big machine sounds. Sounds too much like a Charlie Chaplin movie”, I would say it’s the tip of the iceberg.

Priscilla Rice
Yeah, that’s a good phrase.

Ivan Misner
Because that iceberg, you know, that’s big, it’s large. And oftentimes, members only see the tip, that there’s a massive infrastructure underneath that. I mean, 12,000 people as of this date, that’s not in my wildest dreams that I ever think that we’d have 12,000 people. That’s a pretty big org chart, by the way,I don’t think you can fit that on one page.

Priscilla Rice
Definitely not.

Ivan Misner
So if you if you’re listening to this podcast, I’d love to hear what you think about this idea. I’d love to hear if you’re going to try it. If you’ve ever done anything like it. Please share that with us here on the podcast.

Priscilla Rice:

Thanks so much. It was a great podcast. Very interesting.

Okay. Thank you so much for that great information. For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look froward to having you join again next week for another exciting episode of the BNI Podcast.
