excerpt.rendered: If you want to grow your business, create an org chart for all the roles the company needs–and start replacing yourself in those roles.

title.rendered: Episode 607: Learning How to Say No

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This week Dr. Misner shares more material from his book Who’s in Your Room?

Whenever possible, you want to say yes to opportunities, but sometimes you do have to say no. Here are 7 points to help you do it without sounding like a jerk.

  1. Know the difference between an opportunity and a distraction.
  2. “If I say yes, I’m afraid I’d let you down.”
  3. Refer them to someone more qualified.
  4. “I don’t do that.”
  5. Propose something else.
  6. Don’t Seinfeld it. Be honest. Be direct.
  7. When you say it, mean it. Repeat yourself if you have to.

And always remember, “No” is a complete sentence.

Do you have a favorite technique for saying no without burning bridges? Share it in the comments.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 607

Priscilla:

Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:

Hi Priscilla. It is great to talk to you. This week I am in Bordeaux France. I will be meeting some chapters here and taking a few days off with a good friend of mine Stewart Emery, who co-wrote the book Who’s in Your Room with me. 

Priscilla:

Oh That sounds like fun.

Ivan:

Yeah. Bordeaux is a beautiful, beautiful area of France.

Priscilla:

Oh I bet.

Ivan:

So since I am with my friend Stewart Emery, I thought it might be good to talk about one of the topics from Who’s in Your Room and we will include a link here for anyone who wants to check out the book. One of the sections is about learning how to say no without coming across like a jerk or worse. Sometimes people say they do come across like a jerk or worse. In running a networking organization, I understand that it is important as often as possible to say yes. I believe that whenever possible, say yes to to opportunities that one around. 

But there are in fact times when you so have to say no. One of the things that Steve Jobs said when he was alive about Apple was that he was proudest of the things they said no to. He was even more proud of the the things they said no to than the things they said yes to because sometimes it is very difficult and you have the difficult decision to say no in some situations. 

So as much as I like to say yes whenever possible, I want to give some ideas on how to say no and not come across like a jerk. So here is the first thing. I am going to give you seven points. Here is the first one. Know the difference between an opportunity and a distraction. You have to recognize this distinction by knowing your own personal and professional mission so you have to have a sense of what your role is in business and life. Then you can say no when someone comes to you with something that is a distraction to that mission.

This strategy can be particularly helpful for projects that maybe interest you in theory but don’t necessarily align with your goals. For a real life example, sometimesI have people say, ”BNI is great. I love it but you need to teach people how to sell because they get referrals and they don’t know how to sell.”  I say that is a fantastic mission. It’s just not ours. There are better organizations to teach sales than ours. Brian Tracey’s content, Ami Hopkins’ content, Jeffrey Gitomer’s content. These people are experts at selling. We would be a bad copy of their stuff. So it’s not our mission. It’s great mission. It’s not ours. 

So that is an example of knowing the difference between an opportunity and a distraction and being able to communicate that to someone. 

Here the second one. This is very tangible. This is a technique that I use a lot when I have to say no. This is my favorite go to way of saying no. “If I say yes, I am afraid I would let you down.” It is a really effective way to tell someone  no and let them understand that you like saying yes but if you said yes to this, you wouldn’t be able to do it because you don’t have the bandwidth, the knowledge, the expertise for what they are asking. But you are just not the best person to make this idea or request a success. You don’t want to disappoint them but you don’t want to let them down. So it is a great way of saying no to people, especially if you back it up with number three. 

Number three is refer them to someone more qualified. When I say no to someone really try to refer them to someone who is more qualified or suited to help that person. I may say, “I don’t want to let you down. But there is someone I can recommend you to that I think – I might even tie in number one – this is their mission. This is their focus. I think they would be a better person to help you with it.” People appreciate it, especially if you are going to say no, if you give them an alternative. This doesn’t work for me but here is somebody who might be able to help you. Okay?

Here is the fourth technique for saying no. “I don’t do that.” Sometimes the request and the response can be very simple. For example, this is a very simple example, since I had my health issue years ago, I no longer eat processed sugar. Period. So I often get people who say, “You have to try this piece of cake or pie.” I simply say, “Thanks. I don’t eat processed sugar.” then they will go on of course, “Just a bite. Just a bite.”  And I have no problem saying, “Just a bite? Okay, you have my bite because I don’t eat processed sugar.” You just have to stand by it. It’s a simple, I don’t do that. It’s not something that I do.  That is where you can tie in the mission and vision and your focus. “It’s not something I do.”  You can tie those two together.

Here is another way of saying no. Propose something else. For example, if you are unable to do something that you are being asked to do, offer them something else instead. For example we talk about this in the book, Who’s in Your Room, if you are a restaurant owner, maybe you can’t afford to cater a 5k charity race for free, but you can afford to donate several gift certificates for the charity to raffle. By proposing something else, you can still build the relationship and not just end with no. “I can’t do that, but I can do this.” So propose something else. 

Here is my sixth. I love this one. This is a fun one. Don’t Seinfeld it. One of the really fun things about the old TV series Seinfeld – do you remember Seinfeld? It was how the characters would go off on some crazy complicate subterfuge or rouse and end up getting in more trouble than if they had just been candid to start with. So people, be honest, be direct and don’t Seinfeld it. Use one of these techniques and don’t start getting these kinds of excuses and going off one some tangent. Just stay focused. 

Number seven ties it all together. When you say it, mean it. Be a broken record. Sometimes people just don’t take no for an answer. So try to be polite, smile and repeat what yo said before. Now, sometimes people don’t take no for an answer. Or what if they don’t take no for an answer? My answer to that is should you expect that will happen? Yes. It will definitely happen. You will meet people who won’t take no for an answer. WhatI have found is that if you keep saying no, eventually they get it. Be polite. Don’t be surprised if you repeat yourself multiple times before they understand that what you said you meant. I have been in situations where I have had to say the same thing three times. It took three times before they finally got it. The thing is just don’t get mad saying it again politely three times. “I am really not the best person and I really don’t want to let you down, but I could refer you to somebody else. Or I could give you another – I can’t do that but I can do this.” Just repeat it over and over and at some point they will finally get it.

Priscilla:

Right. Okay, those are all good.

Ivan:

Just seven suggestions that are right out of the book Who’s in Your Room and this is sort of an eighth suggestion but I don’t use it very often. You have to know that the word “no” an be a one word sentence. Occasionally, we encounter situations. I don’t like bringing bridges in my life. I prefer not to burn bridges. Sometimes just saying no, knowing that it is going to burn a bridge is necessary. No can be a one word sentence. I generally make it a three word sentence. No thank you. It’s okay to say no. 

You know, sometimes my podcasts are used in ways that are not particularly what I meant, so when you listen to this, don’t apply this podcast as “Ivan said it’s okay to say no so I am telling you no.” That’s not my intention here. My intention here is to use good discernment on the things that fit your mission, your vision, and your goals in your business and in your life. Use appropriate techniques to say no when it is not a good fit. You are not going to let somebody down. These are the kinds of things that we teach in the book, Who’s in Your Room, which I really like. It is a very actionable book. Great for education coordinators by the way. There is so much actionable content in the book. Since  was spending time this week with one of my coauthors, Stewart Emery, I thought it would be a great topic. 

Priscilla:

It is a great topic. I think it’s so useful, Ivan. It is difficult to say no and what you are aiming at is how to do it with tact and gracefully, right?

Ivan:

Yeah, that’s a great way to put it. How to do it in a caring way so that you are not burning bridges, because I really don’t like to burn bridges. It’s very rare that I am in a situation where I just don’t care and I am willing to burn a bridge. So these are ways of doing that tactfully. One other thing. If you are listening to this podcast, I would love to hear how you say no in a tactful and professional way. If you have something about that, I would love to hear it on BNI Podcast. Thanks, Priscilla. 

Priscilla:

Okay. You are so welcome. For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look froward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the BNI Podcast.

:

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Dr. Misner shares 7 tips for saying no without sounding like a jerk.

title.rendered: Episode 606: Meeting at a Different Time to Get More Visitors (Ask Ivan)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This question comes up at least once or twice every year. A BNI director passed it on from the president of a BNI chapter.

How about once a quarter we move our normal meeting from 7 am to an evening meeting so we can invite more visitors? We’ll do the same meeting, take the same attendance–it just might be easier to get visitors to come. I feel we need to do something different, so I really want to try moving one of our meetings to the evening.

Meeting at a different time was a thought that Dr. Misner himself had in 1986 and 1987. He tried it, and it turned out to be a complete and utter waste of time. People who won’t come to a morning meeting as visitors won’t come to a morning meeting as members.

The timed repetition of meeting every week at the same time contributes to BNI’s success. You can use meeting stimulants to help keep those morning meetings interesting for both members and visitors.

If your chapter has experimented with changing the meeting time or holding a social event outside the regular meeting, let us know in the comments.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla, and I have a question today. It is sort of an Ask Ivan question because somebody sent me this question, and I thought it would make an interesting podcast because it really goes back to 19n86 or 1987, when I first considered this idea and it comes up every year at least once or twice when somebody asks me the question, so I thought after all this time, maybe it would make a good podcast. 

Here is the question: It was sent to me by a BNI Director who said, “ I recently had a President ask a question that I would like your feedback on. The President said, ‘I have an idea. Once a quarter, we move our normal meeting from 7:00 am to an evening meeting so we can invit more visitors. The same meeting, take the same attendance. It just might be easier to get visitors to come. I feel it would be easier to get visitors to come but we need to do something different, so I really want to try moving one of our meetings to the evening.’”

Know here that the question went on and I am going to share a little more of the question here in a minute, but for everyone listening to this podcast, most groups meet in the morning all around the world. A very, very small number meet in the evening. A little larger number meet at lunch. So we do have groups around the world that meet at lunch and a very small number of chapters meet in the evening. Well over 90% – at least 90% or more meet in the morning. So most of our chapters do meet in the morning, and that should tell you something.

Here is a side issue that I want to address. He said, “Episode 585 will allow me to experiment without breaking the rules,” so I want to clarify this. My approach to a no win situation. That is not what 585 said. So as you are listening to podcasts, don’t say, “Oh, well, I am going to apply this podcast to everything I do.” That particular podcast wasn’t about changing the meeting time. Most importantly, is was really about how to deal with a no-win situation, and I don’t think meeting in the morning when 90% of the organization meets in the morning is a no-win situation. My side point here is don’t miss apply my podcasts to other situations. 585 definitely does not apply to the time of the meeting.

The Director asked me after she shared this entire question, do I have any suggestions to help navigate this issue. Here is my answer. I know before we went on the air, Priscilla, you had a thought too and I would love to hear yours. But here is my response:

Meeting at a different time, in this age, the evening, was a thought that I had in 1986 and 1987. I thought it was a fantastic idea to occasionally meet in the evening. And I tried it, and it was a complete and utter waste of time and effort. We discovered that we did, in fact, get new visitors to the evening meeting that would not come to the morning meeting. The problem was they still wouldn’t come to the morning meeting. So we disrupted everyone’s schedule and achieved zero new members. We did it twice twice with the same result. 

So I urge people not to do this. Changing time for everyone in hopes that you get more visitors might in fact work. You will get new visitors but the question is are these visitors who are going to join? The answer to that is no. Based on my experience, the answer to that is no. So why inconvenience everyone when people who have a hard time coming to morning meetings aren’t going to join anyway because your meetings are in the morning. So you had a suggestion, which I think is great and then there are a few other things. So share your suggestion, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Yeah, well, I was thinking if people want to meet in. The evening just to get to know new potential members, then why don’t they create a social event like have a wine event or some kind of meet and greet in a restaurant? 

Ivan:
In a restaurant, or sometimes I have seen them meet at one of the members’ locations. So it is kind of a two prong approach.  You get exposure for a member and their location and you have a social in the evening. I think that is a brilliant idea, and I recommend it. But I wouldn’t replace a meeting with it. You have to remember what is the purpose of bringing in visitors. It is not just to have another body. It is to have potential members. So you could invite the mayor of a city but they are not a potential member. Inviting certain people in certain businesses just to get a body is not what you are wanting. What you want are people who are potential members to the organization.

So my experience is if they are not going to come to an early meeting, they are not going to come. Having evening meeting to get them to just come doesn’t solve the problem. Here is why I think keeping the meetings in the morning – or if you are having a lunch meeting or an evening meeting, keeping them at that time. It’s the power of timed repetition on. Doing things over and over on a long period of time on a consistent basis yields results. It is about doing 6 things 1000 times, not 1000 things 6 times. So that process of timed repetition is a known success process. Changing things up wont necessarily yield the results you want. 

It’s also important to understand that BNI is not for everyone.  You want people who are willing to commit to weekly meeting at the same time and at the same place. Making everyone change to a different time to possibly attract someone who doesn’t want. To go to the meeting at the normal time is just generally not a good strategy. I want to say that it is not that it is a bad idea. I thought it was a really good idea when I tried it decades ago. It just was a bad result. It was a good idea but didn’t get the result that I had hoped. 

Let me give a positive approach to this issue.  Here is something you can do to keep the meetings lively, interesting and fun that you should consider. Those would be things like our meeting stimulants, like cards in a basket, true false quiz, tools of the trade, industry trends, bringing your substitute. If you are hearing this and have no idea what I am talking about, talk to your local BNI Director. We have many, many dozens of meeting stimulants that keep things interesting and fun so that the visitors that come experience a BNI meeting and yet the members are also having a good time and keeping things light and interesting. 

I just don’t recommend that you change the time just to get more visitors because it is not going to be the visitors that are likely to join. Does that make sense?

Priscilla:
Yeah, totally.

Ivan:
So, you know, keep these questions coming. You can email me at AskIvan@bni.com. I am not able to answer every question on this podcast, but the questions that come up over and over again are the ones that I definitely want to touch upon here at BNI podcast. If you have had an experience in trying to change up times I would love to hear it here on the podcast. Feel free to post your experience in changing your time. I would be particularly interested in hearing about social events, which was Priscilla’s suggestion. I would particularly love to hear about social events that you might have done. Now, whether you have done anything maybe at one of the members’ locations or at a restaurant, I think that is a great idea to get exposure to BNI for some people that may not come to the morning meeting but again, they are unlikely to make a commitment if they are not willing to do a morning meeting to begin with, so that’s everything I have for today, Priscilla, thank you.

Priscilla:
Okay. Thank you so much for that great information. For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look froward to having you join again next week for another exciting episode of the BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

title.rendered: Episode 605: Givers Gain Is a Standard, Not a Sword (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 330.

Synopsis

“Givers Gain” is a philosophy based on the law of reciprocity. It’s a great standard to apply to ourselves. But it shouldn’t be used to judge others. Accusing others of not having a Givers Gain attitude usually means those people are not doing something you want. This kind of criticism and judgement can be very destructive to relationships. That’s part of why BNI groups need to be very selective about the attitude of the people they ask to join.

If you find yourself under attack by one of these vitriolic people, the best response is to be yourself. The most effective response is for someone else to tell the caustic person s/he is wrong. Standing up for others will encourage others to stand up for you–and that, too, is an example of Givers Gain.

If you aren’t getting as many referrals from a fellow member as you’re giving, don’t point fingers. Evaluate quality as well as quantity of referrals, and remember that what you give will come back to you from the chapter as a whole, not just the individual.

Do you have a story about dealing with an unreasonably critical person? Don’t name names, but leave a comment to tell us how you handled it.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of BNI Podcast Episode 330 –

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan. How are you?

Ivan:
I am doing great, Priscilla. Thanks for doing such a good job on these podcasts. You have been the host now for many years and I appreciate your work.

Priscilla:
You are so welcome. I just noticed that I think we have been doing this since 2007.

Ivan:
We have. We have. So thank you. You are doing a wonderful job, and these podcasts have really grown in terms of listenership over the years. Today I have an interesting topic on Givers Gain, our core philosophy. Givers Gain is a philosophy based on the law of reciprocity and in the context of networking groups like BNI, people who adopt this philosophy, which is the core of BNI’s program, really dedicate themselves to giving business to their fellow BNI members rather than making their foremost concern getting business themselves.

In doing so, other people naturally become eager to repay their kindness by sending them business in return. Givers Gain is a great way to live life in general and it is a standard which we can all apply to ourselves. The keyword being “ourselves”. Givers Gain is not a sword to be pointed at others who may not adopt the philosophy. We use it in the context of referrals, but I think it can definitely be used as a way to carry on business and life.

Unfortunately, I have seen the Givers Gain concept abused from time to time. As you may have guessed, that is the reason I am doing this podcast now because I saw it abused recently. The entire concept gets misused when we start pointing the finger at others and saying, “So and so doesn’t have a Givers Gain attitude. He is going about it all wrong.”

What’s interesting is when we start saying things like this about other people, it’s often because they are not doing something that we think they ought to be doing in life. Givers Gain is not a sword to wave around at people who aren’t doing what we think they should be doing. It is a standard that we should be applying to ourselves and ourselves primarily, if not only. Ironically, when we point the finger at somebody- point the index finger at someone- there’s three fingers pointing back at us. Have you ever noticed that?

Priscilla:
Yeah, that’s right.

Ivan:
That’s right. When you point a finger at someone, there are three fingers pointing back at you. It’s a perfect reminder of not being that kind of person that blames others because that behavior is what you need to be focused on, not other people’s behavior. People who criticize and point fingers at others can really be caustic. I have seen it in BNI groups. I have seen it in business organizations. I have seen it at the Director level within the BNI organization.

I think it’s one of the reasons I think you should be selective about the people you surround yourself with, particularly in networking groups. Be selective about bringing in people with the right attitude. With that said, there will undoubtedly still be people in your life who are unendingly critical and judgmental. They are just plain vitriolic. I know that I certainly have a couple people in my life, including one person in particular, who appears to have made criticizing me his favorite past time. And there are people who love to talk about you but almost never actually talk to you about the issues that they have.

So what do you do if you practice the Givers Gain philosophy in a sincere and consistent way and yet there is still someone waging a very personal attack on you? Because it happens. And how you do respond when they starting waving their interpretation of what you should be doing with Givers Gain like a sword of criticism?

I think the answer is simple; be yourself. Continue to apply the philosophy to yourself in every way that you possibly can. Vitriolic people are that way because they can’t control themselves. Maybe they are angry. Maybe they had a difficult life. Who knows? It doesn’t really matter because they are who they are and you can’t change them. As much as we would like to steer clear of these people, there are times when it’s virtually impossible.

Telling someone they are wrong about you never works. I know this from personal experience. They will just come in even stronger. I can tell you what does work, though. What really works is when somebody else stands up and says to the person who is badmouthing you that they are out of line or that what is being said simply isn’t appropriate. Like a referral, nothing beats a third party endorsement- or in this case, a third party defense.

So why am I bringing this all to light? Because the fact is if you are in BNI you are going to find yourself around some difficult people occasionally. I mean, the more you can screen, the more effective you will be at not having those around you at a BNI meeting, but sometimes it just happens. Somebody with a really bad attitude is going to get in, and somebody who is combative instead of collaborative, someone who is possibly saying horrible things about somebody else that may even be about you.

So what I would like you to do- I would like to take this opportunity to strongly encourage you to stand tall and speak up. Good people stand up when caustic people say bad things about others. If you practice Givers Gain as your own personal standard, you already know that standing up for others will encourage others to stand up for you.

I just wanted to take this time today to talk on a little deeper level about how I can see givers gain being applied in this organization. I think it is a powerful standard by which we operate and it is something that sets BNI aside from every other networking organization in the world. But we have to understand that it is a standard that we have to apply to ourselves and not a sword to use to wave around at other people. That is my message for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay. Well, I thought that you were going to talk about something like this: I gave you a referral and I used Givers Gain and you didn’t give me a referral. I thought that was what you were going to talk about.

Ivan:>
Right. Well, you know that’s another example. When someone is applying the standard on somebody else, there are a lot of things that can go wrong with that. For example, if I am a florist and you are a real estate agent, you are going to be giving me a whole lot more referrals than I am going to be giving you. So the first thing is that you can’t just take a look at the number of referrals that are being passed but the value of the referrals and the quality of the referrals.

Another thing to remember is that in BNI, what goes around come around but it may not come around directly from the person you gave it to. One of the things that makes our program work is building relationships with a lot of people. When people see you giving and giving and giving referrals to this person and giving referrals to that person, they want to give referrals to you- and not just the people that you have been giving referrals to but other people as well.

There is nothing wrong with sitting down and having a conversation with that person who is not giving you referrals, saying, “Hey, I have been able to give you two or three referrals. Can we talk about how you may be able to reciprocate?” That is a positive, supportive way of having that conversation. But what you shouldn’t do is point your finger and say, “You are not giving me referrals back.” That just leads to animosity and there may be a number of factors involved.

Givers Gain has to be a standard of personal conduct, not a sword to battle other people with.

Priscilla:
Right. Okay. Well, I like that. I think that’s excellent.

Ivan:
I like it, too. I hope we have some members share this at BNI chapters. I wouldn’t mind if a couple of Directors heard it, too.

Priscilla:
Do you have anything else you would like to add?

Ivan:
Just one quick thing. If you are listening to this podcast or reading the transcript, do you have a story about a person who was criticizing you to others or other people to you? It doesn’t have to be a BNI story- any experience of someone who was criticizing you to others or others to you. How did you handle it? I would love to hear your story as well as your feedback on this podcast and the Givers Gain concept in general. Please feel free to leave your comments here. Don’t name names. Don’t point fingers, but tell the story in a way that is a lesson for all of us. I would love to see your comments. Thanks.

Priscilla:
Well thank you so much, Dr. Misner. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by networkingnow.com, the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. Thanks for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: “Givers Gain” is a philosophy based on the law of reciprocity. It’s a great standard to apply to ourselves. But it shouldn’t be used to judge others.

title.rendered: Episode 604: BNI Connect – World Wide Referrals

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Jeremy Walsh, Director of Support and Documentation for BNI Connect Global, joins Dr. Misner this week because it’s now possible to pass a referral to another BNI member anywhere in the world with BNI Connect.

The biggest challenge in rolling out this feature was currency: if a member in London gives a referral to a member in New York, the member in New York gets paid in US dollars, but the member in London needs to know the value of that referral in pounds.

BNI Connect’s electronic referral slips provide two kinds of accountability. First, the alert system notifies the recipient of the referral right away, whether that person is in the same chapter or on the other side of the world.

Second, every PALMS report comes with a Slips Audit Report attached to it, so the vice president and leadership team can look down the list of referrals and see which were in-chapter referrals and which were cross-chapter referrals.

If you have a great story about a cross-chapter or cross-region referral on BNI Connect, share it in the comments.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Priscilla:

Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you?

Ivan:

I am doing fantastic, and I have another guest on my podcast today, somebody who has been on at least 8 times, probably more. Jeremy Walsh. Jeremy is the Director of Support and Documentation for BNI Connect worldwide and he helps to keep BNI Connect moving forward in a positive fashion. Since implementing a positive support strategy and help desk in 2011, the BNI Connect Support Team now provides. 24 hours a day five days a week coverage for support questions and issues globally. 99% of inquiries are being responded to within one business day. 88% are solved within an hour of receipt, and so far, the team has responded to over 125,783 inquiries from members and directors. His live and recorded training is available live and with hundreds of videos that will go on the BNI Connect global YouTube channel, which has logged over 195,000 views – I am jealous Jeremy, I think it has more views than I do. In his spare time, he owns The Right Click, an IT consulting business with 17 employees, three retail locations. He is very active in charitible organizations in his community. Jeremy, welcome to BNI [Podcast] again.

Jeremy:

Thank you so much I am always very happy to be here. I love doing these with you. Thank you.

Ivan:

It’s my pleasure and to me this is a really exciting interview because for the first time, and I started BNI over 34 years ago. For the first time ever, the organization can pass referrals to any member anywhere in the world. We now have a global referral process. Do you want to talk about that a little?

Jeremy:

Oh, absolutely. I am so exited for this. As a matter of fact, one of the reasons I am so excited for this is of those 125,000 support tickets we have gotten, I would probably say like 5,000 just had to do with requesting this particular feature over the past several years. As anybody has been following along with the BNI Connect saga, we started out in 2011 and it was really just a platform for bringing the world together in our data. But then in 2012, we actually introduced the whole online referral process, where we started to not just pass the slips in the chapters but we actually started doing this online through BNI Connect which gave us a whole wealth of information about our memberships because, by the way, I am still a a member. So I am using this on a daily basis. I could not just track the PALMS report how many referrals, who was I giving referrals to and who was I getting referrals from, and really have muh greater insight into the whole referral process. Then about 2014 we were able to now not just refer in out chapter but refer cross cahpter. 

Everytime something like this happens, it takes our network and opens it up just one step further. So instead of just being able to pass referrals from people in our chapter, we can also pass referrals to other chapters in our region. Then we could do cross region. I think this was about 2016 that we opened it up and I could then not just pass to other people- I am in a member in Rhode Island – not just Rhode ISland, but I could pass to people in Massachusetts. To people in Florida. So my grandmother could call up and say, “Hey,my faucet is dripping,” and I could look up a BNI member in Florida and actually pass them a referral to somebody down there. So fast forward now into November 2018. Now we are able to pass referrals to anybody in the world. 

It did take us a little while to get to this point. I will be honest with you. The reason it took this long was simple because of currency and we didn’t really have a way to track the currency correctly because when you do a Thank You for Closed Business, it is not just about referrals but we have the 1-2-1s and the Thank You for Closed Business. When you pass Thank You for Closed Business, I received the money in US dollars when I invoiced my client. But if you are from let’s say London, you actually need to be recognized not in US dollars but in pounds or Euros. Right? So this whole conversion process has to happen and we were finally able to get that into the system so that we could record everything correctly. I am just so excited for this.

Ivan:

BNIConnect is just so much more complex than people understand, but the more you get into it, you understand just how incredibly complicated it is. So, how is this different for the old days of 2011 and 2012?

Jeremy:

We are seeing much greater adoption rates. You know, Ivan, correct me if I am wrong, but I think way back n the day, it was actually kind of frowned upon at one point to do cross chapter. Is that correct?

Ivan:

It was. It was. Somewhere in the middle of BNI Connect, we opened it up for cross chapter, then it was cross region. Now it is cross global. It is cross country. But in the old days, the real old days, 1985 and 1986, we did allow people to give referrals to other chapters but what we discovered was that because there was no way to determine whether the referral was really given, some people gave fake referrals.They would stand up  and say they gave a referral to someone in another chapter last weekend. We discovered that they weren’t always being completely candid because there was no way to track it. About 1986, we stopped allowing people to give referrals that counted in their chapter to other chapters just because there was no way to have any accountability. So from ‘86 until 2011 or 2012, we couldn’t – when did we go to other chapters? Was is 2014, 2015?

Jeremy:

Yeah. I think so. It was 2012 when we first opened it up and then it started coming around I think around 2014 but there was still a lot of resistance.

Ivan:

Yeah. There was. So we went 28 years without being able to do cross chapter referrals and then we were able to do cross region and now cross national, cross country, which really is my ultimate dream for being able to generate referrals in the organization. So I am so excited about it. Tell me a little bit about the accountability of the online activity. 

Jeremy:

Absolutely. One of the things that actually Vice Presidents approach us all the time about is how do we even do the referral reality check, for example, when all we have are these electronic bits that are floating around. Honestly, there are two sides to the accountability piece when it comes to electronic slips. The first side of it is the fact that back in the days that you just stood up with a piece of paper and said, “Oh yeah, I have a referral for John Smith in the other chapter,” that slip actually may or may not make it to the other chapter. The VP really can’t follow up on it. The other member doesn’t even really know that they got that referral. 

But now with BNI Connect, we have two things. We have the alert system. If you pass an electronic referral, the other person gets notified of it right away. So they get an. Email in their inbox saying, “Hey, you just received a referral from so-and-so.” That’s whether it is an in-chapter referral or an across world referral. They will still get that email mail notifiying them. So that right there, there’s a personal accountability knowing they don’t really want to pass a fake referral because that other person is going to know about it,  right?

Ivan:

Exactly.

Jeremy:

The second layer of accountability comes from the system itself and every single PALMS report has what is called the slips audit report which is attached to it. The Vice President and LeadershipTeam  can actually look down that list as see whether it was an in chapter referral, a cross chapter referral – and if it was a cross chapter referral, which chapter did it go to. So they can now link that together and do follow ups in order to do a reality check in the meeting, but also if they really need to reach out tot he other chapter to check on it, they can do it that way as well. So we have really come full circle with it.

Ivan:

We really have. I am just so excited to see us get to this point because I think it is really important to keep our referrals as much as possible in the BNI family. This finally allows us to do that. I’m sure you have probably heard some stories. I just heard a story a few months back about an attorney who was in Europe who got a referral from an attorney in New York. It was the New York attorney’s biggest client and they were doing business globally and they needed a European attorney, so he referred him to a BNI European attorney and that attorney in Europe said that this client has now become their largest client. So the largest client they have from any source came from a referral off of BNI Connect from a New York attorney. To me, that is the system coming to fruition, the process coming to fruition.

Jeremy:

I am so glad that you had this vision to even pull this whole thing together. I think I said this the last time I saw you but thank you for BNI Connect and thank you for having the vision to pull this whole thing together. We are really bringing referrals to the whole world. IT is just an awesome feeling.

Ivan:

It is and I think BNI Connect has finally reached the point to where I had hoped it would get to. It is now going to become better and better with the app and everything else that we have been adding to it. But I have to tell you, Jeremy, you more than probably most people know how painful it was to get to this point. When people ask me what was the hardest thing I have ever done in BNI, I always go to BNI Connect. It was such a monumental undertaking, and I have to do a shout out to all of the BNI Directors who had their own platform, people like Nancy Jacamucci and the Aesop’s in the UK and so many other – Graham Southwell – and they all had these different platforms but I have to tell you, it took some work but they were all willing to work together to collaborate to come up with once system, globally. Those people and many, many others like you, Jeremy, are what made it happen. So thank you.

Jeremy:

Awesome. I do have one special request if that is okay.

Ivan:

Yeah, sure.

Jeremy:

If anybody out there has a story, has a BNI Connect story, especially about a great cross country. Or cross region referral, I would love it if you email me those. I would love to put those together, that we could compile and share.

Ivan:

Could we put them up on the podcast? That would be- they could email it to you, but then I want you to share it on the podcast. If you are listening to this you an email it to Jeremy by going to the support section, right, Jeremy? Support.BNIconnect.com

Jeremy:

Yep. Anyway that you want to get it to me, support@bniconnect.com or go to support.bniconnect.com and you can submit a support ticket. Jeremy@BNI.com, carrer pigeon, whatever works.

Ivan:

I would love to see them here on the podcast as well, so if you don’t mind posting them up on the podcast as well, I would really, really appreciate it. Well, we are well over time, Jeremy. Any other website that you want to mention? Obviiously BNIConnect and a lot of this content is at BNIuniversity.com.

Jeremy:

Absolutely. BNI University. Please log in there and make use of that accumulation of knowledge that we have there.

Ivan:

Jeremy, thanks for being on the show again. Back to you, PRiscilla.

Priscilla:

Okay. Thank you so much you guys, very very good. Well, I think that is it for this week. For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Thanks to BNI Connect, it’s now possible to pass referrals to BNI members anywhere in the world.

title.rendered: Episode 603: Too Busy for BNI

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Mike Macedonio joins Dr. Misner this week to talk about how to handle it when members say they’re too busy for BNI and are thinking of leaving.

Leaving BNI because it brings you too much business is forfeiting an asset. Don’t stop going to the gym just because you’re in shape. Instead, increase your capacity.

Take a look at what you’re busy doing and who you’re doing it for. Are you doing your best work for your best clients? Are you taking on tasks you should hire someone else to do? You may need to let go of things that don’t serve you to make room for things that do.

Sometimes a member who’s thinking of leaving BNI says “I’m too busy” in order to avoid mentioning the real reasons, such as a conflict with another member.

If you’re on the membership team, make sure to ask whether there’s any other reason this person is thinking of leaving.

Watch Mike Macedonio’s videos on this subject and more at BNI-educationalvideos.com.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Priscilla:

Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?   

Ivan:

Well I just came back from the US conference which I mentioned last week and I’m actually doing a writing retreat in Texas Safari Ranch this week which is like a log home and there are wild animals everywhere, none that are threatening to people but wild animals everywhere. It is a blast. It is a great place to do a writing retreat. 

I am really excited. I have a good friend today on BNI Podcast. It is one I have known for many, many years. That is Mike Macedonio. He has been on the Podcast before. He is a New York Times best selling author. He is an entrepreneur. He is a Co-Executive Director for BNI in the Bay Area. In this role, he has helped thousands of local and international business owners build their business and a spectacular life. Two of his proudest moments were twice competing in the – is it Leadville Trail, Mike?

Mike:

Yes.

Ivan:

Leadville Trail, a 100 mile bike race in California. You are a better man than I. I can’t imagine even starting that. He has a passion for not only his marriage and wonderful wife, Dawn Lyons, but for entertaining friends. I have had the opportunity to have had some of his amazing meals many times at his home. So, Mike, welcome to BNI Podcast.

Mike:

Thank you, Ivan.

Ivan:

It is great to have you on and I absolutely love this topic. I reached out to you because I watched you do a video on this andI thought it was brilliant. It is basically dealing with people who say they are too busy for BNI. Mike, I am just going to let you run with it and share what I saw on the video. I may jump in from time to time, but the mic is yours.

Mike:

Ivan, I am sure you have heard it way more than I have, but it is just one of the most disturbing things to me when I hear business owners saying they are getting too busy for BNI, meaning they are going to move on and stop their marketing. It is disturbing for me because in many of these cases, I know the people and their businesses well enough to know that though they may be busy and have a lot of time crunches, their businesses are not necessarily working on all cylinders and they are certainly not optimizing everything they can. 

Again, when I hear these messages that they are too busy, I feel like I want to jump in and save them and just ask them some questions. It is just like, okay, you are too busy? Is it because you are not working with the right type of clients? Are you too busy because you are doing the type of things in your business that you should no longer be doing? 

I guess in my view, whenever I think about, you know, getting consumed with a lot of business coming your way is what are we doing to manage it? The first opportunity we have when we get busy is how do we increase capacity? I will stop right there for a second, Ivan, because I know you have a lot of experience and thoughts. What is your reaction when you hear people saying, “I am too busy for BNI. I am going to stop marketing and go into retreat”?

Ivan:

I find it mind boggling because it is like you have gotten busy because of your efforts in BNI and so now the very thing that got you where you are now successful or more successful, you are going to stop doing. That’s crazy. It reminds me of a commercial that I saw many, many years ago. For the millennials, you probably don’t remember this, but for the Yellow Pages. You remember the Yellow Pages, Mike, and the telephone book?

Mike:

Of course.

Ivan: 

So I remember this commercial they did for this guy. It was actually a radio commercial talking about the Yellow Pages. It was for Tom’s Desks, and Tom had a desk that he sold, but he didn’t want to sell it because it was his floor model. So his business was selling this desk, but he wouldn’t actually sell the desk because it was a floor model. And so the absolute absurdity of that was obvious, and I think this is the same. It is absolutely absurd to say I am so busy that I am going to stop doing the marketing that helped me succeed.

Mike:

One of the comments that I have made is when people are making that decision what they are doing is forfeiting an asset. A lot of people come back to me and say, “Well, Mike. You.can’t consider advertising an asset. That’s more of an investment or cost.”

I would agree with that partially except when you are doing relationship marketing. Relationship marketing is time that has been invested in people. I think when you have a higher network that is helping you get busy, that is an asset.

Ivan:

Yeah. Now, on your video, you talked about you’re too busy – instead of thinking about being so busy, you need to do something else and that is to increase your – do you want to share with them?

Mike:

Sure. Absolutely. Increase the capacity. Who are the other people who need to be brought in, whether they are independent contractors or staff or other support to possibly take things off your plate that you shouldn’t be doing anymore or simply to serve more clients. I think that is probably one of the most obvious, not that it is easy or simple. When I think about growing a business, that is going to be part of our plan. I use the expression that you don’t stop going to the gym just because you are in shape. 

This is one of those opportunities where we are continuing to market but one of the other things that we can be looking at is what are we marketing to? Who are we attracting? Just as many times, when I hear people say they are too busy, they are still taking on the type of business that may not serve them, emotionally and financially.

Ivan:

So what you are suggesting is a couple of things. First, take a look at the clients that you are currently with. Are they really profitable for you and are they the kinds of clients that you want to work with? If they are not, this is a great time if you are that busy to let go of those customers or clients that you have that aren’t fulfilling you in business and maybe ramp up the quality of your customers so that the time you are spending is on the most important customers. You can actually increase your income by doing that. 

The other thing is the capacity point that you made. I talk to so many businesses that say to me, how do you scale the business? Well, the first thing that you do is you don’t stop marketing if you want to scale your business. If you want to scale your business, you do something crazy like hire people, like you were suggesting. It may be outside contractors, maybe employees. Something. But scaling your business is a much better idea. Increasing your capacity is a much better idea than saying I am too busy and. I am not going to keep doing this.

Mike:

Yeah, this is something that we talk about in The World’s Best Known Marketing Secret,  probably too big of a conversation right now, but is your.business on mission? Do you have a defined mission? I look at, you know, my mission serves me in creating my not to do list. If I am getting too busy, there are probably too many things on my not to list that I am still doing.

Ivan:

Yeah. For many many years, I had a list on my Outlook “to do” but it’s the things I should not be doing anymore.

Mike:

I love it.

Ivan:

I had it for years. Every time I was doing something and I thought, “Why am I doing this?” – I remember the time that I was putting antivirus software on all of the computers at headquarters in Opland, by the way, so it wasn’t like 20 years ago. I thought why am I doing this? My time should be spent writing, doing interviews and managing the company, not putting antivirus software on all the computers. That went on my things I shouldn’t be doing anymore list, and I started then thinking about who I could hire to do that kind of stuff. 

That’s daily. So you are not being busy doing stuff that is not going to build your business.

Mike:

Can I say one other notion? When that business is coming to you and you do decide that, really I shouldn’t be taking on this type of work anymore, it’s not something that serves me emotionally or financially, who else could? Are there other resources out there that you know can service those people? You call them friendly competition. People in the business that would take on that type of business, again, that is not necessarily going to serve you.

Ivan:

Yeah, I think you are absolutely right and that is kind of doing a better job that picking and choosing what it is that we do with our business. Now, there is a whole other area that I think we need to consider on this issue of I am too busy, and tell me if you have seen this. Sometimes, when people say they are too busy for BNI, they are really having another problem in the chapter and they don’t want to bring it up. Have you seen that?

Mike:

That is the plain being nice solution. The “being nice” is “I am too busy” as opposed to “I have a conflict with somebody or something is not sitting well with me.” Sure.

Ivan:

I have seen that. As a matter of fact, the first time I saw it was probably 32 or 33 years ago, where a printer who was getting a ton of business said, “I am just too busy. I am leaving. I need to step down.” I was just shocked, and so I called him up and you know the exit interview. You have seen the exit interview that we have in our manuals, Mike.

Mike:

Yeah. Sure.

Ivan:

I write that exit interview for him. I called him up andI said, “We have started doing exit interviews.” I didn’t tell him, “You are the first one,” But I asked him, “May I ask why you are leaving?” He said he was too busy. And then there is this concept called semantic differential questioning that if you ask the same question in different ways, you get a different result. SoI asked him, “Is there any other reason?”  He said, “No, no. That is it.” I said okay. Then I asked him a few more questions and then finally, I said, “If there were another reason, what would it be?” He said, “Alright! The truth. Is so-and-so in my chapter owes me money and I am tired of seeing them every week so I am quitting.” 

Okay, so the wrong person is leaving. Let’s take it to the membership committee. It’s a longer story, but we took it to the membership committee and one of the members said, “Oh my goodness. He owes you money, too? He owes me money, too.” So they kicked out the member who was a problem and the printer stayed. 

So for those people who really feel like they are too busy, you need to get some business sense, to be honest with you, because that is not the way to scale a company. For those of you who have another reason, you need to have a conversation with the membership committee. Would you agree with that?

Mike:

Absolutely. The resource is right there. The resource is the membership committee or the Director, but yes, absolutely. You know, going back to this printer, how much money may he have left behind if he let this one person who wasn’t paying him make that decision for him. Not to even mention the ripple effect of everyone else in the chapter.

Ivan:

Yeah. Absolutely right, but sometimes people just don’t want to make a scene. They don’t want to create a problem. They don’t realize that the problem they are having someone else may behaving as well. 

So he ended up being very thankful that we did what we did. And I kind of nudged him in the direction thinking  this through. 

Find out if the real reason is if someone is too busy. If so, then send them to this podcast and send them to a video that Mike has done. Mike, you have done a whole website with a number of videos, including the one with this topic. Would you share the website with everyone here?

Mike:

Yeah.My wife Dawn Lyons and I have done an educational video YouTube channel. You got get them by putting in BNIeducationalvideos.com. Or just BNI educational videos and yeah, topics like this “I am getting too busy for BNI. How do we get more success out of BNI?” I would love for you to come in and check us out.

Ivan:

Yeah, please.take a look at those videos and take a look at this video. The next time you have someone in BNI say that they are too busy, please refer them to this podcast and refer them to the website.

Mike, my friend, it is always a a pleasure to have you on BNI Podcast. Thanks for being here.

Mike:

Thank you, Ivan.

Ivan:

Over to you, Priscilla.

Pricilla:

Okay.  Thank you both. I think that was really a very interesting podcast. I think that is it for this week. Thank you so much for the great information. For more of Ivan’s content go to www. IvanMisner.com. Thanks so much for listening. This is Priscilla  Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

title.rendered: Episode 602: Book Club for BNI

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Elisabeth Misner and Hazel Walker put together Reading Circle Discussion Questions for Dr. Misner’s new book, Who’s in Your Room?

These are fantastic discussion questions for your group’s education coordinator so that your BNI group can bring in quality people. Here are some examples.

  1. How has the quality of your chapter been impacted by the people in your chapter?
  2. What do you need to change in order to have the chapter of your dreams?
  3. Does your membership committee have a well-defined understanding of who should be allowed into your chapter? Are they screening based on values?
  4. Who are two people who bring out the best in you? How do they link to your values? How have they made a difference?
  5.  

Listen to more podcast episodes on this topic.

Who’s In Your Room Discussion Questions Download

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Priscilla:

Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?   

Ivan:

This week I am in Dallas Texas for the US conference for BNI. We have about, I don’t know, 500 of my closest friends here. 

Priscilla:

Well, that sounds great.

Ivan:

These are always fun.

Priscilla:

So tell us a little bit about the topic today, your idea for book clubs.

Ivan:

So I had someone recently say to me, “You have all those BNI groups and you have done so many books. Have you done any book discussion groups or discussion clubs?” I said no, we haven’t. So I thought that was a great idea. My wife, Elisabeth, and Hazel Walker put together what they call the Reading Circle Discussion Questions for one of my books. I thought what I might do here is talk a little it about the concept of a book discussion group and how you can apply one of my books to this by following the instructions. We are going to have the instructions right here o on the podcast.

We have a Word file that you can actually download and take a look at what I am talking about. Now, if you have a chapter that really wants to immerse itself in a culture of learning and do a book discussion group, this is going to be perfect. If they don’t want to go that far, at least have the education coordinator take the concepts that are in these discussion questions – and I think they are fantastic discussion questions for the educational coordinator time each week, and you can do them over several weeks and have the. Whole chapter talk about it. It would help if most of the people read the book, but at least you can have a dialogue about some of the concepts in here.

In the book discussion outline, we talk about. It from the perspective of Who’s in Your Room, which is the latest book I did. There are some podcasts that I have done that relate to this and as I go through my discussion here, I will call them out. I will let you know which podcast. The concept of the book is imagine that you live your life in one room and that one room has one door and it is an enter only door so when people come into your room or your life, they are there forever. We say it is more than a metaphor because every time you think about that person, even if you get them out of your life, they are still in your head. If they are still in your head, they are still in your life. 

Sometimes that is what happens in a BNI chapter. People come into a BNI chapter and they create chaos in the group. You know, that memory stays with the chapter. There are so many things that we can do better to help ensure that we bring in quality people and don’t have to go through that. 

So what I suggest in your book discussion group or if your education coordinator is doing this from week to week- you are looking at it as who is in your room, but also think about it from the perspective of who is in your network? How doe these concepts apply to your BNI group? Has the quality of your chapter been impacted by the people you have allowed into your chapter? Reflect on the people in your chapter who really resonate with you. Discuss what that is like for you. If you are doing a group discussion I would call out those people. I would say, “You know, so-and-so has really made a difference in my life. This person has really helped me.” Share that with people. 

Now, in the group discussion, we also say reflect on the people in your life who bring you dissonance. Okay, if you are going to do that in the chapter, don’t name names. Just don’t get yourself in trouble. That could be a problem. I think in this chapter of the book, we talk about what do you need to change in order to have the chapter of your dreams. Again we are doing it from the perspective of the life of your dreams. I want you to think of this book as it applies to the chapter of your dreams. 

Again, the book discussion outline covers all of this in detail from who’s in your room. I am giving you content and trying to adopt it here to who’s in your chapter? Chapter two is about guarding your door. That is all about the membership committee. It doesn’t say it in the book but guarding the food is your membership committee. So how strong is your membership committee? Do they have a well-defined understanding of who should be allowed into your chapter and who shouldn’t and why? And do they understand the values of the organization?Are they screening based on values, which is in Chapter Three Determining Your Values.

BNI has seven core values. I believe the membership committees should be done considering the core values of BNI as they look at people coming into the chapter. Givers gain, building relationships, lifelong learning, traditions plus innovation, positive attitude, accountability, recognition. These are BNI’s core values. I have seen some chapters that added a really important core value for them. You know, one or two additions to the core values of the organization. I don’t think its necessary but I have no objection if somebody wants to add a healthy core value. So the membership committee should really know what those core values are and be screening people into your chapter based on values. 

Then it is time to assess who’s in your room, or in this case, who’s in your chapter. Here, this is a great opportunity to share two people who bring out the best in you and how they link back t your values. Again, if you are going to do this as a group discussion, a subgroup of the chapter, I would call people out. I would say, “You have made a difference in my life and here is how.” And be really specific. Even if you are doing this as an educational coordinator discussion and of course, it is very short – I would still all out people who have really helped you in some way. 

Now, here we identify people who have not brought out the best in you. Again, don’t name names. But you know, if you are going to have a deeper conversation, it is okay to talk about the kinds of things you have seen in people who don’t bring out the best in you. 

The next chapter is about training your doorman. Oh by the way, in assessing who is in your room, take a look at podcast numerous 479 Guarding Your Door, the Membership Committee. Take a look at podcast number 594 and for the first topic, imagine you live in one room, take a look at podcast 587. So 587 is Chapter One. 594is Chapter Two. 479 is Chapter Four, assessing who is in your room. 

Now,Chapter 5, Training Your Doorman. Identify someone who is an engine or an anchor in your life. An engine or an anchor.  I now I have done a podcast on that a well.  I think it might be 479 or you can just search on engines and anchors, because that is a really powerful metaphor for the people who help drive you forward or hold you back. 

Then there is an opportunity to discuss people who are already in your room and how to set boundaries for yourself d how to say “No” without being a jerk. That is a really important discussion for people to have in a smaller group and possibly even at a chapter level.

The next chapter is I think probably the most important and that is living the life of your dreams, which in BNI having a chapter of your dreams and creating a balance and harmony in your chapter. We just did a podcast not long ago on that. Podcast 598 was for harmony and balance. 

So I would urge people, members, to take the concept of who’s in your room – it is a powerful book. It’s all about relationships. And I invite you to group discussions, book dissing, where you can go through this content and apply it to your chapter. So you look at who’s in your room and you fill it and replace it with who is in your chapter? The concepts are absolutely perfect for that. We are going to include a couple of links here.

I am going to include the worksheet with all the questions that you can ask if you decide to do a book discussion or the education coordinator wants to talk about there. I m going to also include a link for anyone who wants to sign up for free webinars. I am finishing one set and I will be doing another set very soon. After I do the live seminars, they will stay up there. As recorded webinars so you can still access what we do now far into. The future with a lot of recorded webinars on these different concepts.

So that is my podcast for today, Priscilla. What are. Your thoughts about book discussion groups?

Priscilla:

I think it sounds very interesting, brand new and something that  will bring up to my chapter and see if they are interested in it.

Ivan: 

I would love to see chapters actually do a side meeting one day a week or maybe once a month. Take a chapter and talk about it how this applies to their chapter. I will tell you what- any chapter that does that, email me, connect with me at askivan@bni.com. You connect with me when you are all done covering the whole book and I will send a video message to the group. You know, give me a couple of things that you want me to talk about and I will be happy to do a video message for the group. If you have read the book, I would love to hear your comments here on BNI Podcast.

Priscilla:

I think, Ivan, one of the more valuable things about it is there is too much content to be able to communicate much in the educational moment. It is too short. 

Ivan: 

It really is.

Priscilla:

I have tried to do that a little bit and you have to give it some background. That takes up half of your moment, you know, before you can just leap into the rest of the content. I think the book club idea is a great idea.

Ivan:

My preference is the book club but rather than do nothing, that would be a fallback. With the book club, I really hope that people would get some powerful information out of that and their chapter is going to be way more successful because when you actually wrap your head around what you want in life or what you want in your chapter, you re much more likely to get it when you understand what it is that you want. That’s very much what this book does. If this is successful and people have a lot of luck with it, we will do some book club discussion questions for some of my other books in the past and some in the future as well. 

Priscilla:

Okay. Great.

Ivan:

So that is it for today.  I just want to remind everyone that we are going to put a couple of links here, a link for you to get the book if you want to read it and a link so you can sign up to see the recorded webinars to help you with the book discussion. Thank you.

Priscilla:

Perfect. Okay thank you, Ivan. That’s great. So for more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNIPodcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Dr. Misner shares questions from “Who’s in Your Room?” to help you create your ideal BNI chapter.

title.rendered: Episode 601: Why Make All the Mistakes? (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 259.

Synopsis

Sometimes we make networking harder than it really is, and then we’re not as successful. To become a great networker, it helps to learn from other people’s successes and mistakes.

After an encounter with a Portuguese Man o’ War jellyfish, Dr. Misner was advised to apply vinegar and meat tenderizer to the spots. This solution seemed both too simple and too improbable, but everyone else he asked offered the same solution. By the time he was willing to apply it, he was having heart symptoms. When the EMTs came, what did they do? Applied meat tenderizer and vinegar.

So how does this apply to networking? If you assume you know better than the people who have the experience, you’re going to make a lot of mistakes, and some of them will be painful. Don’t dismiss ideas just because they seem too simple. Learn from the people who have the experience.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of BNI Podcast Episode 259 –

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi Priscilla. Doing great. I am still in Australia visiting BNI chapters. I love to visit BNI chapters and meet members. That’s where I am last week and this week.

Priscilla:
Great. What do you have to share with us?

Ivan:
I have a different podcast. Normally, my podcasts are very specific with how-tos and maybe interviews with somebody about how to do something. But today, I have a story. The story really applies to why make all the mistakes? To become a great networker, you learn from other people’s mistakes and successes. There really are tried and true networking techniques that are so simple that seem like they would just be ineffective. Many times, we try to evaluate them and improve upon them, complicate them- that’s often called feature creep, where we add stuff to an idea that is already pretty good working.

So there was an experience I had on this once while on vacation really kind of reminds me of how we try to make some things harder than they really are and we aren’t as successful as we want to be in the process. I’ll tell you the story and I’ll give you the moral to the story as I wrap up.

Quite a few years ago I was in Hawaii and was enjoying the ocean. Unbeknownst to me, the water was actually pretty thick with Portuguese man o war, which are highly poisonous. I felt this stinging sensation across my chest. I wiped my chest with my right wrist- that’s important- my right wrist and arm. I lifted my arm up out of the water and I look at these tentacles that were dripping off of my arm. I followed them with my eyes and about 8 feet away from my body was this Portuguese man o war jellyfish that was huge. I was kind of alarmed. I shooed the tentacles off my wrist and I swam to shore as quickly as I could.

I ran up to the first hotel employee that I could. It was some cabana boy who was serving drinks to people by the pool. I said, “Hey, I think I have been hit in the chest by a Portuguese man o war. What should I do?” He said, “Well, are you feeling any pressure in your chest?” I said, “No, no.” He saind, “Okay, here is what you need to do. Go to the market that is just off the lobby. Ask for some vinegar and meat tenderizer. You are going to want to spray the vinegar on your chest and then shake the meat tenderizer onto the same spot and rub it all around. And you’ll be just fine.”

I thought that was really bizarre advice. He was way too calm. I had just been hit by a Portuguese man o  war. He was way too calm and it seemed way too easy of a solution- and kind of goofy to be honest with you. I honestly thought this guy was doing his version of let’s goof on the stupid tourist. We can see if he actually does it. Maybe we’ll get pictures or something. I just thought he was crazy.

So I said thanks a lot, walked off and spotted a hotel employee not too far off. I kind of jogged over to him and I urgent told him that I had just been hit by a Portuguese man o war. I showed him and he went, “Yeah, that looks like a Portuguese man o war. Are you feeling any pressure in your chest?” Okay, just like the other guy asked. I said there was no pressure. He said, “Okay, what you want to do is go find the market. It’s right off the front desk. You want to find some vinegar and meat tenderizer. Spray your chest with the vinegar and rub in the meat tenderizer and you will feel a lot better. You’ll be just fine.”

I wasn’t at all reassured. I thought these guys had a staff meeting about how to goof on the tourist. I didn’t believe him, and I ran to the hotel and saw the hotel manager. I thought the hotel manager would give me the straight scoop. He is not going to give me the nonsense. I ran up to him and said that I had been hit by a Portuguese man o war. He said, “Yeah, that looks like a Portuguese man o war. Are you feeling any chest pains, any pressure in your chest?” I said no I wasn’t feeling any pressure in my chest. He said, “Okay. Fine. Just run down the hall. There is a little market. What you want to do is get some vinegar and meat tenderizer and spray it on your chest and then powder yourself up with the meat tenderizer and just rub it in.”

I finally thought he can’t be serious. Alright, I know this is a joke, but I’ll do it. So I reluctantly went down to the store and I just knew there were guys back there laughing at me about it. I figured they were looking on the cameras down the hall and they were all laughing. So I entered the small market off the lobby and I started searching for all the chargrilled products. While I am looking around, I start to get shortness of breath. Suddenly and really quickly, I began to experience this crushing weight on my chest. It was horrible. I started feeling like it was a heart attack.

I was thinking oh my goodness, I am having a coronary. As I stumbled out of the store, which was literally just a few feet down the hall. I stumbled down the hall, got to the front desk and literally dropped to my knees and went flat on my back clutching my chest. I was saying to call 911. They called 911, the paramedics were coming. No kidding, there was this little girl standing by me. I am in the middle of the lobby here, okay?

Imagine a lobby in Hawaii. There is this guy in a bathing suit, a beach porpoise in the lobby, clutching my chest. I am in agony. This little girl points at me and says, “Look, Mommy! Look at the man on the floor!” She was like, “Stay away from that man. This is what happens when you do drugs.” I was like, no not drugs, it was a Portuguese man o war.

The paramedics rush into the scene. They finally get there and are going to give me the real medical attention that I need. After they find out what happened, the paramedic opened up his lifesaving kit, and I knew that they were going to pull out a defibrillator. I was making my peace with God at that time and bracing myself for a big jolt. Instead, he pulled out- you may have guessed it- a spray bottle of vinegar and some Adolf’s meat tenderizer.

He then proceeded to spray the vinegar and sprinkle the meat tenderizer on my chest and thoroughly rub the mixture around. Within seconds, Priscilla, seconds, the excruciating pain began to subside. Within a couple of minutes, other than just a little pain where the stripes were- and remember, I used my wrist to wipe the tentacles off, so what happened was the tentacles were right on my wrist, so it mainlined the poison into me.

But after the vinegar and meat tenderizer, the pain was virtually all gone. It turns out that what I thought was a big barbecue joke on the tourist is a well-known cure for some jellyfish strikes. Not all but some. Meat tenderizer contains an enzyme, I think it’s pappin, which breaks down the toxin proteins and neutralizes them. And the vinegar is sort of the vehicle to put it into the bloodstream. The combination has a very, very quick impact.

Now, why am I telling this story? Here is how it applies to you as a listener at BNI: Thinking back on it, I am amazed at how many people gave me a solution to my problem, but I still had to learn the hard way. You know, I am sitting there thinking who is going to believe the cabana boy? What does he know, right? And the hotel employee, okay, maybe there is a pattern, but I have a doctoral degree. I am smart. These guys are just kidding with me, right? They’re joking. And the hotel manager, okay, well I admit at that point, there was no excuse. At that point, I needed to go on in.

I made one of the biggest mistakes that people in business make. I didn’t listen to the people who have the experience. I assumed that I knew better. The truth is I didn’t know better at all.
