There is nothing like experience. It beats education every day of the week. The only think better is a combination of education and experience- or at least a willingness to learn from other people’s experience.

There are so many basic referral marketing and networking techniques that any good business person knows to be effective. They don’t try to look for something more complicated or involved because they know from their own experience as well as the experience of others what works and what doesn’t work in business.

So if you are listening to this podcast, think back throughout your life. In the future, you may read things that seem too simple or ideas that you think you have heard of before. Don’t dismiss them, embrace them. Especially, if you are hearing this from people whom you know to be successful.

Opinions are like noses. Everybody has one. Everybody is going to give you an opinion, but take the opinion seriously for those people who are in the know, those people who have the experience. Even though their idea may be simple, they are often times- when you are talking about business networking, if they were easy everybody would do them but they don’t. Great networkers learn from other people’s failures and successes.

So get out that vinegar and meat tenderizer and learn from the masters. Learn from people in your chapter, directors, director consultants- hey, maybe even this podcast. Learn from other people so you don’t have to make the mistakes that other people have made. That’s my message for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Great. Perfect I love that story. I want to thank you for this podcast, Dr. Misner.

Ivan:
Thank you, Priscilla, and members listening to this podcast and directors: share this podcast with your members. It’s a lot of fun and it has a great message. Share this message with people that you work with week in and week out with BNI.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by networkingnow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: If you assume you know better than the people who have the experience, you’re going to make a lot of mistakes, and some of them will be painful. Don’t dismiss ideas just because they seem too simple.

title.rendered: Episode 600: Tell Me One Thing That’s Working

content.rendered:
Congratulations to Ivan and Priscilla on 600 episodes of the Official BNI Podcast!

Sometimes a BNI chapter can get so obsessed with what isn’t working that they lose sight of what is working. This is especially true when the chapter is struggling.

If you’re on the leadership team and your chapter is struggling, ask every member to tell you one thing that is working for them. Write the answers on a whiteboard or flip chart.

Almost every time Dr. Misner asked this question in a chapter, he got answers like these.

  • I’m building great relationships.
  • It’s brought out the best in me.
  • I’m a better speaker than I was before BNI.
  • It’s increased my knowledge of other people’s businesses.
  • I’ve learned how to listen more proactively.
  • It gives me a support group.
  • It helps me set marketing goals.

When you conduct this exercise, every member in the chapter can see that BNI works in many ways beyond referrals. Encourage members to talk to each other about what’s working for them.

The more parts of the BNI program your chapter has working, the better the chapter will be. If you focus on problems, you become an expert on problems. If you focus on solutions, you become an expert on solutions. (Check out Mark McKergow’s book The Solutions Focus for more on this topic.)

Share one thing that’s working for you in the comments below.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan! How are you and did you know this is our 600th podcast?

Ivan:
I just heard you say that. Episode 600. That is really exciting. We have been doing this for more than a decade. Well over a decade now. I have to be honest. When I did the first podcast I wasn’t completely certain that this would work, that you know, doing a weekly podcast would be something that people are interested in. Now we have tens of thousands of listeners every week. It is really an honor to be doing this podcast with you for so long, Priscilla. 

Priscilla: 
Thank you, Ivan. Well, I feel exactly the same, so that’s great.

Ivan:
So this is a fun topic and I thought I would pull out of my vault a topic that I have talked about for many years when I went to individual chapters and did presentations at individual chapters. I called it the “Is it working?” Presentation but as we go through this, I’ll tell you why we changed the title. Because I am suggesting that you start with “tell me one thing that is working” rather “than is it working?” Let me give you the history to this.

I would visit chapters and I would see chapters so obsessed with what isn’t working that they couldn’t think about what was working. One of the best ways to do a mindset reset – this is sort of organizational behavior 101 – is to start with the most frustration and lead to where there can be success in a discussion. 

I used to go to chapters many years ago, and I would just stand up and ask the chapter – particularly chapters that were struggling. I would say to the chapter, I would shrug my shoulders and say, “Let me ask you a question. Is it working?” And they would kind of look at me for a moment. I would say, “It’s okay. You can be honest. Is it working?” Inevitably, someone would say, “No. No. It’s just not working.”

I would say, “Okay, Great. Thank you for being honest. So do me a favor,” and I always had a flip chart with me when I did this exercise.  I am going to recommend that if you are listening to this podcast, especially if you are on the Leadership Team or Education Coordinator, I would urge you to try this exercise, but try it starting with this phrase. Don’t ask “Is it working?”  Because it is scary and you don’t know how- I dealt with it so many times I knew how to respond to it. I recommend you start with this question: “Tell me one thing about BNI that is working for you. Tell me one thing. I am going to go around the room and I would like you to share one thing that is working.”

Now, when I did this exercise, I started with, “Is it working?“ I don’t recommend you, the listener, do that. Imagine starting with “Is it working?” and having someone say, “No,” and then going to this question and – I would say it to that one and start with that one person who said no. I said, “Fair enough. Can you just share with me one thing that you believe is working for you?” And they would give it. It often was something like “Relationships. I am building great relationships with other quality business professionals. That is working for me.”

Great. And I would write it on the flip chart. Then I would say.to someone else now, “Give me something that is working for you” and I would get this amazing list. I wrote this down years ago. Here are the kinds of things I would get: “Referrals. I am getting business.“ “It has brought out the best in me.” Sometimes people would say, “It has brought out the best. I am a better business person because of this.” Here is one I got almost every time: “My presentations have gotten better. I am a better speaker than I was before I started BNI.” Man, I heard that one, in particular, a lot. “It has increased my knowledge of other people’s businesses.”

Now, when you do this and you say – I am suggesting that if you are listening to this podcast and you are in a leadership role, you ask the question, “Tell me. One thing that is working.” and then write each of these down on a flip chart. You will hear things like, “It has increased my knowledge of other people’s businesses.” “It is like having 30 business consultants working for me.” “I have made great connections in the community.” “I have improved my business skills.” “I have learned how to listen more proactively.” These are actual comments that I would get, Priscilla. “My meeting day – it’s the best day of the week.”  I love that one and I heard it more than once. “This day of the week is the best day of the week for me because I start my morning out in a real positive way.” Here is another one: “It helps me set marketing goals.”  “It gives me a support group to get feedback from.” 

So I would write this list down, and by the way, every time I would do this, “Is it working?” and I would ask them to give me one thing, I would get almost all of these, if not all of them. And then I would ask the group, “This is more than just referrals, don’t you think?”

There are a lot of benefits to being in BNI. So don’t do “It is working?” but say “Tell me one thing that is working.” And watch as the chapter gives you a list very much like this. Then point out to the chapter that BNI works and it works in many ways, including referrals but not exclusive to everything else. Then I would end the discussion by saying, “If you picked one or two or three of these and you think these are working for you, talk to some of the other people about why the other elements are working for them. Because I am here to tell you, that if you as a chapter, were able to get for each and every member the majority of the things that I just asked about – building relationships, getting referrals, presentations getting better, more connections, increased business skills, better listener, best day of the week, a good start for my business. If more and more of your chapter members are getting the majority of these, your chapter will rock the house. Your chapter would be one of the best chapters in the organization.

What was amazing was when I did this presentation, – and I did the “Is it working?” which I don’t recommend you do, because it is a little bit like stepping off a cliff when you do that. It is a little scary. But when I did this, 100 percent of the time the chapter was like, “Yeah, okay, I never thought of it from that perspective, and from that perspective, it is working because all of us are getting so many different things in addition to referrals.”

Now, referrals are important and I talk a lot about referrals in my podcasts, but I think it was time – and this is perfect for the 600th episode of BNI to tell me one thing that is working. So I would love for you if you are listening to this podcast, to do two things: One, I have already suggested it. Do this at your chapter, “Tell me one thing that is working.” Get your own list.
And Two, I would love for you to share with me right now, in the comments section, one thing that is working for you in BNI. One thing that is working for you in BNI.

I have one or two other comments to close, but Priscilla, what do you think of this concept?

Priscilla:
You know, I have always felt that way about BNI because I have kind of an unusual business. I don’t get a ton of referrals but I do benefit from all those other ways. Being part of that business community, learning all of those skills and listening to everybody’s information every week, and also having the resources in the group that I can then refer to and recommend to my other networks, I think, is so valuable. But do you ever ask them what isn’t working or is that too dangerous?

Ivan:
You know what, listen, when I went to visit the chapters – now I do regional events, but when I visited chapters, I didn’t have to ask the question. When people weren’t happy, they were telling me. They were telling me. Here is the problem with just focusing on the challenges:  If all you focus on are problems, you become an expert at problems. but if you focus on solutions, you become an expert at solutions. I know I have recommended this book in the past on BNI Podcast, but it is called “Solutions Focus” by Dr. Mark McKergow. It is probably the best book I have ever read on why it is important to understand what your challenges are.

You can’t hide from challenges. That is a bad idea. But if all you do is obsess over the problems, you just become a really fantastic expert at problems. What I am afraid I see people do in life – not just in BNI but in life in general – is they obsess over the problems and they don’t focus on solutions. So when I go into a chapter, I know there are problems. There are always problems. Even the best chapters in the world have problems. The best businesses in the world have problems. I know they know what those are but what I am looking to do is to do a mindset reset, like I started the podcast with, to reset the mindset to focus not just on the problems but the solutions to the problems. The best way to start focusing on the solutions is to look at what is working. When you know what is working- this is straight out of the “Solutions Focus” book. When you know what is working, then focus more on those things. When you are focusing on those things, it’s magic. The problems dissipate. 

I urge you to read that book and I urge you to try this out, but I urge you to try this out saying, “Tell me one thing that is working.” Create the list and get everyone to focus on as much as possible, everyone improving on those things. Whatever problems you have, I promise you, you will have less of them by focusing on the solutions. That is it for today, Priscilla. 

Priscilla:
That was great. Okay, thank you so much. Good information. For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Ask each member of your chapter to tell you one thing that’s working for them to find out how far beyond referrals the benefits of BNI go.

title.rendered: Episode 599: Are you Asking for Pennies?

content.rendered:
Mark Appelbaum, regional growth coordinator of BNI Utah North, joins Dr. Misner on the podcast this week to help you be more specific in your referral requests.

Your fellow BNI members and other referral partners are like genies: they interpret your requests literally. Ask for “money” and you might get a penny. It’s money, but it’s probably not what you were thinking of.

If possible, ask for referrals to specific people by name. A little research will get you the name of the person you need to talk to at the company you want to work with. Once you know, ask your referral partners for an introduction.

Ask yourself, “Who are the people who are talking to the people that you want to be talking to?” Find out who they are and ask for introductions to them.

Set up Big Hairy Audacious Goals when it comes to referrals and be specific when you ask.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Priscilla: 

Hello everybodyand welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today with the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr Ivan  Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:

Hi, Priscilla. I am doing fantastic. I am at my home office. i am talking to you from my home office in Austin, TX. i have a guest on this week’s podcast who has been on before, Mark Applebaum. Mark has been a BNI member for 14 years. He has been a Director for 4 years. He has been married to his wife, Tiffany, for almost 18 years. They have two children, Ryan and Jenna. Mark has recently become the Director of Marketing for a local law firm in Utah. He is on today – I love the title of this and I love the  topic – are you asking for pennies, which is about being specific with your asks. 

So Mark, what does it mean, asking for pennies? Explain that.

Mark:

Thank, Ivan and thank you for having me on again. My intention for today’s podcast is to really help members fine tune what they are already doing each week in the hopes that they take the results to the next level. What that means is the reason why it is called asking for pennies is because when it comes to making more money, which is one of the big reasons members join BNI, people in general will often say, “I want more money.”

If you were to say those words, Ivan or Priscilla, I could give you a penny and I would have fulfilled on your ask. Now, was a penny what you wanted? Probably not, but it is what you asked for. You asked for more money. I gave you more money, but it’s probably not what you really wanted. 

One of the great things about BNI is every week, we members get to ask for exactly who and what we want. It kind of a weekly genie in a bottle and our chapter members are the genie fulfilling on the ask. The more specific we are with our ask, the washer we make it for the genie or chapter to give us what we want. 

For instance, if you are a home inspector, more times than not, home inspectors want to be introduced to top producing real estate agents. But if they walked in each week and said, “Um, a good referral for me would be a top producing real estate agent,” it most likely won’t produce the results they want. But if all they did was Google a top real estate agent and walk in. Each week with a specific name, sure, somebody may not know that individual, but somebody my know somebody from that real estate office. What would it be like if that member walked out each week with that exact person, that top producing agent? It would take their business to a whole another level. Sure, they would have to do the work and build the relationships with agents, but getting their foot in the door with that warm referral would make such a big difference, you know.

Ivan:

I’m confused for a second, Mark, because you said Google that person. You’re not suggesting that they give a referral through a Google connection, are you?

Mark:

No, no, no, no. The Google reference was more for the home inspector, Iike, if they couldn’t think of a real estate agent that came to mind, for them to just google a real estate agent in the area. Usually if they were to put “top producing agent in Salt Lake City” let’s say, just the city I am in, then walk into their BNI meeting and say, ‘This is the person that I want to be introduced to. Who knows that person?”

Ivan:

Got it. Got it. Now I understand, and yes, I couldn’t agree more with that approach. Go ahead.

Mark:

Using another example with a different industry, I am going to use commercial insurance agent. Most commercial agents’, I’m sure, dream ideal client would be larger companies, 50 employees or more or 500, whatever the number would be perfect for them, instead of just like a one or two man show. I know here in Utah – I am guessing most states or cities have this resource. Here in Utah, we have Utah Business Magazine which comes out with a book of lists in a variety of categories from hotels to marketing to legal, I mean, you could pretty much pick from whatever you want the top companies in your state or in your city. They usually include how much revenue, how many employees they have, who the contact is at that company. If all they did each week was pick one of those companies each week and say, “That’s who I want. That would be a dream referral for me,” instead of just walking in, “I’m looking for companies that are 50 employees or more.” It doesn’t serve us.

Again, if we are the genie in a bottle, the more specific we can get- as you always say, Ivan, “Specific is terrific.” The more specific we get, the better it is for our members to fulfill on that ask.

Ivan:

I agree with you completely, Mark. Let me tell you a quick story that I think fits what you are talking about. First of all, it is counter-intuitive to most members to be crazy specific. Instead, what they try to do is be very broad, “I do everything. I can do all these things.” It is counter-intuitive. What you are saying I believe is absolutely correct.

One of the best examples of this is when I was showing how this worked to be incredibly specific, I went around the room of a chapter and I asked them to give the name of someone they were looking for, a specific person they were looking for – which is exactly what you are talking about. Here is where it was hysterical: The man’s wife was in the room, and she stood up and said, “I know that person. Why didn’t you ask me about it?” We all just laughed because it is just amazing who who may know what person in the room but if you have never asked, it doesn’t get fulfilled. So I agree completely. 

What are some ideas that you suggest for members who are more, you know, B to C?

Mark:

So, you know, whether it be chiropractors or massage therapists, anyone who is dealing more with the public – insurance, you can’t just Google “Joe Smith” or whoever it is out there, but an approach that I live to use or that I also like to share with my chapters that I am Director over would be the phrase, “Who are the people who are talking to the people that you want to be talking to?” I will say that again. Who are the people who are talking to the people that you want to be talking to? So if you are – the example that I usually use is a roofer, somebody who is putting new roofs or repairing roofs on homes. Often when I ask the roofers in the chapters, “Would you rather do the home that is $150,000 or would you rather do a million dollar home?” Yes, they will do both, but they typically say they would rather do the million dollar house. A couple reasons why is, one, there is more profit, typically, in the larger home and two, that million dollar home owner know other other million dollar home owners, too. So there is more of an opportunity.

So then I take it to the next level. Okay, what are the characteristics of the million dollar homeowner, somebody who usually owns that home? Usually, they are a business owner, they drive nicer cars. They go on on nicer vacations. They wear nicer clothes. Okay, so now you break down a little bit more. Okay, so a travel agent, a custom clothing person or a Haberdasher or a Lexus, BMW or Mercedes sales person – so now if each week, they just picked each of those categories, a different travel agent in the area. A different high end car sales person in the area. And walked in and said, “I want to be introduced to that car sales person because that car sales person, once I build a relationship up with them, will then introduce me to the people I want to be talking to.” 

Again for the. Business consumer, whether it be chiropractors or massage therapists, you know, anyone dealing with health and wellness, whether it be physical therapists, whether it be doctors, whether it be alternative doctors – those are the people who are talking to the people they want or be talking to. So the more they can research those companies, those other businesses in their area and ask for introductions to them, they will lead them to the people that they want to be talking to in most cases.

Ivan:

Well, I think asking for the introduction is key. You can’t be vague. “I need the manager of a hotel” is just too vague. What hotel? What is the person’s name? You are absolutely correct. Again, it is totally counter-intuitive.

I will tell you one other real quick story with the same result. i was in another group where we were getting laser specific. This person said, “I am trying to meet his individual at this company.” She was really specific. Had the name, the company, everything. The person who sponsored her in the chapter – first, she had been trying to get through to the individual, but she couldn’t get past the receptionist, couldn’t get past the assistant, to talk to her. The person who sponsored this person into the chapter said, “Sallie, that is my sister in law. I have her mobile phone number.” It was really funny because had I not done that exercise in the chapter, she may have never asked. Sallie even said to her, “Why didn’t you tell me that?” Of course, what you do think Sallie said, “You never asked.” So you have got to ask. Absolutely right. 

Do you have any closing thoughts on this topic?

Mark:

Really, the closing thought – and I hear you saying this also, Ivan – is who is your BHAG? Who is your big, hairy, audacious goal? If you got to meet or start doing business with this person or this company, what would that be like and how would it be like if you landed that? So if you have been asking for pennies, what could happen when you start asking for exactly what you want? That is really what I want to leave members with.

Ivan:

Yeah, you know, you may not hear people – they are obviously not asking for pennies, but they are asking for business, and I think it is the same concept. You may get business, but is it the same business that you want? If you are asking for pennies worth of business, then that is what you are going to get. So, Mark, this is a great spin on a concept that I think is so critical for the success of BNI members, and I really appreciate you taking the time to do this podcast with me today.

Mark: 

Thank you Ivan for the opportunity. 

Ivan:

That is Mark Applebaum with BNI Utah. Back to you, Priscilla. 

Priscilla:

I think that is it for this week. Thank you so much for the great. information. For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice,as we look forward to having you. Join us again net week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast. 


ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Generic requests yield disappointing results. If you want better referrals, ask for introductions to specific people by name.

title.rendered: Episode 598: The One Rule to Work-Life Balance

content.rendered:
Today’s podcast is based on Dr. Misner’s new book, Who’s in Your Room?

There is no one rule to achieving balance in your life, but there is one truth about creating harmony: the quality of your life depends on the people in your life.

If everyone who came into your life was going to be there forever, would you be more selective about the people you let into your life?

Anyone who is still in your head is still in your room. You need to learn how to screen entry into your room (Episode 594).

To do that, you need to know what your values are. Only people whose values are congruent with yours should be invited in. If you have trouble identifying your values, you can start by listing your deal-breakers, or use the free values worksheet from the Who’s in Your Room website.

Download Free Values Worksheet

Once you’ve identified your values and trained your “doorman,” there are two techniques for removing toxic people from your life.

  1. Benign Neglect. Gradually reduce contact and interaction with them.
  2. Homeopathic Doses. Minimize the amount of time you spend with them by structuring your interactions in very small doses.

What about family members and other people who are in the room when you arrive? You might need to have the people in your life, but you don’t have to have their baggage. Set clear boundaries around toxic behavior, and enforce them.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 598

Priscilla: 

Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today with the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr Ivan  Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:

Hi Priscilla. I am in Austin, Texas and I am waiting to possibly do jury duty. So we are doing this recording. I have never actually done the jury duty before. I have been there but I haven’t actually been called, so let’s see what happens. It should be fun. 

Priscilla:

Yeah. It should be interesting. What are you going to teach us today?

Ivan:

So I am going to share some content with you today. I have had a lot of members asking me to share more material from my book Who’s in Your Room? The Secret to Creating Your Best Life. I am going to share some material from the book today. So Priscilla, and if you are listening, would you like to know the one rule to creating balance in your life?

Priscilla:

Absolutely. I really need that, Ivan. 

Ivan:

So here it is. There is no one rule. Here is the good news. It’s a recipe. It’s a combination of things that help you achieve the life you desire because there is one truth –  although there is not one rule, there is one truth and that truth is that the quality of your life depends on the people in your life. That is also a truth in a BNI chapter. The quality of your chapter depends on the people in your chapter.

So let me just say a summary of the book for those of you who haven’t heard a previously podcast where I talk about the book. Imagine you live your life in one room and that one room has only one door. And that one door is an enter only door. No exit. Whoever comes through that door will be in your life and your room forever. They never get out. Luckily, this is a metaphor. But let’s assume for a moment it’s true. If so, would you if you. Are listing to this, be more selective about the people you let into your life?

Almost everyone I have ever talked to has said, “Oh yeah. Definitely.” So the question then is why aren’t we more selective? In fact, I would argue that the room principle is more than a metaphor. Think for a moment- and we are going to get to the balance question in just a second. But think for a moment about one one who was once in your life.  Priscilla, I want you to think of someone, too, who was once in your life but they are no longer part of your life. But they were somebody who was for you toxic. I want you to think of their name and how they created chaos in your life. Maybe they were angry or toxic as I said or just difficult to be around. Priscilla, do you have someone in mind?

Priscilla:

Yeah definitely. 

Ivan: 

You don’t have to share it. That is the good news. So here is the deal. If you are listening to this, I want you to think of somebody who was in your life. They are not in your life anymore, but man, you can think about the situation and what they did. Here is the deal. If they are still in your head, they are still in your room- because every decision you make in the future for the rest of your life will be based in part on some past experiences you had with this person. 

I interviewed neuroscientist Dr. Daniel Amen for the book and he said “significant input that is received deep into your brain triggers neural activity that cannot simple be erased.” In other words, their fingerprints are all over your brain. This means that if you want to create a life of balance or what I call in the book harmony, you need to learn how to screen entry into your room. If you want to look into a couple of different podcasts that I have done on this topic, go to podcast 594, which talks about the doorman principle, which I am about to speak briefly about, and for work-life balance, or harmony, go to podcast 434. These are two podcasts related to this one. Listen to this one and then go listen to those.

594 talks about the doorman principle. The doorman is somebody who guards the entry into your room and into your life. This is your conscious and subconscious mind,  process of thought and feelings to help you determine whether your door should remain closed or be open to allow someone entry into your life. 

The first thing that you need to do is train your doorman on your values. This is important because you want to allow people into your life who have values that are resonate with yours. Here is where we start getting into the balance and harmony.  They don’t have to be the same values, but they can’t be dissonant or incongruent with yours. 

So when I ask people to name their top five to seven values, you can generally hear crickets. They are like, well, let me think, I don’t know. Here is a great technique to begin the training. I recommend that you start, instead of think about your values, start with your deal breakers. Deal breakers are things that you just cannot tolerate in somebody else. For me, it was somebody dripping in drama. There is just drama everywhere. 

Priscilla, can you think of a deal breaker in your life? Somebody you just don’t want around you?

Priscilla:

I can.

Ivan:

What is the theme? What is it about them that is a deal breaker?

Priscilla:

High narcissism.

Ivan:

Narcissism. That is a great deal breaker. One of the things that you can do to start thinking about your values is start thinking about the deal breakers. These behaviors are behaviors of people in your room who are incongruent with your values. These are things that you refuse to allow into your room and into your life from now on. It is a line in the sand. 

There may be things like being toxic or exhibiting excess drama, being a narcissist, not being truthful, not being positive – not being positive, that’s a real important one in BNI. So these are things you just can’t stand to be around. You start understanding these, it’s easier to move forward towards the things that you want around you. In order to begin the process, I am going to include a link here to a free download to help you determine your values because you can’t have balance and harmony until you really understand your values. The link is WIYRthebook.com/downloads. That will be a hot link right here in the podcast.

So understanding your values and helping train the doorman will help you screen all the future people who try to gain entry into your room? But what do you do with some of those negative or toxic people that you already have there and you want to start creating that life that feels more harmonious or in balance? I am going to give you two techniques that I think are really powerful.

The first is benign neglect. This is where you gradually reduce contact and interaction with someone, assuming you don’t want to burn bridges, and I don’t like burning bridges with people. But you want to remove yourself from the relationship, a gradual disentanglement over a period of time is very effective. This works even when you don’t want it to. Think about friends that you had many years ago that you just lost touch with. You liked them. They were good people, but you just lost touch with them. Through benign neglect, you have lost that friendship. This generally happens through unplanned benign neglect. Now imagine achieving this with a plan, that you have a strategy and a plan for benign neglect in a particular relationship. 

The second techniques goes hand in hand with benign neglect. That is what we call homeopathic doses. A homeopathic does is the minimum does necessary to treat a problem. In this case, it is about dealing with people in your room by structuring your interaction with them in very small doses. A good example would be if you are traveling to a town where you have somebody that you really want to begin creating benign neglect. They are toxic or they are no longer a good fit in your life and your relationship. Instead of letting them know a month in advance that you are coming into town, email them the night before and let them know, “Hey.  I am going to be in town tomorrow. I am free between 1:00 and 1:30 if you want to get together.” 

Don’t you like that? Then you are limiting the amount of time. Heck, you might get lucky and they might not even read the email until after 1:00 tomorrow. Homeopathic doses is a powerful concept that was given to us by a friend of ours, Ariel Ford, and she suggested this is terms of relationships.

If you want to create the life of your dreams, there are many things – in a couple of the other podcasts, I talk about harmony vs. balance, but one of them is the people you surround yourself with. It makes a huge difference in creating the life of your dreams. People affect your happiness, especially if you let them. The secret to not letting them is to curate the room you desire because the quality of your life depends on the people in your life. That is the theme for Who Is in Your Room. I invite people to pick up a copy of the book. We will make sure there is a link to it here as well. 

This is a game changer for people. I wish I had this book. I wish somebody had written this book when I was 17 because even though I have had, I think, a great life, I would have made different choices throughout my life had I had something like this. And this fits BNI so beautifully because the quality of your chapter depends on the people in your chapter. 

I think we still have about a minute or so, Priscilla. Do you have any thoughts on this topic?

Priscilla:

You know, sometimes, Ivan, you are given the people in your life like I am thinking if you are born into a certain family. You don’t have that much choice, you know. So how do you deal with that?

Ivan:

That is a great question. Here is another tidbit. Let me frame it in your mind. They may be in your life, but their baggage does not have to be. They may be in your life but their baggage does not have to be. That means you are clear in communicating with them. I will give you an example and this is right out of the book. Rick, one of my co-authors, when his mother was alive, he used to call her every week on Sunday and he would have an hour of so conversation with her. He always dreaded those callers because she had a lot of emotional issues, according to Rick, and she was very toxic. She would talk. Really badly about other people. He hated those calls. So one day he said to her, “Mom, I love you very much, and I really want to talk to you every week, but let me tell you, from now on, if you start getting toxic about other people and badmouthing other people, I am going to say to you, ‘Mom, I love you. I will call you next week at the same time. I look forward to talking to you. Bye,’ and I am going to hang up.”

He said he did it for about three weeks and he had to hang up. After that for the rest of their life together, they had wonderful conversations every week, and he felt like their relationship ended on such a positive note when she passed on. He was very clear: You are in my life, but your baggage has to stay out. You are in my room, but the baggage has to stay out. That is the answer to your question, Priscilla. 

Priscilla:

Yeah, that sounds good. Okay, that is helpful.

Ivan:

Well good. Listen, I will talk more about content from the book. If you are interested in hearing me talk about more material from the book, please leave a comment. If you have some other techniques on this strategy that I just explained, feel free to leave a comment. I read all of the comments that get posted up on BNIpodcast.com. Over to you, Priscilla. 

Priscilla:

Okay that is great. Thank you so much. I think that is it for this week. For more of Ivan’s content, you can go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: To create harmony in your life, remember that the quality of your life depends on the people in your life.

title.rendered: Episode 597: Networking Wind Sprints (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 444.

Synopsis

Dr. Misner shares a story from his high school football days. When the team members complained about having to do wind sprints and conditioning exercises at practice instead of playing ball, their coach took them to see the LA Rams practice. Imagine their surprise when they saw that the pros were doing the same conditioning exercises their coach had inflicted on them.

If you do not learn to execute the fundamentals flawlessly, if you do not engage in the conditioning necessary, you will never be a champion on or off the field.

BNI meetings are a place to practice the fundamentals of networking until you can execute them flawlessly. If you’re going to be successful in BNI, you need to put effort into those fundamentals.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcript of Episode 444 –

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hey. This week I am in Austin, TX and we are preparing for our first Executive Management meeting of BNI top management coming to Austin. So I am in my new hometown this week, but we are doing a big meeting for BNI.

Priscilla:
That’s great. Tell me a little bit about these networking wind sprints. What is that about?

Ivan:
What do wind sprints and networking have to do with each other, right? Wind sprints are when people get down in three point stance- I first saw it in American style football. You know, the coach would blow a whistle and you would run like a bat out of heck for 10 yards and back or 50 yards and back. Whatever. You know, they are running quickly in short spurts.

It’s part of the fundamentals of football, and I will tie it into BNI and into networking in just a minute. I want to tell a story. I learned a really important lesson about the fundamentals of success when I was playing football as a high school kid a long, long time ago.

We had a pretty good team my junior year of high school. I was, you know, 16 years old when I started the year. Most of the other team members were juniors. The following year, the team had mostly seniors, and there were some pretty high expectations for the season, you know, because it was going to be mostly seniors and they would be a little more mature. A situation like that can maybe- you probably haven’t ever seen this in teenagers, Priscilla, but it maybe made us a little bit over-confident.

When the season started, we thought we were really experienced. In football, they have this brutal rite of passage that s called Hell Week. It is called Hell Week for a really good reason. The conditioning that the team is put through is pure hell. The team does very little other than drills and exercises. They have to do isometric exercises, wind sprints, short distance sprints as fast as you can run, hitting the bags, tacking dummies, running plays, hitting the ground. Did I mention wind sprints?

Priscilla:
You did.

Ivan:
Wind sprints, running up and down stadium steps, hitting the sled. They have these big, giant sleds, and they are made of iron, Priscilla. They aren’t made of aluminum. They are made of iron. Then they take the two fattest coaches known to mankind. They stand on it and you have to hit it and knock them back. And did I mention wind sprints? So many wind sprints! Lots and lots of wind sprints.

We were doing these drills for days. We never even saw the football. I saw the football when the coach threw it at my backside for not running fast enough. Otherwise, we didn’t get to touch the ball. We weren’t doing plays.

We knew we were a team and we didn’t need to go through all this nonsense. We just wanted to play ball and not run around the field and hit bags. So we had this bright idea to maybe have a chat with the coach. Tell the coach we didn’t want to do wind sprints anymore. We didn’t want to do conditioning. We just wanted to play ball.

Now, from my experience, coaches generally have two answers for anything they don’t like. The first is “no”. The second is “What part of no don’t you understand?”

When we went to the coach and told him we don’t want to do it anymore, imagine our surprise when he said, “Okay. Alright. I will make you a deal. You get here an hour early tomorrow. We are going to take a little bus ride. After the bus ride, I just want to say a few words. If you want to drop the conditioning program after that, you can do it.”

We were like, “Are you kidding? Roadtrip? No wind sprints? We’re in, coach. We’ll go.”

So we were all in early the next day. We rode this bus down to a state university in southern California where I grew up. At the time, the university was one of the practice fields for the Los Angeles Rams, what was at that time the Los Angeles Rams. We realized we were going to see the Rams practice live. We were stoked. We were in awe.

It is one thing to be in a football stadium looking down on the field. But is a completely different thing to be on the field. We were on the field. It just looked so much bigger. Of course, the players- Priscilla, have you ever seen a professional football player in full regalia from three feet away from you?

Priscilla:
No, but they are so huge.

Ivan:
These guys are huge. For those of you listening to the podcast, look around the door room. Look at the door frame for the door, okay? Imagine a man like that with a big helmet, hair in his teeth, one eye in the middle of his head. These guys were gigantic. They were some of the amazing players. Rosey Grier, Merlin Olsen, Jack Youngblood. These were our heroes. These were incredible, incredible players.

We watched as our heroes stepped out onto the field. And were were in total awe as they lumbered out to the grass and for the next two hours did wind-sprints. They tackled dummies, hit bags, ran in place, hit the sled and of course did lots and lots of wind sprints.

The truly amazing thing about this is they weren’t just doing the same conditioning exercise that we were doing. They were doing the exact same conditioning exercises in the exact same order that we were doing them in. Turns out that our high school head coach was college roommates with one of the assistant coaches for the Rams. He gave our coach the conditioning book for the Rams. That was basically what we were doing.

Granted, it was probably Rams Light, but it was the same kind of program. So we returned to campus and the coach took us out, put us in a big semicircle, and the coach looked at us and said, “Boys, it doesn’t really matter if you are talking about Pop Warner football (which is the little kiddie football), high school football, professional football or life. If you do not learn to execute the fundamentals flawlessly, if you do not engage in the conditioning necessary, you will never be a champion on or off the field. It doesn’t matter if we are talking about football, school, work. When you leave this school and go on to college, you have to learn the fundamentals and do the drills that will make you successful in your life. When you go on to your professional careers, you will see that there are fundamentals there that you have to learn in order to be champion in that profession. Only those of you who are willing to develop the physical and mental conditioning necessary to execute the fundamentals will ever succeed. This is something you have to choose. I can’t choose it for you, so you have to decide what kind of team you will be.”

I will never forget he said, “I can’t push a noodle. I can’t make someone do something they don’t want to do. You have to make that commitment yourself. So, I am going to step into my office. You guys talk and decide. Take a vote and decide what you want to do.”

And he walked away. What do you think we voted on, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
I am pretty sure you decided to do wind sprints.

Ivan:
Yeah. It took a couple of seconds. Man, he had us all worked up. He had us all lathered. We were like “Yeah, wind sprints!” I hate wind sprints. “Yeah wind sprints! We’ve got to do wind sprints!”

What a great lesson!. We had a great season, but more importantly, we learned a great life lesson. Success comes to those who execute the fundamentals flawlessly, to those who are willing to do the conditioning necessary to be successful. It only comes to those who drill and learn and drill some more. The secret to success without hard work is still a secret.

That is where networking comes in to wind sprints. I go to chapter meetings and I watch the meeting. You know, Priscilla, let’s see if we can pick something out of a meeting. If you had to pick one thing that is repetitive and done at every meeting, what would it be? That everybody does?

Priscilla:
The introductions, for example.

Ivan:
Yeah, the introductions, I would completely agree. So I see people doing the introductions and they are jogging through their wind sprint, through their introduction. They are thinking about what to say while the person before them is speaking. Or they just make it up on the fly, and you can see them just jogging through the process.

On the other hand, I have seen people who come in prepared. They have a mini lesson plan that they want to teach someone about what it is that they do and how they can be referred. That preparation makes all the difference in the world. That is the conditioning that I am talking about. That’s the wind sprints of networking in BNI. It’s doing the hard work necessary and not just jogging through the process. That is how you get successful in a BNI program.

Priscilla:
That makes sense. That’s good.

Ivan:
Yeah, so look, we all have a choice. We can do the conditioning and I am not just talking about sports now. We can do the conditioning necessary or we can just jog through the process. I am here to tell you, having run BNI now for more than 31 years, that the little bit of extra hard work yields substantially greater results over and over and over again. Well, that is everything that I have got for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, excellent. Thank you, Dr. Misner. Well, I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com. It’s the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. Thanks for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: If you do not learn to execute the fundamentals flawlessly, if you do not engage in the conditioning necessary, you will never be a champion on or off the field.

title.rendered: Episode 596: I Want Referrals, and I Want Them Now!

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Strong referral relationships are like close personal friendships. It takes about 50 hours of interaction to move a person from “acquaintance” to “casual friend.” It takes a total of 90 hours to become “real friends,” and 200 hours to become close friends.

When BNI members reach the 90-hour mark, they start receiving more referrals. When those same individuals cross the 200-hour mark, they generate an average of five times as many referrals as they did in the first year.

The time-confidence curve illustrates the way your fellow networkers need to gain confidence that referring people to you will not hurt their reputations.

Here are four questions to ask yourself to gain perspective on your efforts and results. Keep asking them until the answers become obvious.

  1. Am I being realistic about the amount of time it will take in my profession to gain the critical level of confidence I need?
  2. Am I regularly making stimulating, educational presentations to my fellow networkers about the value I provide my clients.
  3. Am I doing business with others in my network so I can give them dynamic testimonials and steer business to them so they will return the favor?
  4. Am I meeting regularly with my networking colleagues to learn about their businesses so I can confidently refer my contacts to them?

It takes time to build friendships. The best way to speed up the process is to spend time in the process.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Priscilla: 
Hello and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today with the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr Ivan  Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla. I have what I think is a fun topic: I want referrals and I want them now! I love the way you said it with an exclamation point. Now! I run into people and they are almost desperate for referrals. Desperation is not referable. I am sure this isn’t for every listener. When you hear this, I am not talking about you. I am talking about those people who come across desperate for referrals.

So you want referrals and you want them now. Well, you want referrals and you want them now. You can’t have them unless you have built meaningful relationships with your referral partners. 

Like I said, I am often asked “How long does it take for people to receive referrals from their network? How do we speed this process up?” That is the one that I love. “How do you speed this process up?”

Building a referral-based business is all about building a powerful personal network. If your network is a mile wide and an inch deep, you will never get any kind of referrals that make a difference in your business. This means that you have to go deep in building a number of strong relationships. From my experience, strong referral relationships are a lot like building close personal friendships. Now, Facebook has redefined what a friend is, but I am taking about truly close friendships.

In a study that I recently read that was published in 2018 by the Journal of Social and Personal Relationships, it was found that it takes about 50 hours of interaction to move from being an acquaintance to becoming a casual friend. It takes a total of 90 hours to become what they called real friends and – get this, Priscilla – a total of 200 hours to become close friends. 

Priscilla:
Hmmm, that’s a lot.

Ivan:
That’s a lot. And according to this study, friendship status was examined as a function of hours together, shared activities and regular conversation. Now, this fits the BNI experience so well because people who are in BNI for a year, that is when things oftentimes start clicking for them, and at two years, they are really, really strong and generating on average more referrals. That is that 200 hour mark in that second year. So when I read this, I was like oh my goodness, they could have written this about BNI. So you know, when it comes to how long does it take to build a close relationship, it is somewhere between that 90 and 200 hours. That sounds right to me. This matches up perfectly with what I have seen in BNI.

When BNI members hit that 90-hour mark, they almost always begin receiving more and more referrals. Based on an independent study published in 2012 for BNI, when those same individuals cross the 200 mark, they generate an average of five times the number of referrals they did in the first years. Yes, you heard that right: more than 500% more referrals when they built a strong relationship with their referral partners. That is just a shocking number. 

So the best way to speed up the process is to actually spend time in the process of building relationships with the people who you are networking with. Networking truly is—and you have heard me say this a lot—more about farming than it is about hunting. This is hard data that helps support that. It is about building relationship and friendships with other business professionals. 

Now, it is important to note that there is a confidence curve that you have to negotiate before doing business with someone. We talk about the time confidence curve in our Member Success Program Training and in other places, but I am going to include it here in the podcast just to remind you. If you take that curve too fast, you will flip your reputation and end up in a very awkward position. Now, when it comes to getting referrals from your network, confidence is a vital component. Not your confidence, but the confidence that your fellow network member has in you. None of them want to risk their personal reputation by referring business to someone that they do not know, like and trust, that they are not at credibility with in the VCP process.

So even though you may have known many of your networkers for some time, until they have gained a certain level of confidence that referring contacts to you will not harm their reputation with their clients and friends,  you are still a referral stranger unless you have hit that point. So, the time confidence curve is here in the podcast. It is also in my book, Networking Like a Pro. It illustrates the dynamics of the process. Your success in getting referrals depends partly on your competence, of course, but also on how far up the confidence curve the referrer’s confidence in you has progressed. If you are at a point in the relationship where you are just starting, you are not going to get many referrals, but if you are at a higher level in the relationship and you have known each other for a while but you haven’t quite achieved the necessary confidence level with this person to get the referral from them, then you are still not going to get referrals. It is only when you hit that critical confidence level on the curve where people now feel comfortable with you. And by the way, the time confidence curve varies with profession. For a florist it may be really quick, but for a financial planner it is really, really long. 

One way to expedite this process, which is the question that I am often asked, is to do regular 1-2-1s with people and go deeper in knowing how to refer them as well as teach them how to refer you. It is not always easy to know how far you have progressed up your confidence curve. Many networkers spend a lot of time and effort trying to build others’ confidence in them and then when they are on the brink of success, they get discouraged and they stop going to the networking meetings they were going to or they stop coming to BNI.

Oftentimes, they stop right at that point where things are going to kick in. I remember I once heard a story about a guy who had been in two years and certainly made more than his investment back, but it wasn’t the big numbers he was hoping for. He almost didn’t do that third year membership. Three weeks into his third year, he got the biggest referral of his life. Not just from BNI, but from all referral sources. It was three weeks into that third year. That was a great example. He was in one of those professions that takes time. He was a financial planner. So, you know, if you drop out, you are going to lose those types of connections.

Here is what you can do to gain perspective on your efforts and results that they are producing. Ask yourself the following four questions and keep asking them over and over until you have attained success and the answers become obvious. Here are the four questions:

  1. Am I being realistic about the time it will take in my profession to gain the critical level of confidence that I need? 

  2. Am I regularly making stimulating educational presentations to my fellow networkers about the value that I provide to my clients? I recommend that you go back and listen to the podcast I did with Charlie Lawson. I think it was entitled “Don’t Bore Me to Tears”, which is a great title. He talks about how you do a really good weekly presentation.

  3. Am I doing business with others in my network so that I can give them dynamic testimonials and steer business to them sot that they will return the favor?

  4. Am I meeting regularly with my networking colleagues to learn about their businesses so that I can confidently refer my contacts to them?

So if you are following these simple tactics and are asking yourself these questions, then you are well along the road to getting the referrals from others in the networks that you participate in, BNI in particular. So I will leave you with one last thought and then if you have questions or want to talk some more, Priscilla, I am happy to do that. 

Here is my last thought: remember it takes time to build friendships – unless you are in kindergarten, then you can do it in about five minutes. 

Priscilla:
Right. Well, I do have something to say which is I am a big proponent for using each other’s services. I think that can really move along the time frame. If you give a testimonial for somebody that you just used their services, if you loved what they did, that can really change the timeline. 

Ivan:
Yes.

Priscilla:
I just had it happen to me. I used a personal organizer in our group. It had been a long time before I used her services. She was wonderful. Now I can just truly say that to everybody and to anybody I refer her to.  You know, it changes everything. So I think I is a great – I know you are not supposed to sell to your fellow members, but I really think it helps for fellow members to use each other’s services so they have the personal experience. 

Ivan:
I am not sure I have ever said to anybody that you shouldn’t sell to your fellow members. I think you don’t want to target your fellow members exclusively. I agree with you, Priscilla. I think using each others’ services is brilliant because then you can come back and give those testimonials which will impact your referrals. I am a fan, but I just caution people not to exclusively give internal referrals. Some chapters call them internal referrals, which is member to member. An external referral is an outside member to a member. Some chapters call them Tier 1 and Tier 2 referrals. Whatever you call it, you want to transition to the latter over time. You want to transition to those outside referrals.

But it is hard to transition to outside referrals if you can’t speak about the person personally, which is your point, I think. Yeah?

Priscilla:Right.

Ivan: 
I couldn’t agree more. So to me it is not either or. It is both and. It should begin with the internal referrals and graduate over time to the external referrals. Good point, Priscilla, thank you.  That is everything I’ve got today. 

Priscilla:
Okay, well that sounds great. Thank you, Dr. Misner. I really appreciate it. So for more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast. 

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: BNI members average 5 times as many referrals after 200 hours in a chapter as when they started.

title.rendered: Episode 595: How Do You End a Conversation?

content.rendered:
Synopsis

How do you end a conversation at a networking meeting? Here are four techniques.

  1. Say “It was really nice meeting you. Do you have a card so I can have your contact information? Thanks.” This is the simplest option and the one Dr. Misner uses most often.
  2. Recap the part of the conversation you liked most, and then use #1. Ask permission to write a note on the back of the card.
  3. If they say something that makes you think of someone else at the event, make the introduction on the spot.
  4. Invite them to go to another networking event that you go to–like your BNI meeting.

Keep it simple. Keep it honest. Whatever you do, don’t Seinfeld it. Be polite and friendly. Don’t make excuses. The real key is how you follow up.

Tell us how you end your networking conversations by leaving a comment at the end of this podcast.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Priscilla: 
Hello and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today with the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer, Dr. Ivan  Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla, and I think we should mention that we are using a new recording system, and I hope everyone likes it. I believe it will level out my piece of the conversation. So we would love to hear. Last week’s podcast and this week’s podcast are on the new system, so we would love to hear what you guys think.

Priscilla:
So what is this topic that you have?

Ivan:
The topic is how do you end a conversation? As the Founder of BNI, I often get asked about the best way to end a conversation in a networking situation. Candidly, I really think the answer is pretty simple. So I am going to start this podcast with the simple solution. Then, for those of you who love to overthink things, I will give you some other options.

Here are four techniques: One, say something simple like, “It was really nice meeting you. Do you have a card so I can have your contact information? Thanks.” That’s it. No fuss, no muss, no big deal. Don’t apologize because you have to go network with somebody else. Definitely don’t say you see someone else you need to talk to. Those are the things that make it feel awkward. Simply thank them very much and move on. 

I was doing an interview not long ago, before I give you two, three and four, I was doing an interview not long ago, and it was with somebody who knew me through BNI. I told him that and he said, “Oh my goodness. You did that to me. It was so smooth. I never thought about it. I felt like you listened to me. We spoke. We had a conversation. It wasn’t really long, but I really felt like we had a conversation. Then you said those very words. ‘It was really nice meeting you. Do you have a card so I can have your contact information? Thanks a lot.’” He said it was really, really smooth. 

So this is where I say don’t overthink it. I really believe that this is the easiest of the four techniques and it is the one that I generally use because I am there to network. I do meet a lot of other people. Many times, they are BNI events and some of the BNI members want to meet me. So it is an effective technique. 

Here is number two: frame what you liked about the conversation or recap part of the conversation that you found most interesting and then do number one. I have done that a fair number of times as well because there may have been something the person said and I want to anchor it in my mind. I want to remember it. So I will ask them. Or I will talk to them about it. “So let me just recap what you said. You said [this and that]. That was really amazing. Thank you for sharing that with me. It was a real pleasure meeting you. Can I have one of your cards?”

Now, what I may do in that case is I will say to them, “Is it okay if I write on the back of your card a note or two?” Now, this is important because I know that it is inappropriate to write on the back of cards in some cultures, particularly in Asian cultures, so I never do that in Asian countries. I carry a little notepad with me rather than write on the card. But in most countries, it is fine. Don’t even ask in [Asian] countries. But in most countries, where you know it is okay, ask if you can write on the back. It is one way that I can remember the conversation. That is number two.

Number three: if they say something that makes you think of someone else that they should meet, tell them. And tell them that you promise to make the introduction. If the other person is there at the event, make the introduction on the spot. Being a connector at a networking event is always a good thing. So that is another great way to end the conversation. 

Here is number four: invite them to participate with you at another networking meeting that you go to regularly. They may want to get out and meet more people. This is a great chance to connect them to another network of individuals, particularly BNI. Invite them to your BNI group. It gives you the chance to meet them again. “It was really interesting talking to you. I would love to chat with you again sometime. I am really active in another networking organization, BNI. I would love to invite you to come and visit it. I would love to learn more about what you do.” That is a great technique.

Now, for those of you who want more, I have read all kinds of exit lines, and unless they are absolutely true, I don’t recommend most of them. Keep it simple. Keep it honest. If you want some of those exit lines, here are a few, but I don’t like them. But I have read about them and they may resonate with you. Here is one that I read from another “expert” at networking:

“It was great meeting you. I have to get home by X:00 to have dinner with the family. I hope we connect again.” Then you step away, but you better leave the place because if you are there another 30 minutes talking to other people, it is the liar-liar-pants-on-fire sort of excuse.

Here is another one: “It has been nice meeting you. I need to run to the restroom, but thanks for talking.” Again, if you do need to go, you better go. 

Here is another one that I read. Again, these are not things that I use. I don’t think they are that strong, but I have read them and I think some people might think they resonate with them. “I have a deadline on a project. I really need to take off, but it was great meeting you.” And leave. I honestly think that is too much information for people. Just tell them that it was nice meeting them, that’s great. 

So I have a couple more thoughts and then I would love to hear your thoughts on it, Priscilla. Those first four are what I recommend. The last three are okay, but don’t fib. Don’t make it up. If you really have to do something, tell them. Otherwise, simply doing what I say in the first one is great. Whatever you do, don’t “Seinfeld” it. That is a phrase I use in my latest book, Who’s In Your Room? Don’t “Seinfeld” it. One of the really funny things on the old TV series, Seinfeld, and the characters and how they go off on some crazy, complicated rouse and end up in more trouble than if they had just been candid to start with. Be honest and be direct. “Seinfelding” it almost always fails and both you and the other person feel uncomfortable. 

Remember to keep it polite and friendly. Don’t make excuses. politely move on. The real key is how you follow up, it then that is a different podcast that I have done before, using the follow up after you network. So what do you think, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
I really like your first four examples and I don’t think that the last three that you brought up – it’s too much information. You don’t need to provide it to the person. You don’t have to be embarrassed that you need to end the conversation. And then you are stuck saying or having to do whatever excuse you used to get out of talking, which might not suit what you need to do next, which might be to meet other people. So I am in agreement with you.

Ivan:
I have always found that ending the conversation is really not that complicated. I really feel that people overthink it. Obviously, I lean towards number one. However, if I have the chance to do particularly the one where I can connect them to somebody else in the room, number three, I love that even more. I don’t always have that opportunity, not with everybody I meet. I would say that almost every networking opportunity that I go to, I do use that technique at least once. There is someone in the room who says something where I say, “Hey, I know somebody in the room that does that or that you might want to talk about that issue. Let me look around. Oh, they are right there. Would you like me to introduce you?” Then I walk them over. I think that is a fantastic technique. You just can’t do it to every single person you meet. 

Priscilla:
Right, right. 

Ivan:
So that is it. Now, what I would love for you to do is try a couple of these things out and come back to my podcast and tell me how they work. Or if you do some of them now, tell me how they work. Or even if you use those three that I said I don’t particularly like, if you use it and you like it, tell us specifically in the podcast how you use that in ending the conversation and how it has worked out for you. I am open to hearing the good, the bad, and the ugly. Ending a conversation should be pretty easy if you mean what you say to the person you are talking to, and you are polite and professional. That is everything that I have for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay. Great, Ivan. Thank you so much. For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

 

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: How do you end a conversation at a networking event? Keep it simple. Thank them for the conversation, get their contact info, and make an introduction if you can.

title.rendered: Episode 594: You Need a Doorman

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This week’s topic comes from Dr. Misner’s book Who’s in Your Room? Think about this episode from a BNI membership committee perspective as well as a personal perspective.

If you live in a room with an enter-only door, you need a “doorman” to screen people before letting them in. In a BNI chapter, the membership committee acts as the doorman.

But how do you decide who belongs? It comes down to your core values. BNI has a well-defined set of core values. For your personal “doorman” to do a good job, you too need to be clear about your core values. Start by establishing deal-breakers, like “drama” or “narcissism.”

Check out the book if you haven’t already, and be sure to visit the International Networking Week website!

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Priscilla: 

Hello and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today with the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer, Dr Ivan Misner. 

Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you? 

Ivan:

 I’m doing fantastic. This week I happen to be on a cruise for BNI Canada in the Caribbean. They did an International Networking Week cruise. As most people probably know, this is International Networking Week. I think it’s our 12th annual International Networking Week around the world. If you haven’t already, go to InternationalNetworkingWeek.com and check out the video there. 

Priscilla:

Okay, perfect. Tell me about this topic.

Ivan:

So, it’s called “You Need a Doorman.” This is some material from the book that I’ve talked about in a previous podcast, the public edition of “Who’s in Your Room?” Which just came out 3 months ago. It hit number one in self-help. This is one of the topics in the book. I promised that I would talk about some other chapters from the book. As you are listening to me talk, think about BNI because although this book is talking about the individual, but this topic in particular “The Doorman Principle” is really about BNI and BNI’s membership committee. So, as I talk about this from an individual’s context, I want everyone to think about it from the BNI membership committee’s perspective. 

We ended up with the word/phrase “doorman” but you can call it a bouncer, door person, guard or whatever you want- BNI membership committee is just that. Let me give a quick review of “Who’s in Your a Room?” book. The concept is you live your entire life in one room. That one room has one door and it’s an enter only door so that when people come and enter your life, they are there forever. What we talked about in the last podcast is we need to have this doorman concept in your head to screen people who come in. BNI membership committee is the same thing, you screen people who come in. So let’s consider the door. There it sits, it’s closed but not locked. It’s ready to swing open when the next person pushes his/her way in. Now when you are really young, you can only sit and marvel at the people who come into your room and it takes a few years before you understand that not all the people who come in are people you necessarily want isn’t the room. That’s true in BNI as well. But even after you figure out the meaning and the function of the door, you have to spend time learning how, what and why you should allow entry into the room. You have to determine ways to control who comes into your room. 

So, what defines the kind of person that you want your door man to let into your room? Is it someone who is successful or a positive attitude? These are certainly things that are particularly relevant in the BNI context. You got to get in control of that door, and an individually speaking, that means your doorman. Your doorman is in your conscious and subconscious mind. It screens out people that try to enter your life, and in BNI that’s your membership committee. Right now for you individually and possibly for the chapter, that door is swinging open and shuts on it’s own. People of all sorts are flooding in and they may or may not be a good fit. So when you are fully ready to take charge of your door, you have to assign that duty to this imaginary person that becomes part of your personality. Your doorman stands at the entrance of that one way door and admits or bars those who want to enter. Your doorman does this from a set of criteria that you provide individually and in BNI with the membership committee. Happy, creative and positive people can go right in! Negative, pessimistic people thou shalt not pass. 

In order to determine who to let into your room, you need to make some important choices. These choices have consequences. How do you determine what you look for when you want to allow people into your room or into your chapter? The bottom line is that you got to understand your values. BNI has a full set of core values that we incorporate organization. It begins with really understanding BNI’s core values. But for you individually, you’ve got to get good with your personal, individual core values. It is very difficult sometimes- when I talk to people, Priscilla, I’m not going to put you into the spotlight, but sometimes when I talk to people they say, “Oh, I know my values.” I say, “Great! Give me five or six of your values.” They go, “Uhm… Uh… Well, honesty is important.” I say, “Good! What else? Give me a few more.” Then they hum and haw and they don’t know. You cannot screen people who are coming into your life, just like you can’t screen people who are coming into your chapter, unless you a good with your values. 

I am going to give you a technique that you can use personally that will help you get started in understanding your values- and I think in another podcast, I’ll go deeper in talking about values, but here’s a beginning point; Think about your deal breakers. They are values that are total deal breakers for you that when someone around you has this behavior, it is completely unacceptable. I want you to think of one of your deal breakers, if you don’t mind sharing them, Priscilla. But let me give you an example of a couple for me. For me when I was working one this book, I realized that drama was one of my deal breakers. The people who are constantly dripping in drama, you know what I’m talking about, they have so much drama.
