Now I have drama, you have drama and most people have some sort of drama but you know the kind of drama that I’m talking about where it is every day. I looked around my room and I realized that I had a ton of people with drama that were in my life. Then I realized that it was all my fault. I let them in, and sometimes chapters have members that come in and they haven’t done an affective job of screening those members so they get in the chapter and the chapter isn’t as successful as it could be. By the way, you want to screen for both aptitude and attitude. Aptitude is good for making sure they are good at what they do, but attitude is the secret sauce. You want to make sure these people have a positive and supportive attitude, which so happens to be one of BNI’s core values. Our seven core values are 1) Givers Gain being our principle core value. 2) Building relationships. 3) Lifelong learning. 4) Traditions plus innovations. 5) Positive attitude. 6) Accountability. 7) Recognition. Those are our core values.

I would urge chapters and their membership committees to screen people- the “doorman” of the chapter that is the membership committee, should screen people by having conversations with them and the organizations core values. “These are our core values. How do they sit with you? How’s lifelong learning? Are you willing to participate in our member success program training? Will you participate in BNI university? Are your okay with accountability?” You have to understand that this is all about building relationships. It takes time. So, you have conversations with them about the values and see how they respond. Not everybody is a good fit for BNI. This is where the “doorman” for the organization, the membership committee, but also in your life the “doorman” principle is absolutely critical.

Let me share one more if you don’t mind. My wife and I, if we go out on a date with another couple, we feel strongly that the couple must love and respect each other. Both. They must treat each other with love and respect, but again, not everyone is perfect and we may go out with a couple and may catch them on a bad night but generally speaking we want to hangout with other married couples who love and respect one another. When we came to that conclusion, we checked out our room and realized that we let people into our lives where some of them didn’t treat each other with love and respect. So, we learned how to deal with them in the room. I’ll talk about how to deal with people who have got into your room, and you got to deal with those, but I don’t have time to do that today- but we then started screening all future couples based on that criteria. So there’s a few deal breakers for me. Do you have a deal breaker, Priscilla?

Priscilla:

I do. My dealbreaker is narcissistic people. I had a tendency to attract narcissistic people for a period of my life in the early part, but I realized I can’t handle that anymore. I don’t want those people in my life. 

Ivan:

I think that is a great value. The way I describe it, if their ego enters the room before I do, that’s not a relationship I want to have. As you might suspect, I have met many, many people and some of them are big names that people might know. So, I agree completely. Again, nobody is perfect but I try hard to not let my ego enter the room before me whenever possible. I believe that a humble person doesn’t think less of themselves, but rather they think about themselves less. That’s the behavior that I strive for and certainly the behavior that I look for. Those are some great examples.

Let me wrap it up. If you have a chance, check out the book it’s a public edition and has an orange cover. We’ll make sure to have a link here. I really believe, Priscilla, that this book will change people’s lives. I wish I had this book when I was 17 years old. I wish somebody had written this when I was 17. It’s so powerful in helping to create your own life. I think it works perfectly in a BNI chapter in creating the chapter of your dreams. The book is called “Who’s in Your Room?” It’s on Amazon and is available in most bookstores. I’ll talk about values and a couple of the other chapters in other podcasts.

That’s everything for today, Priscilla, unless you want to add anything.

Priscilla:

No, I think it’s great, Ivan. I’m so glad you are talking about the book. I got it downloaded on my Kindle, I’m ready to read it. I’ve read a portion of it already but I haven’t completed it yet.

Ivan:

Okay, there is going to be a test next time we talk then.

Priscilla:

Okay, good. I think that is it for this week. Thank you so much for the information, Dr. Ivan Misner. For more of Ivan’s content go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.


ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: An effective doorman for your personal room (or BNI chapter) lets people in based on how well they fit with your core values.

title.rendered: Episode 593: Growth Is Not the Goal

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Tim Roberts, BNI National Director for the United States and host of the BNI & the Power of One podcast, joins Dr. Misner on the podcast this week to explain why strong contact spheres matter more than chapter growth.

It’s easy to overlook the fact that most BNI chapters don’t offer an equal opportunity for success in their chapters. Each member’s referrals come from within their contact spheres. A member in a contact sphere of 10 has more opportunity than a member in a contact sphere of 2.

When you set your chapter growth targets, aim to create contact spheres of equal size. Take a look at your chapter and see which contact spheres and power teams need strengthening. Concentrate your visitor outreach there to increase member retention and member success.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Priscilla:

Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Like Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief visionary’s Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner.

Hello, Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:

I’m doing fantastic, Priscilla. Thank you so much. I have a guest on my podcast today, his name is Tim Roberts. He’s the US National Director for BNI, as well as an Executive Director of BNI in New Hampshire, Orlando and Jacksonville. Tim’s been involved in BNI for many, many years with family before him. It’s great to have him on my podcast. Tim also hosts The BNI and the Power of One podcast, which he’s had me on a couple of times. I think that it’s time that I bring Tim on, He’s got a great topic.

Tim, welcome to my podcast!

Tim:

Thanks for having me, it is a great honor!

Ivan:

My pleasure! So, the topic is “Growth is Not the Goal.” So, what a minute, what do you mean by that? We always said that growth was important for a chapter, so tell us what you mean.

Tim:

Yeah, it may be a little bit of a clickbait in this title to capture the audience, but growth to me is something that I believe people have made the only goal in a lot of chapters. Growth is extremely important, it’s vital to the success of any chapter to be growing. But what I’ve been realizing over the years and talking with my chapters, kind of talking about across the country of the US this year is that we can’t be setting goals solely on just growth just to hit a number. To say, “Our chapter is 20 members. We want to get 30 members.” Without purpose or meaning or strategy behind that. I think oftentimes we select numbers that are arbitrary. It sounds great to go from 20 to 30, and think growth- when you see that, is an indication of a lot of other successes. But if we just set the goal on flat, straight growth with no meaning then we may not be offering equal opportunity to everybody in the chapter. I really look at it as growth as a tool. Growth is a tool to enable everybody in the chapter to become more successful in their BNI journey, rather than just the chapter achieving some arbitrary number for hitting some milestones.

Ivan:

Well, I like the concept that growth is a tool to success. You know, in success there is no “one thing” that helps one be successful; it is always a recipe. So, there are a number of things that I think that can make anyone successful, including a chapter. Growth is one of them, but it is not the only one. What else is really important?

Tim:

I think ultimately for true, long term success for any chapter needs to be heavily focused on retention. There are a lot of programs out there that are geared towards retention of chapters. There are many great programs and tools that exist like BNI University and all the live trainings that are going on around the world. But that is where I think growth comes along in play. Growth is a tool that ultimately- I look at all of our chapters that are success teams. There are teams of local business people, coming together, everyone is joining and becoming a partner of the team because they are trying to become more successful. I love the word “success” because people get to define it for themselves. So, everybody may have a different definition of that, some might be joining to growth their business, looking to retire, pay college tuition, pay for a dream home or family vacation- whatever that is. But they’ve come together team to help each other through Givers Gain, through the systems and structure of BNI to achieve that. If we really focus as a team and realize we are all hear to achieve that and have passion behind that, then I think the one thing I think a lot of our chapters are blink to but need to be paying attention to is the fact that most chapters don’t offer an equal opportunity for success in their chapter right now.

Ivan:

What do you mean by that? That is an interesting comment.

Tim:

When you look at them, we know that through the studies of the great folks you’ve written and all the education that has come up through BNI and through the years is that the majority of any member referrals they are gong to receive are really going to come from their contact sphere, which is ultimately their power team. Just because of opportunity that exists by people having similar target markets or similar complementary businesses. The fact is that most of our chapters are not balanced out. They are out of whack in terms of if you think of 5, 6, 8 however many CONTACTS you may look at in a chapter, there’s usually one, two or three that are really heavy. And then there’s the one, two or three that are very light. And when thats the case, we can say, “Well look, you have a contact sphere of ten and somebody else in your chapter has a contact sphere of two.” They don’t have the equal opportunity to be successful in that chapter as you do, based on the fact that your contact sphere is more developed. 

So for me, one of my messages that I started realizing is I was kind of blind, I’d been in BNI for 18 years and I wasn’t really thinking this way for most of it. It’s not really visible in a chapter at all times. We don’t really look at people inside their contact spheres in the weekly meetings. We can really miss this. I want to tell you just two quick stories that made me say screw this.

We have a chapter that is 74 members, who in one year lost and replaced 48 different people. From the outside, you looked at the chapter that was three times the average chapter size in the region, producing millions of dollars for each other, one of the largest chapters in the region for a long time but you may not have payed attention to the fact that they had a serious retention issue. I went to the chapter to find the reason for that and they said, “We think we have a retention issue.” I was like, “We think we have a retention issue? You very clearly do.” They said, “We don’t understand. We are the largest chapter, why would we have that?” I said, “I’ll show you… Everybody who is in a trade, stand up.” It was over 20 people that stood up. Then I said, “Okay, everybody who is in health and wellness, stand up.” There was only two. “This is why you have a retention issue. You guys all over here are making a ton of money that you will never need, but you are losing all of the other members because they don’t have the same opportunity. Unless we help them have that same opportunity, you are going to continue to have this problem.” That is when I start talking about how much we really care about “Susie Q” the member, because she is part of our success team and we need to help “Susie Q” and make sure she has the opportunity to be successful. That is where growth now becomes a tool. It is not about hitting some numbers, it is about making sure “Susie Q’s” contact sphere is well rounded and that she has the opportunity to capture that and thus be a member for a long time. 

Ivan:

So, in your experience in talking to chapters- I think that was a visual way to describe it by aging everyone stand up, but has that resonated with the chapters, do they embrace that?

Tim:

They have and I think it is because it is a different why and purpose behind growth than just being around for the challenges in BNI and being around for so many years, our methods sometimes can get a little stale. People have heard it before and it leads to some pushback. It just gives them a different why and purpose for them to say, “oh, okay that makes more sense than just If you have more members, you make more money.” Which is statistically proven but certain members are already making a lot of money so they don’t really care. Others aren’t making anything so they themselves don’t believe it. It highlights the real issue inside a chapter. I think it also helps retention because if “Susie Q” is all alone, we know one of the main reasons people leave BNI is because they feel like nobody will notice. I think that is because they are in these contact spheres alone. If we highlight that, then we show that we care. 

Real quick to prove a point, I had a round table meeting back when I owned the state of Maine.  

I was talking about this and we were talking about visitor days. The gentleman next to me said, “Oh my chapter just canceled our visitor day that was scheduled two weeks from now.” I said, “You canceled a visitor day?” He said, “Yeah, we just inducted our 31st member.” I said, “Okay. Why did you cancel visitor day?” He said, “Well, Time, we know you want to grow but we really feel that 30 is an ideal number for our chapter.” I said, “So, you accidentally hit 31 members?” He kind of giggled and said yes. I said, “Alright, let’s go through this exercise.” I had forms, and we have these in BNI University where, to put in context, you fill all out the members who have contacts that they should belong to. He did that and just to get all the contact spheres to the same size as the current largest one, they need 29 more members. I said, “You are so far away from growing. You just thought 30 was a great number for your size and you felt comfortable. Unless you are willing to tell all these other people that being at 30 is more important than their opportunity for success.”

Ivan:

That is brilliant. The people who are doing really well have the contact spheres. They are making a lot of money. They are getting a lot of referrals, unlike the ones that are in small power teams are not- like you are saying. That’s one of the dangers about the concept of average. An average number may look good in a chapter, but there’s a problem with average. Our good friend Andy Heart talks about with statistics if your head is in the oven and your feet are in the freezer, then on average you are okay. Not so much. One extreme for sure is not going to be doing well, and those people who don’t have a fulfilled power team are one them.

So, what you are really talking about is growth is a tool, targeted growth is important and targeting for power teams and contact spheres are important. If you want to know the difference between them, look at our past podcasts because we spent sometime on it.

Any last thoughts before we wrap up?

Tim:

I just think that the more that we can focus on our current members and opt for that equal opportunity for success, the more success people are going to see and the happier they are going to be. The longer that team is together the more money everybody is going to produce, too. They really go hand-in-hand. We should not be looking at it as a separate thing. It’s how they work together. The longer you retain, the easier it is to grow, too. I think with the right why and the right purpose, chapters and the success teams are only at the cusp of what we can produce at BNI. 

Ivan: 

Great point! This is must listen for all chapters. Education coordinators, I would love for you to share this with your chapter, I think it is really powerful. I also recommend that you go to Tim’s podcast website, BNIPowerOfOne.com. He’s got great content on there as well. 

Tim, thank you so much for being on BNI podcast today.

Tim:

Thanks for having me!

Ivan:

Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:

I think that is it for this week. Thank you both for the great information. For more of Ivan’s content go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Strengthen and balance your BNI chapter’s contact spheres to offer all members an equal opportunity for success and increase member retention.

title.rendered: Episode 592: The Value of Visitors (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This episode is a rebroadcast of Episode 522, originally released on August 30th, 2017.

David Clegg joins Dr. Misner this week to talk about the value of visitors in BNI. Visitors pass referrals to BNI members, whether or not they join. The average Thank You for Closed Business for a visitor in BNI is $1000.

If everyone in every BNI chapter brought one visitor per month, the average chapter would experience a bump in TYCB of $330,480. (Numbers come from BNI Connect.) The average member would experience an average of $12,000 in additional thank-you-for-closed-business from visitors.

If you follow the Power of One, as discussed in Episode 517, you’ll increase your bottom line considerably. One of the easiest ways to invite visitors is to use the acronym GRIP: Do you want to GROW your business? Would REFERRALS help? Are you INTERESTED in …? Here’s the PLACE we meet.

If you invite visitors on a regular basis, your chapter will grow and thrive, and so will your own business.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 522 –

Priscilla:

Hello everyone, and welcome back to the official BNI podcast, brought to you by The Networking Success channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and other key experts.

I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I’m joined on the phone today by the founder and the chief visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner.

Hello, Ivan! How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Galveston. I’ve got a little vacation getaway condo in Galveston. So I am working this week and will be taking vacation next week, but I will still have a podcast next week.

Today, I have a guest. It’s a very interesting story. My guest today is David Clegg. David has been with BNI for seven years, and he manages 6 chapters for BNI. He and I launched a chapter this year. David is going to talk about the value of a visitor and the power of one really impacts the amount of business being generated in a chapter. But, before you jump into that, David, welcome to my podcast!

David:
Thank you!

Ivan:
You have a great story as to what you do. Do you want to share that with everyone? I love the way you put it.

David:
Well, thank you. I tell people all the time that I work for my 18 year old daughter. My daughter when she was nine years old—of course she just graduated from high school this year, but when she was nine she asked me for spending money, and I told her no. I said I’ll never give you spending money, but I will teach you how to earn it!

So we went around the neighborhood looking for dogs to walk, knocked on every door, and someone is going to pay you to walk their dog—which she was surprised by. So she started walking dogs through the neighborhood, then we started watching dogs in the house, and about eight years after that, we now have—well what’s  been 10 year fully, we have a 12,000 square foot facility and we do doggy day care, boarding, grooming, training, and pet photography. It is called, appropriately, Alley’s Walkabout. That’s the name of my daughter, and she has 27 employees.

Ivan:
That’s an amazing story, David! Thanks for sharing, I really appreciate it.

David:
I’m real proud of her.

Ivan:
Yeah, I bet you are! And for good reason, my friend! So, talks to me a little about the power of one and the importance of visitors.

David:Well the value of visitors, I believe, cannot be overstated. I think it’s probably one the greatest opportunity cost’s in every chapter. As you know, a opportunity cost is when we make one decision, and it negates the ability to make another decision. So in this case, when we make the decision not to invite someone, or not to be strategic about inviting someone, it’s actually costing the chapter quite a bit of money.

Ivan:
So, explain that. Talk that through so that people can wrap their heads around that idea.

David:
Sure! Let me start at a global level and work it down to a individual member. A few months ago, I checked out membership and it was 222,000 members. I know it’s up a little bit since then. With about 8,060 chapter. Which means our average chapter has seven members. Well, if globally 222,000 people commit and execute one visitor every month, and bring visitor to the chapter, the average visitor globally – let’s just say it’s a 1,000 dollars, thank you for close business to a visitor is a thousand dollars. Again, it’s higher than that—

Ivan:
By the way, the number he is quoting is right out of BNI Connect, everyone. These are harden, fast numbers as of the day he was looking at them. They are available now. These are not made up numbers, these are numbers right out of BNI Connect. Sorry, David.

David:
Well, in fact, you can see them on the front page of BNI.com. So, what that would mean is 2,664,000 visitors globally or over 2.6 billion dollars in thank you for close business. So, the average chapter would experience a bump in thank you for close business to the chapter of 330,480 dollars just from visitors, per member, per month.

So, what does that mean for the average member listing to this podcast? It mean, they would receive and additional $12,000 in thank you for closed business.

Ivan:
That’s on average. Some are more, some are less. But still, that pretty impressive! So, what you point out is something that not everybody realize about visitors, they do in fact bring referrals. They come to a meeting and they go, “Oh my goodness, I need that!” And they pass a referral. And that number gets added to thank you for close business

David:
And many times you’ll run into visitors that have never seen anything like BNI. And they’re just dumbstruck by how organized it is, “Wow, this is terrific!” They literally get enthused about trying to use people in the chapter meeting

Ivan:
They do. I have even had people in chapters who have come up to me afterwards and they say, “You know, I almost didn’t come because of this and I’ve gone to a whole bunch of meetings. Oh goodness! This is so structured and organized and there was so much business generated today!” It’s always exciting to see how the visitors respond.

David:
And typically, they are thrilled by the idea of having a team to work with.

Ivan:
Absolutely! So, the message here is, if you follow the power of one, which is to have each member bring one visitor once a month- and there’s more to it. We just did a podcast a few weeks ago pursuing – I don’t know if you remember the podcast with Colin, and we did, “The Power of One.” And let’s see if I can find it while we are talking. But, we just did one. It should be the Power of One, Episode 517. So, if you haven’t heard about this, bring one visitor per-member per-month is one piece of it. But, what David is talking about here is it will increase your bottom line, thethank you for closed business chapter considerably.
Is there anything you want to add?

David:
Yes. One of the easiest ways to invite visitors—I’m sure we’ve all heard GRIP and other acronyms that help keep the process mind. So, “Do you want to grow your chapter?” G is Grow, R is would Referrals help, I is are you Interested in, and the P is talking about the Place you meet. So you go through this acronym to hit all the steps and that certainly is effective.

One of my favorite methods to use is to ask people. We have a realtor in our chapter, Jewel Buckley, who is with Coldwell Banker. So, if I’m talking to a trades person, it would’ve been set for meeting jewel, I’ll simply ask the question, “Do you know Jewel Buckley? She transacts about a 150 houses a year-” on and on.

“I’d love to meet her.”

“Well, we meet pretty regularly. I’m seeing her this Thursday if you want to join me.”

It’s just about meeting Jewel and it’s just the connection, then all of the sudden they have 30 other people to meet.

Ivan:
Well, that’s great information, David. I really appreciate being willing to come and share this. Any closing thoughts before we Wrap up?

David:
Just keep it on the forefront of your mind. It’s muscle memory. If you are inviting on a regular basis, you’re going to develop that muscle of inviting people to your chapter meeting. And through that, your chapter will grow and thrive.

Ivan:
You know, that a good way of putting it, muscle memory. Bringing in visitors will inevitably increase the size of chapter you’re bringing in, right? When you have a chapter of over thirty members, it becomes a force multiplier, where you don’t have to go out and sell as much. Because, people are bringing you referrals. Before we on the podcast, David, you said it was a little scary to take that step off the cliff to rely on referrals to get your business. It’s incredible how it works isn’t it?

David:
Absolutely, it really is.

Ivan:
All right, David, thanks so much for being our guest on my podcast. It’s a real pleasure having you here. Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great! Well thank you both. It was wonderful information. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Visitors pass referrals to BNI members, whether or not they join. The average Thank You for Closed Business for a visitor in BNI is $1000.

title.rendered: Episode 591: From Self-Employed to Entrepreneur

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Cindy Mount joins Dr. Misner this week to talk about the difference between being self-employed and being an entrepreneur.

The distinction between these two terms makes a big difference in your retirement plan and your overall quality of life after retirement.

Many people are self-employed when they come into BNI. They are the only ones working in their company and do everything themselves. The only value in the business is their labor.

But over time, by putting what they learn in BNI into practice, they become entrepreneurs: they own a business with employees, and the business is an asset. When they retire, they can choose to sell the business or take a residual income from it.

If you want to take that next step and scale up, BNI provides you all the resources you need:

  • Learn management skills like interviewing and mentoring by serving on the membership committee.
  • Learn how to lead your company by serving on the leadership team.
  • If you need to improve your public speaking skills, take on the role of the education coordinator.
  • If you need to learn more about how to market your business, get on the marketing committee.

Embrace all the roles and responsibilities in chapters and use them as building blocks for your own business.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Priscilla:

Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner.

Hello, Ivan! How are you today?

Ivan:

I am doing fantastic. We are here in the new year, and I’m excited about a lot of the podcasts. I have guest with us today, Cindy Mount. She’s been in business since 1995 and she’s been in BNI since 1998. In September of last year, she celebrated her 20th anniversary in BNI. 20 years in the organization. She’s a contributing author to two New York Times best selling books, and I’m somewhat familiar with both of them; “Masters of Networking”, and “Masters of Sales.” Both of which I was involved with, as well. And something that no one knows about Cindy, I’m actually going to share two things about her. Two things that no one knows about Cindy is that she won her first Rodeo Queen Ribbon in 1974. So Cindy you must have been like 10. And she was an Alberta Forest Service employee and she was one of the first women on the firefighters overhead team, which I think is even more interesting. 

Cindy, next time we see each other I want to hear more about that because that’s really incredible!

Cindy:

It was a remarkable job. I absolutely loved that time of life- not saying this time in life is bad, but it was a remarkable time.

Ivan:

Well, we are really happy to have you in BNI. Your topic today is self employed to entrepreneur. I think that’s a great topic and I think there are a lot of solo-preneurs in the world and they didn’t really understand what being an entrepreneur is. Most people confuse self employed and entrepreneur. So I would love for you to talk about that subtle difference if you would.

Cindy:

Sure. I’ve been around BNI, like you said, for 20 years now. So it’s been a while and I watch people come in to BNI typical;y self employed in a lot of cases, and then they make that transition to entrepreneur. I’m going to explain that a little bit. I’ve done everything to starting a group called, “The Entrepreneurs Association”,  to teaching a piece of our self employment benefits program here in Ontario. One of the things that I see is how people confuse those two terms. And while they are closely related, they have a slightly different yet really important distinction. That’s my humble opinion on it. That small distinction is going to make the difference in your retirement plans and your overall quality of life when you do decided to retire, at least that is what I believe. I look at some of the BNI members who, like I said before, get the full value of BNI. So what is that difference? Well, if I give you and example, I think that is probably the best way to demonstrate the difference. 

So let’s talk about an accountant in our region, I’m going to call him John so that we can protect the innocent. He came out fo a business with this place, and because he is a talented accountant, he decided to open up his own accounting firm and practice and he found BNI. Now fast forward 10 years later, with the help of BNI and the things that he learned through BNI- which I’ll expand on in a minute, but through the element of BNI he now has an accounting business. So, he has gone from this solo guy behind the desk, self employed with a paycheck to owning a company. Owning me a business to where he has ten full time employees, three part time contractors, three full time contractors, he’s “INC” and he’s entrepreneurial. He now owns a business and it’s become an asset. So, from a single self employed individual, to a entrepreneur who owns a company, something that he has required as an asset and he can sell this on his retirement. Or he can sit back and let people work and take a residual income from that. So, it’s about building an asset.

Another example is this self employed electrician that came into BNI and over a period of time, he now has seven truck on the road, he owns an electrical company and he too has built an asset.

Ivan:

So are we talking about scaling your business?

Cindy:

It is, yeah.

Ivan:

So really it’s about taking it from a solo-preneur to an entrepreneur. And interestingly enough, that is what we talked about last week in a interview with Megan, and how important it is to really. Mama get your time so that you can focus of scaling your business. Because how many times do we say people in BNI say, “I’m getting really busy. I’m going to step out”? Like seriously? I’m building my business and I’m making money, so I’m going to stop doing all the things that have made me successful? 

Cindy:

Exactly. They walk away a little shy of that success. Just keep pushing a little, and yet for some people or indie visuals, that’s all they want. They want to be self employed and walk away, they don’t want employees but with BNI it is more than referrals. It is more than just getting the business. If you want to take that next step and scale up, if you want to become entrepreneurial and build an asset all of the elements of BNI are there. You need a board of advisors. A successful company has a board of advisors. Why wouldn’t you surround yourself with some of the key people from your chapter and forms a board of advisors? If you need to learn hiring skills for your company as you start to bring on staff and how to interview, how to review, and who to manage that conflict when it happens. Get on the membership committee. Take the BNI membership committee training. Learn the interview skills and adapt them to your company. A lot of companies bring on summer students or college students and they have funding for that. Why not become a mentor in your BNI chapter? Learn all the mentoring skills that go with that and when you do bring on that student- it’s power when you can provide for that student, so what I’m saying is that BNI is much more than those referrals. Every skill that we teach members whether it is on the Executive Leadership team, a good President, who to lead the chapter, how will you lead your company? The good membership or Vice President who is hiring coaching and dismissing, you can learn all of those skills through BNI. If you are a little bit on the shy side of public speaking, stand up there and become a education coordinator and learn how to rock it! 

So, I believe BNI can do much more than just put in your pocket. Get on the marketing committee. Learn everything we have in BNI on how to market the chapter, and go out there and market your business on social media. 

Ivan:

I think if members really focus on BNI as being one of their pieces of their marketing department, they are going to be way more successful in the long run! If they understand that they are and entrepreneur and not a solo-preneur, that in particular will make their engagement more beneficial, would you agree?

Cindy:

I definitely agree. Taking all the pieces of BNI an incorporating them into your business. Like you were saying earlier, upscaling that business to ta new level. It’s not really about- you hear this when people start BNI , “I have got to make time to do BNI. I have got to schedule time to find referrals. I have to schedule time to do this, that or the other.” And instead of thinking of BNI that way, think of BNI as something you naturally do that becomes a part of who you are and how you naturally do business, you are going to be way more successful. 

Ivan:

Yeah, I think when people look at BNI and being part of their networking efforts then they look at it as, “I need to do this as part of my networking efforts.” As opposed to, “I need to do BNI.” It is all part and parcel to your relationship building. When I started BNI 34 years ago that was my vision, that BNI would be part of people’s referral marketing efforts. When you look at it from that perspective, I think it’s a different way to look at the time you are spending.

Well, listen, we are almost out of time, Cindy. We are almost out of time. If there anything else you would like to add before wrap?

Cindy:

I just want to encourage people to embrace all of the roles and responsibilities in their chapters, and to look at them as building blocks from their own business, adapt them to your own and become a hugely successful entrepreneur.

Ivan:

Well, I love your content. I had no idea you were the Rodeo Queen of 1974. I was in high school then- you were absolutely a child then I’m sure. 

It was great to have you on this podcast! I really appreciate your information. Thank you for being on today on BNI Podcast.

Cindy:

Thank you very much, Ivan!

Priscilla: 

Okay, great. I think that is it for this week. Thank you both so much! 

For more of Ivan’s content go to IvanMisner.com. 

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Cindy Mount explains the difference between self-employed and entrepreneur and how you can use BNI to build your business into an asset.

title.rendered: Episode 590: I’m Really Busy

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Today’s theme is activity vs. results, the conversation Dr. Misner has had since he started BNI 34 years ago this week.

Meaghan Chitwood, Director of Global Training for BNI explains that your activities have to change as you become busier, because you need to get more results from the same amount of time.

An example of this is conducting one-to-ones. During your first one-to-one with a member, you’ll probably have a more general discussion to get to know each other. But on subsequent one-to-ones, share the specific things you’re working on and the referrals you need.

If you’re doing something that works for everyone else and it’s not working for you, ask yourself “How can I do this differently and get a better result?”

Take a look at the tasks you’re engaging in—both within and outside of BNI—and eliminate the tasks that aren’t getting results. If you think BNI is taking too much time, talk to your director consultant about how to work your BNI program more effectively.

If there’s a skill you need to hone in BNI, search BNI® Business Builder for that topic and you’ll find tons of resources.

Complete Transcript of Episode 590

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio from Berkeley, CA. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner.Hello Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla Thank you very much. This is the 34 anniversary of BNI. 34 years this week.

Priscilla:
Congratulations. Ivan: I don’t feel that old, Priscilla. You know?

Priscilla: I know.

Ivan:
I know, I know. I had somebody a few years ago say, they came up to me and they said it is really nice to be a part of an organization where the Founder is still alive.

Priscilla:
Oh wow.

Ivan:
Ok, I am still alive. Yeah, I hope to be around for a long time. It’s incredible that we are 34 years old this year and I am really excited to have as a guest of the podcast this week somebody who has been involved in BNI for a long time. I have with us Meaghan Chitwood.

Meaghan became a member of BNI in 2003. She became an Executive Director in 2004 so she was on the fast track. She is in the northern Alabama area and she joined the Global Support Team as the Director of Global Training in 2016. Her roots in the BNI world are so deep that it would be hard to get rid of her if we wanted to. That was in her bio, and I must say, Meaghan, we don’t want to get rid of you. It is awesome to have you on this podcast. Welcome.

Meaghan:
Thank you so much. Thank you so much.

Ivan:
So let’s talk about this topic this is really a great topic. Talk about that you are really talking about in many ways activity versus results. Talk about that concept and what you have in mind here because I think from Day 1 when I started BNI in 1985 the conversation I had was activity versus results. That is really what your theme is.

Meaghan:
Yes, absolutely and I think many people who know me personally and also if you listen to my bio, I am really busy is something that people say about me all the time and I actually don’t really like saying that ever. I am as busy as I want to be. That’s the way I look at tit. It is activity versus results. It’s figuring out are my activities getting me the ultimate results that I want?

When we all joined BNI, it was a while ago but I do remember it. We were joining BNI to become busier. We wanted more work. We wanted more referrals. What we tend to see if you have looked into your chapter lately, every once in a while, you will have somebody stand up and say, “I am too busy.” Somehow we have overshot the right amount of busy-ness.

I want to just give some tips on how you can rethink that and try and look at things because your activities have to change as you become busier because you have to figure out a way to get more results out of a shorter amount of time in essence.

Ivan:
Can I just say I think you are right on the mark, there. We all have a certain number of hours in the day. It is how we apply that. If you are an entrepreneur and you are busy and are becoming busier, you have to do something crazy like hire people, delegate or do things to have other individuals step in and assist because we are all busy. The question is are we productive? That’s the question here.

Meaghan:
Yes. Absolutely. I use this metaphor. My children and I and my family like to be on the lake. You like to be on the lake as well. And I think of it as being on a paddleboard. Imagine being on the paddleboard in the middle of the lake safely with no boats coming. Safely in the middle of the lake. You close your eyes and you start paddling in the direction that you think you are paddling and then the wind is blowing and there is a little bit of a current and maybe a boat comes by. I am still paddling exactly the way I was paddling but I can end up going absolutely in an opposite direction if I am not paying attention to my activity. If I continue to do the same activity, I am going to get knocked over at some point. I found myself saying I need to squeeze a second out of every minute of every day so that I can get more things done. It is just that matter of changing the activity or changing the quality of the activity that you are going to do. I would love to share some examples of how you can do that with BNI activities.

Ivan:
Please do that because that is key for members. Drill down.

Meaghan:
Let me give you a couple of examples. When you first meet somebody for the first time and you are just getting to know someone, you really have to give them time and you kind of have to see where the conversation goes. You are trying to figure out do we have things in common? That first 1-2-1 tends to be one that is a little more meandering in the tell me about your kids, tell me about your this, tell me about your that. It is a little more meandering and you just kind of have to five it a little bit more time.

But fast forward. If you know that person really, really well, your 1-2-1s can literally be like 15-30 minutes. “Here are the deals that I am working on right now. Do you know anybody who you could help connect me to?” The 1-2-1 is the same in activity but they look very very different because I already know you. You already know me. We already hang out. Right now is not for hanging out. It is for us to quickly get some strategic business done. This is just one example of how you can change the way that you do the activity to get the results.

Another one I want to share which is a similar route like 1-2-1, is doing a lot of 1-2-1’s and you’re not getting referrals from the 1-2-1’s, then you might want to look at how you are doing the 1-2-1. Because you can walk in- and again kind of like my last example walk in with a list of 10 people and you might be able to introduce me to five of them if we already know each other really well. Whereas, if you’re doing a bunch of 1-2-1’s for people and you’re not resulting in anything, you need to look at it and say, “Okay, what do I need to do differently to get to the end result of the referral?” Which is why we do the 1-2-1’s in the first place! You got to just look at it and just say, “Okay what can I do differently to make this really work?” And like I said in the very beginning, we all joined BNI to get more business. We have to figure how to catch that business when it comes to play. Sometimes it is basic time management skills, “Okay, I’m going to manage this better and I’m going to make phone calls at this time of the day because I’ve got to be able to return all these referrals that I’m getting.” I got to think through how to use my time management to really make sure that I’m on top of my game on a regular basis. 

Ivan:

Time management skills are key. I have a person that I know that talked about being busy and never had time to do whatever and they were a coffee barista. Like really dude? You don’t have time? You make coffee for a living!  It’s how we use that time that is key. I meet BNI members that say, “I don’t have time to do this or that.” But how you manage that time is the key, and I think that is what you’re talking about here.

Meaghan:

Absolutely! Let me give you can example: task lists. I have a task list but what I found what was happening about two weeks ago was I putting more stuff on the task list than what was coming off. It was more like I was spending all day long creating a task list that I need to do, and no time in the day for doing the things on the task list. But you know what I could have said? I could have said that task lists don’t work. But task lists work for every other person on the planet- except me! For some reason it just wasn’t working for me. So I had to look at it and say, “How can I do this differently and get a better result?” Luckily, I asked the right person the right question and he said, “Put the task on your calendar for however long you think it is going to take you .” And I’m telling you, since then, I’ve had the most productive last few weeks. But I mean, I’ve been doing this for 15 years, you would think that I would have been able to come up with that on my own. But I didn’t. I have to spend the time to sit back and say, “Okay what is working about this? What activities am I doing? Oh, these activities aren’t working. What do I take off my plate and equates do I put on my plate? How do I do this differently so that I can really make it work going forward?” We want BNI members to be in BNI for life! But it kills me every time I hear someone say, “Oh, I’m too busy and I can’t do BNI anymore.” And that makes me think if you are doing the activity to get the result you need?

Ivan:

That is true. Sometimes, though, when people say that they don’t have time for BNI, there are other issues. But let’s not assume that’s the case here. I think it is a time management issue, for sure. One of the things I like to do for time management, that I have said on my podcast before, is I color-code everything in my calendar and it’s related to the things I should be doing verses the things that I have to do. And I color-code in my calendar the things that I really want to to be doing or should be doing all in green. The things that drive forward my business to my role are in green. Everything else is in different colors. And by the way Meaghan red is bad, very bad. Usually red has lawyers involved. 

Meaghan:

Red has such a bad rep these days.

Ivan:

Yeah, well there’s a good reason. Anyways, it is about affective time management and using your time wisely. So I would recommend the directors and/or members of BNI to color-code their calendars for things they really should be doing- and  marketing is one of the things they should be doing.

Meaghan:

Absolutely. Also, if you do feel like BNI is taking too much time, reach out and talk to somebody about it because many of our Director consultants are available to help you figure out what you’re doing affectively and what you aren’t doing affectively. We don’t want BNI to take up a lot of time. It doesn’t have to, but you do have to work it. But you don’t have to work it 40 hours a week. So we want to help you find that balance and help you nail down what you can do, like I said, to squeeze every second you can out of the activities that you are doing.

Ivan:

Well, we need to wrap up. There are a number of things that people can do to really learn how to be more affective in BNI, and one of them is a project that you head up, and that is BNI University. I love BNI University! Meaghan, I want to put on record just saying that this is the formation of everything I had hoped we would be able to do in BNI- in terms of educating people in how to network more affectively. That’s one of the things I think people should spend a little time on, because it is sharpening their axe, you want to talk about BNI University very briefly? 

Meaghan:

Yes. So for anything that you do in BNI and you need help on how to hone that skill and do it more affectively, go into BNI University and just search for that topic. If it is referrals, it’s 1-2-1’s. There stuff on that that topic, and a bunch of other things you can find. There’s a bunch of great resources there!

Ivan:

There really is! And if you love BNI University, Meaghan made that happen. If you don’t like it, well, it’s somebody else’s… 

Meaghan really made BNI University happen. And I am really impressed with that program. I know how much work you have done on that, and I’m really impressed, Meaghan. I love it! I recommend everyone to go to that. It is one for the ways to make your time really effective because you’re sharpening your axe.

Anything you want to say before we wrap up here, Meaghan?

Meaghan:

I will also make sure that there is a resource available to you on BNI University to help you with some ideas on how you can look at what you’re doing, and make sure her activities that you are doing are getting the results that you want as well. So look for that on BNI University.

Thank you so much for having me, I really appreciate it. I feel so special being here on the 34th anniversary of BNI. I want to sincerely congratulate you for creating this amazing organization that all of us get to take a part in.

Ivan:

Thank you, Meaghan. I appreciate it. I got to tell you, I love this business. I love this organization. I love what I do. I’m happy to be here, and I’m happy to have you here on out 34th anniversary. 

Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:

Okay, well that is it for this week. Thank you both so much. We are never too busy to hear such great information.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

title.rendered: Episode 589: Following the BNI System

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Mark Appelbaum, regional growth coordinator of BNI Utah North, joins Dr. Misner on the podcast this week to share an analogy about the BNI System.

Why do people pay $1 million+ to invest in a McDonald’s franchise? It’s a proven system. If you follow the system, you’re guaranteed results.

When you join BNI, you’re investing in a system. If you follow the system, you’re guaranteed results.

BNI is part of your marketing system. Showing up early, planning your 30-second presentations in advance, scheduling one-to-ones–these are not things you’re doing for BNI. You’re doing them for yourself.

To read more of Dr. Misner’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 589 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio from Berkeley, CA. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla. I have a guest again for BNI. Hi name is Mark Appelbaum and Mark has been a BNI member for 14 years. He became a BNI Director four years ago. He has really grown his role in BNI Utah North. As a Director he is responsible for a dozen chapters and has successfully launched quite a few chapters in the region. His passion is teaching members how to give visitors a “wow” experience. I love that, Mark. And teaching members successful networking habits. Mark i married to his wife Tiffany and has two children, Brian and Shannah. Welcome to BNI Podcast.

Mark:
Thank you so much, Dr. Misner. It is an honor to be here and I am grateful for the opportunity.

Ivan:
It is great to have you and please call me Ivan. Let’s get started. You have a great analogy that I would love to begin with. Why don’t you share with everyone about BNI systems.

Mark:
Thank you. So I love this analogy when it comes to relating it to BNI. When I visit my chapters, I ask them four complete questions. I say name me a company that is known throughout the world, it has been around for 70+ years, it costs billions and billions of dollars to invest in the company. Some people get it by now but if they don’t I ask them one more. They make happy products for kids. Most people figure it out that it is McDonalds.

Now, when I ask them why do people invest so much into McDonalds? The resounding response is it’s a proven system. When I ask members if you’re going to invest into a McDonalds and you were reading the manual and the manual said, “Cook the French fries for 15 seconds.” Now, on a personal note, my. Seven year old daughter has never been to a McDonalds which I am proud of but if I was reading that manual and I bought this McDonalds. if it said to cook the fries for 15 seconds, I would follow that.

But what if somebody said, “I like crunchier fries. I am going to cook them. For 30 seconds.” Would you do that? Probably not.

Ivan:
But we do it in BNI, don’t we?

Mark:
We do all the time because they. Think, oh, let me try it this way. What is funny is BNI is a 33 year proven system with 8400 chapters plus around the world with over 240,000 members.

Ivan:
By the way, as of yesterday, Mark, 8,745 chapters.

Mark:
8,745 chapters around the world. It is a proven system and everything we have you do from showing up early for the open networking to 1-2-1s to weekly and feature presentations. When you follow the proven system, it’s proven to grow your business and make you more money, so why would you want to do it any other way?

Ivan:
I couldn’t agree with you more, so go ahead.

Mark:
So a number of months ago I was on BNI University and I know that it’s only available to some parts of the world. But there is a training that you did called Welcome to BNI. In part of that, I think it was section six or seven, you share a story. A new member comes to you who has been a member for two or three months. He complained to you that he has been a member for two months and he hasn’t received any business. I love how you respond to him. I am not going to share all of the details in your response to him. People can go on and listen to Welcome to BNI and listen to it.

Ivan:
Give a couple of them. I remember the story. Giver them one or two so people can get the context.

Mark:
Okay. You say, “Okay, great. I remember you saying that when members come up to you, you were really upset that members didn’t get business.

Ivan:
I was hurt.

Mark:
Yeah. Because this is your baby. In this case, you. Reply to them saying, “Hey, are you showing up early to the open networking?” The member says no. “Are you bringing marketing material to explain more about what you do?” No. “Are you doing 1-2-1s to learn more about your fellow members?” No. “Are you offering to be on leadership roles tp support the chapter?” No. “Are you surprised by your results?”

Ivan:
And “Are you preparing your weekly presentation?” That was one. I think that was a big one. Are you doing it in advance, your weekly presentation? No. And what did I say to him when he said no to all of that?

Mark:
“Are you surprised by your results?”

Ivan:
Because I am not. I think I even say this, “If you’d have come to me two months ago and said, ‘I am going to join but I am not going to do this, I am not going to do this.’ I would have told you not to join because it is a waste of time. “ It’s a waste of time if you are not going to follow the system.

Mark:
Exactly. This is my observation and with time and experience, especially time so short in BNI, is that I think the mindset that they are in, they think they are joining a meeting or a group of people, when in reality, they are investing in the system, and part of that system happens to be a meeting with a bunch of people. It’s just part of the. System. Sure, there are tone of organizations, Chambers of Commerce and opportunities to get your name out there. Also in my experience, none of those are [systems]. Those are more meetings that you attend. BNI is a system that you are investing in, and if you follow the system, it is proven.

You know, last year when I attended the BNI National Conference,I  met a phenomenal Director and Realtor from Scottsdale, AZ named Brenda. One of the things she shared with me and a few others is something she shared with incoming members. She asked, “Are you going to be a 90 minute member or are you in 100%?” I loved that. I love that because if you are a 90 minute member, you walk in at 7:30 and you leave at 9:00. If you are thinking that you are going to get results from that,. That’s not the system.

If you are going to invest in showing up early, going to 1-2-1s, practice your weekly presentations, really honing in your feature presentation. That’s part of the system Investing in BNI University and listening to podcasts sometimes. That’s part of the system.

Ivan:
Can I just add one thing to that, Mark> Some people say they don’t have time to do all that. This is part of your marketing system. It is not about BNI. It is about marketing your business. You are investing in your business by doing these things. This is your sales efforts, your marketing efforts. Some people look at me and say they don’t have time to do this for BNI. You are not doing it for BNI. You are doing it for yourself. BNI is the platform for you to do these things. Let me step off my soap box now. Go ahead.

Mark:
I am on my soapbox too so it is okay. I think it goes back to when they join a meeting, they join this group and they think, oh, if I show up at 7:30 I am going to get business. That is just not how it works. Like you are saying, if they are investing in all the different parts that we are training them on – like where. Else can you team up with 20, 30, 40+ other professionals who don’t do what. You do to help you grown your business? And are committed to a Givers Gain mentality?

I love the way that I kind of word this. You created this process where, magically, by not focusing on me but by focusing on the person in front of me, it helps me.

Ivan:
That’s exactly right.

Mark:
That is how it works. Is there anything else that you wanted to add to that?

Ivan:
One of the reasons that I think BNI works as well as it does – people join this organization after 33 years as you said, and it is fully formed and they look at it and think maybe they can tweak it. They don’t understand it exists as it exists because I screwed up so much so many times over the last three decades that I kept improving it, honing it, and making it better along with many, many other people. Not just me. We made this process better. It has been a 33 year, almost 34 year, journey. So it is from the mistakes we have made that has made the system work. Don’t reinvent the wheel.

I had someone say to me once because I was asking him to do something and he said, “But I just want to follow your program. You tell me what the program is and I am going to follow it. I am a lawyer. I don’t have time to do other stuff. I just want to follow your system.” I said great, he followed it, and he did fantastic.

Mark:
I love that. You know, from a personal – when I first joined 14 years ago, I was first introduced to it when I was living in Arizona 16 years ago. At the time, I was kind of new in business. I wasn’t doing well financially. So I would be that orbit sprinkler where I was passing out cards all around the room. I would take the mindset of being interesting instead of interested and what can you do for me? Once I learned all those flaws, all the things not to do and BNI taught me that over the years, what not to do, I could now teach what to do and teach those habits. By doing these habits and showing up weekly and all of the things that we are teaching, this will work for you. Just trust takes time, all of it takes time. It is not something that you just plug in and all of a sudden you are cloning the business. But if you follow the system, it is proven to work.

Ivan:
It does take time and it does work. I have for years said if you want to be successful in business, do six things 1000 times not 1000 things. Six times. BNI is a great example of that. It is doing things over and over again consistently over time based on building relationships.

Mark, any closing thoughts before we wrap up?

Mark:
No, again, I just want to thank you for the opportunity and I look forward to hearing how this goes.

Ivan:
Well, thanks, Mark . I appreciate you being on and I love the them for this podcast. It really is about following the system. If you are in business and you are pretty good at what you do, you want people to take your advice. I have been doing this for a long time. Mark has been doing this for a long time. We are pretty good at what we do. Take our advice. Follow the system. Don’t reinvent the wheel. It really does work. It works in 8700 chapters of more than 73 countries around the world. Thanks so much for being on BNI Podcast today, Mark. You were a great guest.

Mark:
Thank you.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
I think that is it for this week, Dr. Misner and Mark. Thank you so much. For more of Ivan’s content go to IvanMisner.com. Thanks so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again ext week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Why do people pay $1 million+ to invest in a McDonald’s franchise? It’s a proven system. If you follow the system, you’re guaranteed results. When you join BNI, you’re investing in a system. If you follow the system, you’re guaranteed results.

title.rendered: Episode 588: Want Referrals? Don’t Bore Me To Tears

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Charlie Lawson, “unnatural networker” and BNI national director for the UK and Ireland, joins Dr. Misner this week to talk about storytelling, and tells a great story in the process.

What sells your services is not listing what you offer. To give your networking contacts the tools to find you referrals, you need two things:

  1. How does the client feel before you do the work? What’s the situation?
  2. How does the client feel after you do the work?

It’s not important to describe the process. Create an emotional connection between the person you’re talking about and the client. To get an engaging story, tell it from the client’s point of view. Facts tell, stories sell.

To read more of Dr. Misner’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 588 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio from Berkeley, CA. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla. I have a good friend that I’m interviewing with today, Charlie Lawson. Charlie is the Co-National Director of BNI in the UK and Ireland. He is really proud to be a part of developing people, businesses to change lives and enrich communities. But what I think is really interesting is that despite running BNI in the United Kingdom and Ireland, Charlie is, by his own definition, an unnatural networker. He says that most experts talk about networking as though it is easy to talk to strangers, but Charlie is uncomfortable talking to strangers. He helps other unnatural networkers gain confidence by putting networking across the point of view of someone who would rather avoid it completely.

Charlie, it is really a pleasure to have you on BNI Podcast.

Charlie:
Thanks for having me on. I am definitely an unnatural networker, and if I can avoid talking to strangers and chat with my friends and mates instead, then that is much easier. But yeah, it is a nice angle. It is a elephant in the room when I do whisper it.

Ivan:
Yeah, and you know with BNI it’s all about relationships so it is a little bit easier. Your topic for today is “want referrals- don’t bore me to tears.” And I love that title, it is a great title. It’s really about storytelling. So, tell us about storytelling. Why is it so important? It is one of the pieces that we don’t talk enough about, so I’m really happy to have you on. Charlie, go ahead.

Charlie:
I urge everyone to look back at their last chapter meeting and think back on their weekly presentation, were they all captivating and interesting? Did they grab your attention? Some of them I’m sure, but I would imagine not all of them were. If you think back to any networking convention, BNI meeting or somewhere else, how were the conversations there? Were they all engaging? Did they all grab you? Or did you actually find yourself asking, “really do I have to carry on this conversation? Or will I have to be bored to tears?” I think that for me, being an unnatural networker, it’s hard enough for me to talk to strangers in the first place. If I think that I’m boring them to tears, I am very conscious that I want them to be engaged in the conversation. I want to be liked. If an introvert wants to make it, you have got to make it as easy a possible. So, I want to know that people are really listening, and I found that stories are a much better way to do that.

Since we are talking about storytelling, I will share a quick story about Dena. Dena was a BNI member in Yorkshire, in the north of England, and she got a call on a February afternoon at about 5 o’clock- if you have ever been to Yorkshire in February you know by 5 o’clock that it is cold, dark and it was also raining that day. So when Dena answered the phone, she could tell that there was a lady on the other end of the line who was obviously quite upset from the commotion. Dena could hear kids running around and making noise, there was a lot going on. And she said, “What’s going on? How can I help?” Dena ran a listing agency, so she was selling properties to get people put in accommodations- not buying properties but more of a short term, not just an overnight accommodation. What the lady was trying to do- she said, “I’ve got a real problem.” Turns out that she had been chucked out of the house by her partner. Her partner had literally said, “You gotta go to find somewhere else to live.” And left her with the kids. And she was calling Dena to see if there was anything she could do. She said, “I know this doesn’t usually happen at short notice. I know there’s usually contracts and other things of that sort all depending of the availability of the place, but I need somewhere to stay tonight.” Dena managed to source her a property. Not only that, she was able to, in the short term circumvent the process and say, “Hey, look, let’s just get you in the property and we will work out the details tomorrow-“ when it was possible to do it. Not only that, right as she was about to hang up the phone and say, “Look, here’s the details, just come to the property.” She said, “Hang on, where are you?” Turns out that lady had literally been chucked out by her ex partner and was standing out on the roadside with her kids in tow, with a couple of suitcases and boxes, and she had nowhere to go. She had no car. She had no way to get there. So Dena got in her car and said, “I am going to come and pick you up.” She picked them up and took them to the place where they managed to sort everything out. My question for anyone is, would you refer business to Dena?

And I can imagine the answer for most people would be, “Yeah, what an amazing service she offered. She was able to do something short notice- that she may not always be able to do, but she was kind, compassionate, caring and she wanted to help.” What Dena realized was that they were in a real grave situation there and she could make a difference for this lady.

Ivan:
Let me swoop in for just a second here, Charlie. That’s a great story and I want to break it down slightly. What Dena did was establish the setting- and I gotta be honest, what you did with Dena’s story was establish the setting, laid out the crisis and then you described the solution that she gave and how she was able to help and serve that potential customer. That is what makes a great story. Nobody in that room would be bored to tears! I can’t imagine anyone can be bored listening to this podcast. That is. A great example of a great story.

Please go on, Charlie.

Charlie:
When you hear weekly presentations, you hear networking conversations, and it’s often not like that though. It is often- take the listing agent again. A listing agent might be saying at the weekly presentations or networking conversations, “Well I can offer short term leases and last minute solutions. I’ve got a great range of services. I can offer inventory services. I can do this and that.” That doesn’t sell the services of the listing agent. What sells the services of the listing agent is the fact that we know we’d be comfortable referring her. And that is where storytelling happens. It’s not just for making it more engaging and interesting to hear, even though you enjoy what you hear more, it actually makes us do something with it. It becomes memorable. And when it comes memorable, it becomes the sort of thing we bring back up in a future conversation. And next time someone asks me about listing agents, who am I going to think about? I’m going to think about Dena because I have heard that powerful story.

And then I’ll start to break it down and start to think of what’s the difference there. If I am listing off my products and services, effectively that’s just not the information we need to make referrals. We are assuming here that we’ve got a good relationship. Once we’ve got a good relationship- you know that is a prerequisite for networking obviously, you have got to have a relationship there. But once we’ve got a relationship, we want to give our networking contacts the tools to find those referrals. And just listing our products and services doesn’t work. What we really need to figure out is how did the client feel before we did the work? What’s the situation they are in, and how are they feeling about life? You know, generally in that situation, there is going to be some level of emotion, so be careful here. Often when we think of emotion we think of sadness because that’s an obvious emotion, but there are all sorts of emotions; worry, anxious, anger, happiness- all sorts of different emotions. Let’s take Dena’s client for an example. How was she feeling? Well, she was feeling scared, very worried, she’s got kids running around. Imagine how she is feeling. That’s a horrible situation to be in. And what we have got to try to do is when we are telling our stories, is putting it across from the client’s point of view so their emotion comes through. That’s when you get the visceral connection to a story. Even though we may not have been in that situation ourselves and of course that is quite an extreme story, but it’s the visceral connection that makes us feel and be like, “That poor lady! What happened? How does the story end?” Then we don’t need to hear what Dena actually did. In terms of the due process, she actually did get the lady into a flat- but we do not really care about that. What we really care after that is, how did the person feel afterwards?

I’ll refer to my friend Andy Bounds. He’s spoken at BNI events in the UK, Ireland and other countries as well. He is a sales guy and messaging guy, and talks about the ‘after’. You know, you don’t buy a product for the product. You buy a product for what the product gives you. I’ll try to give an example: You don’t buy a pair of glasses for the glasses, you buy them for sight. The sight is the ‘after’ that we like. So, the ‘after’ in this case is how did the lady feel afterwards? What was the situation’s result? You can then feel comfortable to refer them.

When you are focusing on stories in your weekly presentation and your networking conversation, think about it from the client’s point of view, not your point of view. When you are thinking from your point of view, you are just sort of trying to say what you did. Imagine if I told Dena’s story like, “I received a call and I realized I had to get somewhere last minute, and I had to look up a list of accommodations to find this out…“ It’s just not that interesting. When it’s from a clients point of view, we get an engaging story. We don’t bore people to tears. Also, people start to do something with it.

Ivan:
Well, we are almost out of time, and I want to give you a moment to wrap up. Before I do, I just want to repeat for everyone who is listening, two key factors that Charlie just shared for everyone. Number one, in your networking meeting, how do they feel before you turned up? In your presentation you want to describe how people felt before you turned up. And then, you want to talk about how they feel after you did the work. Those two things are key and I am really happy that you are on this podcast to share with us, Charlie.

Any closing thoughts to leave us with before we wrap up?

Charlie:
Yeah, the moral of the story is that I think storytelling is more interesting, memorable, and crucially more referable. And that is why we go to networking events and BNI because we want to build our business through referrals. We’ve got to give our fellow members the tools to find those referrals, and I think storytelling is a great way to do it. Plus, it won’t bore people to tears when it comes to your networking.

Ivan:
I love it, Charlie. Everyone who is listening to this, you have to share this with members of your chapter. Education Coordinators, this is a great educational slot. Share this content with your BNI chapters. Remember, facts tell, stories sell. And stories are what give that visceral feel that you were talking about, Charlie.

Thanks so much for being on the BNI podcast. It was a pleasure having you on.

Charlie:
It was my pleasure.

Ivan:
Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, well thank you both very much. I think that is it for this week. Thank you so much for the great information. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: To give your networking contacts the tools to find you referrals, tell the story from the client’s point of view. Facts tell; stories sell.

title.rendered: Episode 587: Imagine You Live in One Room

content.rendered:
Synopsis

In this week’s episode, Dr. Misner shares the concept behind his new book, Who’s in Your Room: The Secret to Creating Your Best Life. (Note that an earlier, BNI-specific version of this book appeared in Episode 358, back in 2014.)

Imagine you live your entire life in one room. Inside are all the people who enter and all the relationships and obligations that come with them. You can update and expand your room to accommodate new possibilities in your life.

But this room only has one door–and the door only lets people in. Whoever enters will be with you in your room for the rest of your life. The quality of your life depends on the people in your room.

You may think this is a metaphor, but to quote Dr. Daniel Amen, any significant input that is received into your brain that cannot simply be erased or deleted as though it never happened. If people are still in your head, they’re in your room.

The purpose of this book is to help you take your room, and your relationships, from dissonance to resonance. This is an action-oriented book: every chapter gives you specific things you need to do, both for yourself and for your BNI chapter.

[To sign up for the free webinars, complete the information on this link: https://tinyurl.com/FreeRoomWebinars]

If you’d like to hear more podcasts drawn from this book, let us know in the comments.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 587 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you today? I hear that you have a new book that you are going to talk about.

Ivan:
I do. I have to tell you, Priscilla. I am more excited about this book than any book I have ever done. I honestly feel this will make a bigger difference in people’s lives than any of the books that I have done because it is a self help book that is open to the public. It is I think of interest to anyone at any age, and at the same time, I think it is also perfect for BNI members.

I talked about an original version of this four years ago. You can find it on BNI Podcast. The BNI edition of Who’s in Your Room? This is the shortest book I have ever written, the public version of Who’s in Your Room, and it took longer to write than any book I have ever written because we really condensed it down. We took the basic premise of the BNI edition, but we made a book that is incredibly actionable.

What I would like to do with this podcast is share the concept, and if you like this and are listening to this podcast and love this concept and would like me to all more about it throughout the year, I am happy to do additional podcasts that will pull content from the book.

We are going to talk about how you can go to webinars. I like to do podcasts that are evergreen, but this won’t be evergreen, but it will last a long time. There will be a link that we will embed in this podcast where you can actually go to webinars that I will be doing going deeper into the content of the book with my co-authors.
